description: Berber tribes controlling trans-Saharan trade routes began consolidating Islamic practices and legal structures that would later form the basis of the Almoravid movement. Muslim merchants from North Africa intensified gold and slave trade with West African kingdoms.
outcome: These networks enriched North African dynasties and spread Islam into sub-Saharan West Africa.

title: Deaths in 877 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 877 CE: Al-Muzani — jurist (Imam al-Muzani — Greatest Student of al-Shafi'i) in Egypt / Cairo.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Mansur establishes independent emirate in Tlemcen
description: Al-Mansur ibn Muhammad established a short-lived independent emirate in Tlemcen, breaking from Aghlabid control. The emirate represented the growing fragmentation of North African authority.
outcome: Demonstrated the weakening of centralized authority in the Maghreb during this period.

title: Syracuse falls after year-long siege
description: After a nine-month siege, Aghlabid forces under Jafar ibn Muhammad captured Syracuse, the Byzantine capital of Sicily. The fall marked a decisive moment in the Muslim conquest of the island.
outcome: Established Muslim control over most of Sicily and created a lasting Arabic cultural influence on the island.

title: Ibn Tulun Mosque completed in Cairo
description: Ahmad ibn Tulun completes his grand mosque in al-Qata'i (Cairo), with its distinctive spiral minaret inspired by the Malwiya of Samarra — one of the oldest intact mosques in Egypt.
outcome: One of Egypt's greatest architectural monuments; oldest intact mosque in Cairo

title: Death of Ibn Qutayba in Baghdad
description: The polymath Ibn Qutayba died in Baghdad after producing influential works defending traditional Sunni Islam against Mu'tazilite rationalism. His works on hadith, theology, and Arabic literature shaped medieval Islamic scholarship.
outcome: His synthesis of religious and secular learning influenced Islamic education for centuries.

title: Death of Abu Yazid al-Bistami
description: Bayazid Bastami (Abu Yazid al-Bistami), the great Persian Sufi master known for his doctrine of fana (annihilation in God) and ecstatic utterances, dies in Bastam. His teachings on mystical intoxication profoundly influenced later Sufi thought.
outcome: His concept of mystical union became central to the development of ecstatic Sufism.

title: Battle of Tabaristan - Zaydis establish highland state
description: Zaydi Shia forces under al-Hasan ibn Zayd defeated Abbasid troops in the mountains of Tabaristan (Mazandaran), establishing an independent Zaydi state. This marked one of the earliest successful Shia breakaway states from Abbasid rule.
sources: Madelung, Religious Trends in Early Islamic Iran
outcome: The Zaydi state in northern Iran persisted intermittently for decades, influencing later Shia movements.

title: Deaths in 880 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 880 CE: Fatima al-Fihri — founder (Umm al-Banin — Founder of Al-Qarawiyyin) in Fez, Morocco.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Farabi born — Second Teacher after Aristotle
description: Abu Nasr al-Farabi is born in Turkestan; he will be called 'the Second Teacher' (after Aristotle) for his synthesis of Greek philosophy with Islamic thought.

figures.name: Al-Farabi
figures.role: born; future 'Second Teacher' of Islamic philosophy

outcome: His political philosophy and logic shaped all subsequent Islamic and European thought

title: Al-Niffari's mystical visions in Iraq
description: The Sufi mystic Muhammad ibn Abd al-Jabbar al-Niffari began recording his visionary experiences (mawaqif) in Iraq, developing a unique contemplative vocabulary. His enigmatic writings described direct encounters with divine reality beyond rational thought.
sources: A.J. Arberry, The Mawaqif and Mukhatabat
outcome: His mystical writings influenced later Sufi metaphysics despite remaining relatively obscure.

title: Thabit ibn Qurra translates Ptolemy's Almagest
description: The Sabian mathematician and astronomer Thabit ibn Qurra completed an improved Arabic translation of Ptolemy's Almagest in Baghdad. He also corrected earlier translations and made original contributions to astronomy and mathematics.
outcome: His translations and original work preserved Greek astronomy and influenced medieval European science.

title: Yellow Turban Huang Chao rebellion affects Muslim traders in China
description: The massive Huang Chao rebellion in China culminates in the sack of Canton (Guangzhou), where rebel forces massacre thousands including a large community of Arab and Persian Muslim merchants. This devastates the maritime Silk Road trade networks connecting China to the Islamic world.
sources: Abu Zayd al-Sirafi, Akhbar al-Sin wa'l-Hind
outcome: Severely disrupted Muslim trade with China and diminished the Muslim merchant community in Canton for decades.

title: Zanj revolt finally suppressed near Basra
description: After fourteen years of rebellion, Abbasid forces under the future caliph al-Muwaffaq finally defeat and kill the leader of the Zanj, Ali ibn Muhammad. The suppression ends the most serious internal threat to Abbasid Iraq, though the region is devastated.
outcome: Restored Abbasid control over southern Iraq but left the region economically and demographically devastated.

title: Samanid dynasty rises as champions of Persian culture
description: The Samanid dynasty consolidates power over Transoxiana and Khurasan from their capital Bukhara, becoming the great patrons of Persian literature and the New Persian language.
outcome: Launched the Persian literary renaissance; their court produced Firdawsi's Shahnameh tradition

title: Deaths in 886 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 886 CE: Abu Ma'shar al-Balkhi — scientist (Albumasar — Greatest Islamic Astrologer) in Balkh / Baghdad, Iraq.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 887 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 887 CE: Ibn Majah — scholar (Imam al-Hafiz — Sixth of the Six Masters) in Qazvin, Iran; Abbas ibn Firnas — scientist (Andalusian polymath and aviation pioneer) in Cordoba.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Death of Ibn Qutayba in Baghdad
description: Ibn Qutayba, polymath scholar who bridged traditional Arabic learning and Islamic sciences, dies in Baghdad. His works on literature, history, hadith, and philology, including Adab al-Katib and Uyun al-Akhbar, became foundational texts for Arabic literary culture.
outcome: His encyclopedic works preserved classical Arabic literary traditions while integrating Islamic knowledge.

title: Deaths in 889 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 889 CE: Abu Dawud — scholar (Imam al-Sunan — Master of the Prophetic Practices) in Sijistan / Basra / Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Tabari begins writing his universal history
description: Muhammad ibn Jarir al-Tabari begins composing his monumental Tarikh al-Rusul wa al-Muluk (History of Prophets and Kings), the most comprehensive work of Islamic historiography ever written.

figures.name: Al-Tabari
figures.role: author; wrote the definitive Islamic universal history

outcome: Created the foundational text of Islamic historiography used by all subsequent historians

title: Deaths in 892 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 892 CE: Al-Baladhuri — scholar (Author of Futuh al-Buldan — Islamic Conquest Historian) in Baghdad, Iraq; Muhammad al-Tirmidhi — scholar (Imam al-'Ilal — Master of Hadith Analysis) in Tirmidh (Termez), Uzbekistan.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Abbasid capital returns to Baghdad
description: After nearly 60 years in Samarra, the Abbasid court permanently returns to Baghdad under Caliph al-Mu'tadid, though by now the Caliphs control little beyond central Iraq.
outcome: Baghdad regained its status as capital, but the Abbasid empire had fragmented irreversibly

title: Al-Hallaj begins preaching mystical doctrines
description: The controversial mystic al-Hallaj began publicly preaching his doctrine of divine union in Baghdad and Persia. His ecstatic utterances, including "Ana al-Haqq" (I am the Truth), would eventually lead to his execution.
outcome: His martyrdom became a defining event in Sufi history, raising questions about mystical expression and orthodoxy.

title: Al-Mu'tadid restores Abbasid capital to Baghdad
description: Caliph al-Mu'tadid abandons Samarra and returns the Abbasid capital to Baghdad after 58 years, symbolically restoring the caliphate's prestige. He begins rebuilding the city and reasserting direct caliphal authority after decades of Turkish military domination.
outcome: Marked a brief revival of centralized Abbasid power before renewed fragmentation in the 10th century.

title: Al-Mu'tadid consolidates power and reforms military
description: Caliph al-Mu'tadid implemented military and administrative reforms, reducing the power of Turkish military commanders and restoring direct caliphal authority. He personally led campaigns and reorganized provincial governance.
outcome: His reforms temporarily strengthened the Abbasid state but could not reverse long-term decentralization.

title: Deaths in 895 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 895 CE: Dinawari — scientist (Persian polymath and father of Arabic botany) in Dinavar.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 896 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 896 CE: Sahl al-Tustari — Successor (Tabi'i) (Qutb al-Tafsir — Pole of Quranic Exegesis) in Shushtar / Basra; Ibn al-Rumi — poet (Major Abbasid poet known for descriptive and philosophical verse) in Baghdad.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Isma'il Samani defeats Saffarids at Battle of Balkh
description: Isma'il Samani, founder of the Samanid dynasty, decisively defeats the Saffarid ruler Amr ibn al-Layth at the Battle of Balkh. This victory establishes Samanid dominance over Transoxiana and Khurasan, creating the greatest Persian cultural renaissance of the early Islamic period.
outcome: Established the Samanid state as the preeminent power in Central Asia and patron of Persian culture.

title: Deaths in 897 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 897 CE: Al-Yaqubi — scholar (First Universal Islamic Historian) in Baghdad / Egypt / Khorasan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Qarmatian state established in Bahrayn
description: Abu Sa'id al-Jannabi established the Qarmatian state in eastern Arabia, implementing an egalitarian Ismaili Shia system. The Qarmatians created a communal society that rejected many mainstream Islamic practices.
outcome: The Qarmatian state would challenge Abbasid authority for decades and famously steal the Black Stone from Mecca in 930.

title: Ismaili Shia missionary network spreads across Muslim world
description: The Ismaili da'wa (missionary network) operates secretly from Yemen to North Africa, preparing the ground for the Fatimid revolution that will create a rival caliphate.
outcome: Laid the groundwork for the Fatimid caliphate — Islam's most successful revolutionary movement

title: Deaths in 901 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 901 CE: Thabit ibn Qurra — scientist (Renovator of Hellenistic Knowledge) in Harran / Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Fall of Taormina to the Aghlabids
description: The Aghlabid emir Ibrahim II conquered Taormina, the last major Byzantine stronghold in Sicily. This completed the Muslim conquest of Sicily after nearly a century of campaigns.
outcome: Sicily became a major center of Islamic culture and learning in the Mediterranean.

title: Fall of Taormina completes Muslim conquest of Sicily
description: Ibrahim II al-Aghlabid completed the conquest of Sicily with the capture of Taormina, the last Byzantine stronghold on the island. This marked the end of Byzantine rule in Sicily after over two centuries of gradual Muslim conquest.
outcome: Sicily became fully integrated into the Islamic world, fostering Arabic culture and administrative systems.

title: Qarmatians sack Basra
description: Qarmatian forces under Abu Sa'id al-Jannabi launched a devastating attack on Basra, one of the most important commercial cities of the Abbasid Caliphate. The raid resulted in massive destruction and loss of life, demonstrating the growing power of the Qarmatian movement.
outcome: The attack showcased Qarmatian military capabilities and threatened Abbasid control over southern Iraq.

title: Samanids patronise New Persian literature
description: Under Samanid patronage, the New Persian literary tradition flourishes; Rudaki, the 'father of Persian poetry,' composes at the Bukhara court, and Bal'ami translates Tabari's history into Persian.
outcome: Birth of the Persian literary tradition that produced Firdawsi, Rumi, Hafez

title: Deaths in 907 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 907 CE: Abu al-Husayn al-Nuri — Sufi mystic (al-Nuri — The Luminous; Martyr of Love) in Baghdad, Iraq.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Fatimid Caliphate established in North Africa
description: The Ismaili Shia imam Ubayd Allah al-Mahdi overthrows the Aghlabids and establishes the Fatimid Caliphate in Ifriqiya, creating a rival to the Abbasid caliphate.
outcome: Created the only Shia caliphate in history; rival to both Abbasids and Umayyads of Córdoba

title: Ubayd Allah al-Mahdi proclaims himself Caliph
description: Ubayd Allah al-Mahdi, the Ismaili leader, proclaimed himself Caliph in Raqqada near Kairouan after overthrowing the Aghlabids. This established the Fatimid Caliphate as a rival to the Abbasids in Baghdad.
outcome: The Fatimid Caliphate would challenge Abbasid and later Umayyad legitimacy for two centuries.

title: Deaths in 910 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 910 CE: Hakim al-Tirmidhi — scholar (al-Hakim — Imam of the Science of Sainthood) in Tirmidh, Central Asia; Junayd of Baghdad — Sufi mystic (Sayyid al-Ta'ifa — Master of the Sufi Group) in Baghdad, Iraq.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Deaths in 912 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 912 CE: Ibn Khordadbeh — scholar (First Islamic Geographer — Book of Routes and Realms) in Khurasan / Baghdad; Qusta ibn Luqa — scientist (Christian Polymath of the Islamic Golden Age) in Baalbek / Baghdad; Ahmad ibn Yusuf — scientist (Arab mathematician who wrote on ratios and proportions) in Baghdad.
outcome: 3 notable figures died this year.

title: Abd al-Rahman III becomes Emir of Córdoba
description: The greatest Umayyad ruler of al-Andalus begins his 49-year reign, transforming Córdoba into the most advanced city in Europe.
outcome: Began the golden age of al-Andalus; Córdoba rivalled Baghdad and Constantinople

title: Al-Tabari completes Tarikh al-Rusul wa'l-Muluk
description: The Persian historian and exegete al-Tabari completed his monumental universal history covering from creation to 915 CE. His history became one of the most important sources for early Islamic history.
outcome: The work established the standard chronological framework for Islamic historical writing for centuries.

title: Al-Muqtadir appoints Ibn al-Furat as vizier
description: Abbasid Caliph al-Muqtadir appointed Ibn al-Furat as vizier for the first time, beginning a turbulent period of repeated appointments and dismissals. Ibn al-Furat's financial policies and political maneuvering characterized the weakening of central Abbasid authority.
outcome: This era marked increasing vizierial power and Abbasid political instability in the early 10th century.

title: Death of Abdullah ibn Muhammad of Córdoba
description: Emir Abdullah ibn Muhammad of Córdoba died after a troubled reign marked by internal rebellions and the rise of autonomous Muslim lords. His grandson Abd al-Rahman III succeeded him and would transform the weakened emirate into a powerful caliphate.
outcome: His death marked the end of the emirate's weakest period and the beginning of its golden age under Abd al-Rahman III.

title: Deaths in 915 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 915 CE: Al-Nasa'i — scholar (Imam al-Naqd — Most Precise of the Six Masters) in Nasa (Khorasan) / Egypt / Damascus; Al-Jubba'i — philosopher (Philosopher of Basra) in Basra.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Battle of Garigliano
description: Christian forces led by Pope John X and Byzantine forces defeated Muslim forces entrenched at the Garigliano River in southern Italy. This victory ended Muslim raids from this base into central Italy and the papal territories.
outcome: The defeat marked the end of sustained Muslim military presence in central Italy.

title: Deaths in 918 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 918 CE: Ibn Surayj — scholar (al-Shafi'i al-Saghir — The Second Shafi'i) in Baghdad, Iraq.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Razi writes al-Hawi fi'l-Tibb
description: The Persian physician Abu Bakr al-Razi compiled his comprehensive medical encyclopedia al-Hawi. The work synthesized Greek, Indian, and Islamic medical knowledge and was translated into Latin as Liber Continens.
sources: Siraisi, Medieval and Early Renaissance Medicine
outcome: The work remained a standard medical textbook in both Islamic and European universities for centuries.

title: Qarmatians sack Basra
description: The Qarmatian movement, led by Abu Tahir al-Jannabi, launched a devastating raid on Basra, one of the great cities of Iraq. The attack was part of their campaign against Abbasid authority and conventional Islamic practices.
outcome: The raid demonstrated the Qarmatians' military threat to the Abbasid heartland.

title: Qarmatians steal the Black Stone from the Kaaba
description: Qarmatian forces under Abu Tahir al-Jannabi raided Mecca during the Hajj season, massacred pilgrims, and removed the Black Stone from the Kaaba. The sacred stone was taken to their capital al-Ahsa and held for over twenty years, causing profound shock throughout the Muslim world.
outcome: The theft was one of the most traumatic events in medieval Islamic history and severely damaged the legitimacy of the Abbasid Caliphate.

title: Deaths in 922 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 922 CE: Al-Hallaj — Sufi mystic (Shahid al-'Ishq — Martyr of Love) in Fars / Baghdad; Ibn 'Ata al-Adami — Sufi mystic (Shahid al-Mahabba — Martyr of Love) in Baghdad, Iraq.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Execution of al-Hallaj in Baghdad
description: The mystic Husayn ibn Mansur al-Hallaj is executed in Baghdad for declaring 'Ana al-Haqq' (I am the Truth/God), becoming the most famous martyr of Sufism.

figures.name: Al-Hallaj
figures.role: Sufi mystic; executed for his ecstatic declaration 'I am the Truth'

outcome: Became Sufism's most famous martyr; his death defined the tension between mysticism and orthodoxy

title: Deaths in 923 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 923 CE: Al-Tabari — historian (Imam al-Mufassirin — The Imam of Quranic Commentary) in Tabaristan / Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Battle of al-Fakhkh and Death of al-Hallaj's followers
description: Following al-Hallaj's execution, his disciples faced persecution in Baghdad. The Hallajiyya movement was suppressed by Abbasid authorities who viewed mystical claims of divine union as heretical.
outcome: The persecution shaped the development of more cautious expressions of Sufi doctrine.

title: Deaths in 925 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 925 CE: Al-Razi (Rhazes) — scientist (Galen of the Arabs — Father of Clinical Medicine) in Ray (near Tehran), Iran.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Abd al-Rahman III conquers Bobastro
description: Abd al-Rahman III captured the fortress of Bobastro, ending the half-century rebellion of the Muwalladun leader Ibn Hafsun's descendants. This victory consolidated Umayyad control over al-Andalus.
outcome: The conquest enabled Abd al-Rahman to expand his authority and later declare the Caliphate of Córdoba.

title: Tomislav becomes first King of Croatia after Byzantine recognition
description: Tomislav of Croatia received recognition from Byzantine Emperor Constantine VII, establishing the Kingdom of Croatia as a buffer state between Byzantine and Muslim territories. This formalized Christian-Muslim boundaries in the Adriatic.
outcome: Croatia became a key Christian power facing Muslim naval forces in the Adriatic Sea.

title: Al-Razi dies in Baghdad
description: Abu Bakr Muhammad ibn Zakariya al-Razi, one of the greatest physicians and scientists in Islamic history, died in Baghdad. Known in the West as Rhazes, he made groundbreaking contributions to medicine, chemistry, and philosophy, including the first clinical description of smallpox and measles.
sources: Ibn Abi Usaybi'a, Uyun al-Anba fi Tabaqat al-Atibba
outcome: His medical works remained authoritative texts in both Islamic and European medicine for centuries.

title: Deaths in 929 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 929 CE: Al-Battani — scientist (Albategnius — Greatest Arab Astronomer) in Raqqa / Antioch, Syria.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Abd al-Rahman III declares himself Caliph
description: The Umayyad emir of Córdoba takes the title of Caliph, creating a third caliphate alongside the Abbasids and Fatimids — the Islamic world now has three rival Caliphs.
outcome: Three simultaneous caliphates; Córdoba declared equal to Baghdad and the Fatimid capital

title: Deaths in 930 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 930 CE: Abu Kamil — scientist (Egyptian mathematician who advanced al-Khwarizmi's algebra) in Cairo; Ibn Rustah — scholar (Persian geographer who compiled al-Alaq al-Nafisa on peoples and lands) in Isfahan.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Deaths in 931 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 931 CE: Ibn Masarra — philosopher (First Philosopher of Andalusia) in Córdoba, Andalusia.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Buyid dynasty captures Baghdad
description: The Shia Iranian Buyid dynasty captures Baghdad and reduces the Abbasid Caliph to a puppet; for the first time, a Shia dynasty controls the Sunni caliphate's capital.
outcome: Abbasid Caliphs became powerless figureheads; real power held by Buyid military commanders

title: Mardavij founds the Ziyarid dynasty
description: The Gilaki military leader Mardavij established the Ziyarid dynasty in northern Iran and briefly controlled much of the Iranian plateau. He attempted to revive ancient Persian imperial traditions and Zoroastrianism.
outcome: Though short-lived, the dynasty influenced the rise of the Buyids and Persian cultural renaissance.

title: Al-Qahir deposed as Abbasid Caliph
description: The Abbasid Caliph al-Qahir was deposed and blinded by military commanders after attempting to assert independent authority. His successor al-Radi was installed, marking further decline of caliphal power.
outcome: The Abbasid Caliphate became increasingly ceremonial as military strongmen held real power.

title: Al-Muqtadir killed in civil war
description: The Abbasid Caliph al-Muqtadir was killed in battle against rebellious troops led by his brother al-Qahir near Baghdad. His long but weak reign of 25 years was marked by constant political instability and the rise of powerful military commanders.
outcome: His death exemplified the decline of Abbasid authority and the fragmentation of the caliphate.

title: Death of Abu Bakr al-Baqillani's teacher Ibn Mujahid
description: Ahmad ibn Musa ibn al-Abbas ibn Mujahid, the great Qur'anic scholar, dies in Baghdad. He was the first to formally canonize the seven variant readings (qira'at) of the Qur'an, establishing the foundation for Qur'anic recitation traditions that continue today.
outcome: His systematization of the seven canonical readings became the standard for Qur'anic recitation across the Muslim world.

title: Deaths in 933 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 933 CE: Abu Hatim Ahmad ibn Hamdan al-Razi — philosopher (Philosopher of Tehran) in Tehran; Ibn Duraid — scholar (Arab lexicographer and author of Kitab al-Jamhara) in Basra.

figures.name: Abu Hatim Ahmad ibn Hamdan al-Razi

outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Deaths in 934 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 934 CE: Ubayd Allah al-Mahdi — ruler (Founder of the Fatimid Caliphate) in Syria / Mahdia, Tunisia; Abu Zayd al-Balkhi — scientist (Persian polymath and pioneer of mental health geography) in Balkh.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Deaths in 935 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 935 CE: Al-Tahawi — scholar (Imam Ahl al-Sunna — The Creed of the Majority) in Taha / Cairo, Egypt; Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari — philosopher (Founder of the Ash'ari school of kalam in Sunni Islam) in Basra.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Al-Ash'ari founds orthodox Sunni theology
description: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, a former Mu'tazili, develops a middle path between rationalism and literalism that becomes the dominant school of Sunni theological thought.

figures.name: Al-Ash'ari
figures.role: founder; created the dominant school of Sunni theology

outcome: Ash'ari theology became the mainstream of Sunni Islam, followed by the majority to this day

title: Construction of Madinat al-Zahra begins
description: Abd al-Rahman III began construction of the palatine city Madinat al-Zahra near Córdoba as a symbol of Umayyad power. The sprawling complex included palaces, mosques, gardens, and administrative buildings.
outcome: The city became a marvel of Islamic architecture and symbol of Andalusian sophistication before its destruction in 1010.

title: Al-Ash'ari publicly renounces Mu'tazilism
description: Abu al-Hasan al-Ash'ari publicly abandoned Mu'tazilite rationalism in the mosque of Basra and announced his new theological method. This marked the beginning of classical Ash'arite theology, which would become mainstream Sunni orthodoxy.
outcome: Ash'arism provided a middle path between rationalism and literalism in Islamic theology.

title: Deaths in 936 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 936 CE: Al-Ash'ari — scholar (Defender of Sunni Theology — Founder of Ash'ari Kalam) in Basra / Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Ibn Muqla dismissed and mutilated
description: The famous calligrapher and vizier Ibn Muqla was dismissed from office and had his right hand cut off on orders of Caliph al-Radi. Despite his mutilation, he continued to write with his left hand until his death.
outcome: Ibn Muqla's calligraphic innovations in Arabic script endured despite his tragic end.

title: Death of Abu Bakr al-Khallal
description: Abu Bakr Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Khallal, the great Hanbali jurist and compiler of Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal's legal opinions, died in Baghdad. He played a crucial role in systematizing the Hanbali school of Islamic jurisprudence.
outcome: His compilation work preserved and organized the Hanbali legal tradition for future generations.

title: Deaths in 940 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 940 CE: Abu Ishaq Shami — Sufi mystic (Syrian Mystic — Key Bridge in the Chishti Silsila) in Syria / Chisht, Afghanistan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Farabi writes The Virtuous City
description: Al-Farabi completes his masterwork on political philosophy, combining Plato's Republic with Islamic political thought to envision the ideal Islamic state.
sources: Al-Farabi, Kitab Ara Ahl al-Madina al-Fadila
outcome: Created Islamic political philosophy; influenced Ibn Sina, Maimonides, and Aquinas

title: Death of al-Maturidi in Samarkand
description: Abu Mansur al-Maturidi, founder of the Maturidi school of theology, died in Samarkand. His theological works provided an alternative to Ash'arism and became dominant in Central Asian and Hanafi circles.
outcome: Maturidism became the dominant theological school for Hanafi Muslims, especially in Central and South Asia.

title: Deaths in 941 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 941 CE: Al-Kulayni — scholar (Thiqa al-Islam — Greatest Shia Hadith Compiler) in Kulain (Rey) / Baghdad; Rudaki — poet (Father of Persian Poetry) in Rudak, Khorasan (Tajikistan).
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Death of Saadia Gaon
description: Saadia ben Joseph al-Fayyumi, known as Saadia Gaon, died in Baghdad after serving as head of the Sura Academy. A towering Jewish scholar who wrote in Arabic, he engaged deeply with Islamic Kalam theology and philosophy, creating the first systematic Jewish theology.
outcome: His works in Arabic exemplified the intellectual cross-pollination between Jewish and Islamic thought in the Islamic Golden Age.

title: Deaths in 943 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 943 CE: Sinan ibn Thabit — scientist (Physician who organised the first system of public hospitals in Baghdad) in Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Maturidi dies in Samarkand
description: Abu Mansur al-Maturidi, founder of the Maturidi school of Sunni theology, died in Samarkand. His rationalist approach to theology paralleled and sometimes rivaled Ash'ari thought, becoming dominant among Hanafi communities.
sources: Rudolph, Al-Maturidi and Sunni Theology in Samarqand
outcome: Maturidi theology became the standard creed for Hanafi Muslims across Central Asia, Turkey, and the Indian subcontinent.

title: Deaths in 944 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 944 CE: Al-Maturidi — scholar (Imam al-Huda — Founder of Maturidi Kalam) in Samarkand, Uzbekistan; Abu Mansur al-Maturidi — philosopher (Philosopher of Samarkand) in Samarkand.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Death of Al-Istakhri
description: Abu Ishaq Ibrahim ibn Muhammad al-Istakhri, the Persian geographer and traveler, died after completing his influential geographical work Kitab Masalik al-Mamalik (Book of Routes and Kingdoms). His work provided detailed descriptions of the Islamic world's regions and trade routes.
outcome: His geographical work became a standard reference and influenced later geographers including al-Muqaddasi.

title: Deaths in 945 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 945 CE: Abu Bakr al-Shibli — Sufi mystic (Shaykh Baghdad — Companion of al-Hallaj) in Baghdad, Iraq; Al-Hamdani — scholar (Tongue of Yemen — Arab Geographer) in Sanaa, Yemen.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Byzantine recapture of Crete begins
description: The Byzantine general Constantine Porphyrogenitus launched a major but unsuccessful campaign to recapture Crete from Muslim rule. The island had been under Arab control since the 820s.
outcome: Though this campaign failed, Byzantine efforts continued until Nikephoros Phokas recaptured the island in 961.

title: Ahmad ibn Buya enters Baghdad
description: Ahmad ibn Buya, founder of the Buyid dynasty, entered Baghdad and was granted the title Mu'izz al-Dawla by Caliph al-Mustakfi. The Buyids, who were Shi'a, now controlled the Sunni Abbasid caliphate.
outcome: Buyid rule marked a century of Shi'a political dominance over the Sunni Abbasid caliphate.

title: Deaths in 946 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 946 CE: Al-Suli — scholar (Greatest Chess Master of the Medieval Islamic World) in Baghdad, Iraq; Ibrahim ibn Sinan — scientist (Mathematician who advanced the geometry of conic sections and sundials) in Baghdad.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Deaths in 950 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 950 CE: Al-Farabi — philosopher (al-Mu'allim al-Thani — The Second Teacher) in Farab / Baghdad / Damascus.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Mutanabbi — greatest Arabic poet — flourishes
description: Ahmad ibn al-Husayn al-Mutanabbi, universally regarded as the greatest Arabic poet, composes his masterworks at the court of Sayf al-Dawla in Aleppo.

figures.name: Al-Mutanabbi
figures.role: poet; composed at Sayf al-Dawla's court in Aleppo

outcome: Considered the greatest Arabic poet; his verse is still memorised and quoted across the Arab world

title: Qarmatians return the Black Stone to Mecca
description: The Qarmatian sect returned the Black Stone to Mecca after holding it for 21 years following their sack of the city in 930. The ransom negotiations involved the Abbasid and Fatimid caliphates.
outcome: The return allowed pilgrimage to resume normally and marked the declining power of the Qarmatian movement.

title: Al-Mas'udi completes Muruj al-Dhahab
description: Ali ibn al-Husayn al-Mas'udi completed his great historical and geographical work Muruj al-Dhahab wa-Ma'adin al-Jawhar (Meadows of Gold and Mines of Gems). The work synthesized geography, history, and ethnography from his extensive travels.
outcome: Muruj al-Dhahab became a foundational text for Islamic historical and geographical knowledge.

title: Al-Mas'udi dies in Egypt
description: Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn al-Husayn al-Mas'udi, the great historian and geographer known as the 'Herodotus of the Arabs,' died in Egypt. His comprehensive historical and geographical works, especially Muruj al-Dhahab, combined history, geography, and ethnography in unprecedented ways.
outcome: His innovative approach to historical writing influenced later Islamic historiography and preserved invaluable information about pre-Islamic and early Islamic societies.

title: Ibn Hawqal begins his travels
description: The merchant and geographer Ibn Hawqal began his extensive travels throughout the Islamic world, which would last nearly thirty years. His observations formed the basis for his geographical masterwork Kitab Surat al-Ard.
outcome: His detailed descriptions of trade routes and cities became essential sources for Islamic economic history.

title: Al-Mas'udi writes Meadows of Gold
description: Ali al-Mas'udi completes Muruj al-Dhahab (Meadows of Gold), an encyclopedic work combining history, geography, and natural science — he is later called the 'Herodotus of the Arabs'.
outcome: Created a model of universal history combining science, geography, and culture

title: Death of al-Istakhri
description: The Persian geographer Abu Ishaq al-Istakhri died after producing his major work Kitab al-Masalik wa'l-Mamalik. His atlas of the Islamic world with detailed maps influenced later geographers including Ibn Hawqal.
sources: Miquel, La géographie humaine du monde musulman
outcome: His geographical system and maps established conventions used by Islamic cartographers for centuries.

title: Al-Idrisi the Elder founds Hammudid dynasty in Morocco
description: Ali ibn Hammud al-Idrisi established control over parts of northern Morocco, founding what would become the Hammudid dynasty. His descendants would later rule Málaga and Algeciras in al-Andalus.
outcome: The Hammudids represented Idrisid legitimacy and would challenge Umayyad power in Andalusia.

title: Death of Al-Mutanabbi
description: Abu al-Tayyib Ahmad ibn al-Husayn al-Mutanabbi, widely considered the greatest classical Arabic poet, was killed by bandits near Baghdad while traveling from Shiraz to Kufa. His powerful panegyrics and profound poetry had made him famous across the Islamic world.
outcome: His death while reportedly refusing to flee from attackers became as legendary as his poetry, which remains canonical in Arabic literature.

title: Deaths in 956 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 956 CE: Al-Masudi — historian (Herodotus of the Arabs) in Baghdad / Cairo / Fustat.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 957 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 957 CE: Al-Istakhri — scholar (Author of the Book of Climates) in Persepolis / Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 960 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 960 CE: Ahmad ibn Fadlan — scholar (Abbasid traveler who recorded the Vikings on the Volga) in Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Conversion of the Karakhanids — Islam enters the Turkic steppe
description: The Karakhanid ruler Satuq Bughra Khan converts to Islam, bringing the first major Turkic dynasty into the Islamic world and opening Central Asia to Turkish-Islamic civilisation.
outcome: First mass Turkic conversion to Islam; opened the path for the Seljuks, Ottomans, and Mughals

title: Nicephorus Phocas reconquers Crete
description: The Byzantine general Nicephorus Phocas successfully besieged and captured Chandax (Heraklion) after a long campaign, ending 135 years of Muslim rule in Crete. The island's Arab population was killed or enslaved.
outcome: The reconquest secured Byzantine control of the eastern Mediterranean and disrupted Muslim naval power.

title: Al-Hakam II succeeds Abd al-Rahman III
description: Al-Hakam II became Caliph of Córdoba upon his father Abd al-Rahman III's death. He was known as a great patron of learning and expanded the library of Córdoba to over 400,000 volumes.
outcome: His reign represented the cultural zenith of the Umayyad Caliphate of Córdoba.

title: Death of Abd al-Rahman III
description: Abd al-Rahman III al-Nasir, the greatest Umayyad ruler of al-Andalus who had proclaimed himself caliph in 929, died after a reign of nearly fifty years. Under his rule, Córdoba became the most prosperous and culturally advanced city in Western Europe.
outcome: His reign represented the zenith of Islamic power and culture in the Iberian Peninsula.

title: Al-Hamdani dies in Yemeni prison
description: Abu Muhammad al-Hasan ibn Ahmad al-Hamdani, the great Yemeni geographer, historian, and poet known as 'the tongue of Yemen,' dies imprisoned in Sana'a. His geographical work Sifat Jazirat al-Arab (Description of the Arabian Peninsula) remains the most comprehensive medieval survey of Arabian geography and tribal genealogy.
outcome: His geographical and ethnographical works on Arabia became indispensable sources for understanding pre-modern Arabian Peninsula.

title: Alptigin founds Ghaznavid state
description: The Turkish slave-soldier Alptigin seized control of Ghazna after losing a power struggle in the Samanid court. His establishment of an independent principality laid foundations for the future Ghaznavid Empire.
outcome: The Ghaznavid state became a major power that would later extend Islam into the Indian subcontinent.

title: Alptigin establishes himself in Ghazna
description: The Turkish slave-commander Alptigin, after falling from favor with the Samanids, seized the fortress town of Ghazna in Afghanistan. This marked the foundation of what would become the Ghaznavid dynasty.
outcome: Ghazna became the center of a powerful sultanate that would conquer much of northern India.

title: Deaths in 965 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 965 CE: Al-Mutanabbi — poet (Greatest Arab Poet of the Classical Era) in Kufa / Aleppo / Shiraz.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Death of Al-Mutanabbi's contemporary Al-Qali
description: Abu Ali Isma'il ibn al-Qasim al-Qali, the great philologist and lexicographer from Baghdad who had settled in Córdoba, died. He brought Eastern Arabic scholarship to al-Andalus and compiled the important linguistic work Kitab al-Amali.
outcome: His presence in Córdoba strengthened the intellectual connections between the Eastern and Western Islamic worlds.

title: Deaths in 967 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 967 CE: Abu al-Faraj al-Isfahani — scholar (Author of the Book of Songs — Greatest Arabic Literary Anthology) in Isfahan / Aleppo / Baghdad; Sayf al-Dawla — ruler (Patron of al-Mutanabbi — Hamdanid Prince of Aleppo) in Aleppo, Syria.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Kilwa Kisiwani emerges as major Swahili trading port
description: The East African city of Kilwa on the Swahili coast emerged as a major trading center under the Shirazi dynasty. The city controlled gold trade from Zimbabwe and connected African interior trade to the Indian Ocean network.
sources: Chittick, Kilwa: An Islamic Trading City
outcome: Kilwa became the dominant power on the Swahili coast and a key node in Indian Ocean trade for centuries.

title: Jawhar al-Siqilli begins conquest of North Africa
description: The Fatimid general Jawhar al-Siqilli led military campaigns westward from Egypt, consolidating Fatimid control over North Africa. A former Slavic slave who rose to become the most powerful Fatimid commander, he would later found Cairo.
outcome: These campaigns secured Fatimid dominance over the Maghreb before their eastward expansion into Egypt.

title: Deaths in 968 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 968 CE: Abu Firas al-Hamdani — poet (Hamdanid prince-poet and author of the Rumiyyat prison poems) in Mosul.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Fatimids conquer Egypt and found Cairo
description: The Fatimid general Jawhar al-Siqilli conquers Egypt and founds the new city of al-Qahira (Cairo) as the Fatimid capital, shifting the dynasty's power base from Tunisia to the Nile.
outcome: Founded Cairo — one of the greatest cities in Islamic history; Egypt became the Fatimid heartland

title: Seljuq converts to Islam
description: Seljuq ibn Duqaq, chief of the Oghuz Turkish Qiniq clan, converted to Sunni Islam near Jand. His descendants would form the Great Seljuk Empire that dominated the Middle East in the 11th century.
outcome: The conversion initiated the process that brought the Seljuks into Islamic civilization and Middle Eastern politics.

title: Fatimids found al-Qahira
description: Fatimid general Jawhar al-Siqilli founded the city of al-Qahira (Cairo) as the new Fatimid capital after the conquest of Egypt. The city was designed as a royal enclosure for the Fatimid court and army.
outcome: Cairo became one of the great capitals of the Islamic world and remains Egypt's capital today.

title: Al-Azhar Mosque founded in Cairo
description: The Fatimids found al-Azhar Mosque in Cairo, which becomes al-Azhar University — the oldest degree-granting university in the world and the foremost centre of Sunni learning to this day.
outcome: Founded the institution that remains the world's foremost centre of Islamic scholarship

title: Al-Azhar Mosque completed in Cairo
description: The construction of al-Azhar Mosque was completed under Fatimid Caliph al-Mu'izz li-Din Allah. Initially serving as the congregational mosque of the new city, it soon became a center of learning.
outcome: Al-Azhar would become the most important center of Islamic learning in the Sunni world.

title: Bulghar becomes capital of Volga Bulgaria
description: The city of Bulghar on the Volga River was established as the capital of Volga Bulgaria, a Muslim Turkic state that served as the northernmost outpost of Islamic civilization. The city became a major center for trade between the Muslim world and northern Europe.
outcome: Bulghar became the northern terminus of the Volga trade route and a crucial link between Islamic and Viking civilizations.

title: Al-Azhar becomes a university
description: Al-Azhar transitions from a mosque to a full university with regular courses in Islamic law, theology, logic, grammar, and the sciences — the oldest degree-granting university in the world.
sources: Dodge, Al-Azhar: A Millennium of Muslim Learning
outcome: World's oldest degree-granting university; remains the supreme authority in Sunni scholarship

title: Al-Muizz enters Cairo
description: The Fatimid caliph al-Muizz li-Din Allah ceremonially entered Cairo from Tunisia, transferring the center of Fatimid power to Egypt. General Jawhar al-Siqilli had conquered Egypt and founded Cairo in 969.
sources: Brett, The Rise of the Fatimids
outcome: Cairo became the capital of a major caliphate that challenged Abbasid and Umayyad legitimacy.

title: Al-Mu'izz enters Cairo
description: Fatimid Caliph al-Mu'izz li-Din Allah made his ceremonial entry into Cairo from Tunisia, establishing it as the seat of the Fatimid Caliphate. He brought with him the bodies of his ancestors and the treasures of the dynasty.
outcome: The Fatimids now ruled from the center of the Islamic world, challenging Abbasid authority.

title: Adud al-Dawla becomes sole Buyid ruler
description: Adud al-Dawla of the Buyid dynasty consolidated power over all Buyid territories, ruling from Baghdad to Iran. He initiated extensive building projects including hospitals, libraries, and irrigation works, presiding over a cultural renaissance despite being a Shi'i ruler over a predominantly Sunni population.
outcome: His reign represented the height of Buyid power and cultural achievement in the Islamic world.

title: Deaths in 974 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 974 CE: Al-Qadi al-Nu'man — scholar (Chief jurist of the Fatimid Caliphate and founder of Ismaili law) in Cairo.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Aziz billah expands Fatimid empire
description: Fatimid Caliph al-Aziz expands the empire to include Syria and the Hijaz, making the Fatimids the most powerful Muslim dynasty controlling Mecca, Medina, Jerusalem, and Cairo.
outcome: Fatimids at their peak: controlled the three holiest cities of Islam simultaneously

title: Death of Al-Hakam II
description: Al-Hakam II, the Umayyad Caliph of Córdoba and great patron of learning, died after expanding the Córdoba library to over 400,000 volumes. His peaceful reign focused on culture and scholarship rather than military conquest, making Córdoba a center of learning.
outcome: His death led to the rise of Hajib al-Mansur as the real power behind the throne and eventual political instability.

title: Sabuktigin succeeds Alptigin in Ghazna
description: The Turkish slave commander Sabuktigin secured control of Ghazna and began expanding into Khurasan and the Indian subcontinent. He established the Ghaznavid dynasty as a major independent power.
outcome: Sabuktigin's reign prepared the ground for his son Mahmud's massive conquests into India.

title: Sabuktigin raids Peshawar region
description: Sabuktigin of Ghazna, Alptigin's successor, launched raids into the Peshawar region against the Hindu Shahi kingdom. These campaigns marked the beginning of sustained Muslim military pressure on northern India.
outcome: These raids paved the way for Mahmud of Ghazna's later conquests in India.

title: Ibn al-Haytham born in Basra
description: Abu Ali al-Hasan ibn al-Haytham, known in the West as Alhazen, was born in Basra. He would become the father of modern optics and one of the most important scientists in history, making groundbreaking contributions to physics, astronomy, mathematics, and the scientific method itself.
outcome: His future work on optics would revolutionize the understanding of light and vision in both Islamic and European science.

title: Sabuktigin defeats Jayapala at Battle of Ghazni
description: Sabuktigin, the Ghaznavid ruler, defeats the Hindu Shahi king Jayapala in battle near Ghazni when Jayapala attempts to halt Ghaznavid expansion eastward. This victory consolidated Ghaznavid control over the region between Ghazni and Peshawar, opening the path for future invasions into the Indian subcontinent.
outcome: This defeat marked the beginning of the decline of the Hindu Shahi dynasty and set the stage for Mahmud's later conquests in India.

title: Deaths in 979 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 979 CE: Ibn al-Jazzar — scientist (Tunisian physician whose Viaticum became a standard European medical text) in Kairouan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Ibn Sina born — greatest Islamic polymath
description: Abu Ali ibn Sina (Avicenna) is born near Bukhara; he will write the Canon of Medicine and the Book of Healing, shaping both Islamic and European science for centuries.

figures.name: Ibn Sina
figures.role: born; future author of the Canon of Medicine

outcome: His Canon was the standard medical textbook in Europe until the 17th century

title: Adud al-Dawla builds hospital in Baghdad
description: The Buyid ruler Adud al-Dawla constructed the great Bimaristan al-Adudi hospital in Baghdad with 24 physicians and facilities for teaching medicine. It became a model for Islamic hospitals.
outcome: The hospital represented the peak of Islamic medical institution-building in the medieval period.

title: Ibn Yunus begins astronomical observations in Cairo
description: The Egyptian astronomer Ibn Yunus began systematic astronomical observations at the Fatimid court in Cairo. He compiled the Hakimi zij, dedicated to al-Hakim, which included precise calculations of planetary motion.
sources: Saliba, A History of Arabic Astronomy
outcome: His work refined astronomical tables and influenced later Islamic and European astronomy.

title: Deaths in 982 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 982 CE: Ibn Khafif — Sufi mystic (Shaykh al-Kabir — The Great Shaykh of Shiraz) in Shiraz, Iran.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Death of Al-Muqaddasi's teacher Ibn Hawqal
description: Abu al-Qasim ibn Hawqal, the great Arab geographer and traveler, died after completing his comprehensive geographical work Kitab Surat al-Ard (The Face of the Earth). He had traveled throughout the Islamic world from Spain to Central Asia, documenting trade routes, cities, and peoples.
outcome: His detailed geographical descriptions provided invaluable information about the 10th-century Islamic world's economy and society.

title: Deaths in 984 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 984 CE: Lubna of Cordoba — scholar (Mathematician, scribe, and librarian of the Cordoban caliphal court) in Cordoba.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 985 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 985 CE: Al-Sijistani — philosopher (Philosopher of Baghdad — Custodian of Philosophy) in Baghdad, Iraq.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Muqaddasi writes the best geographical survey
description: Muhammad al-Muqaddasi completes Ahsan al-Taqasim, the finest medieval geographical work, based on his personal travels across every region of the Islamic world.

figures.name: Al-Muqaddasi
figures.role: author; personally traveled every region he described

sources: Al-Muqaddasi, Ahsan al-Taqasim fi Ma'rifat al-Aqalim
outcome: Created the most accurate and detailed geography of the medieval Islamic world

title: Al-Maqdisi completes Ahsan al-Taqasim
description: The geographer al-Maqdisi (al-Muqaddasi) completed his masterwork describing the Islamic world based on personal travels. He provided detailed accounts of cities, trade routes, peoples, and regional economies.
sources: Al-Maqdisi, Ahsan al-Taqasim fi Ma'rifat al-Aqalim
outcome: His geography provided the most comprehensive description of Islamic lands at the end of the 10th century.

title: Al-Muqaddasi completes Ahsan al-Taqasim
description: The geographer al-Muqaddasi (also known as al-Maqdisi) completed his comprehensive geographical work Ahsan al-Taqasim fi Ma'rifat al-Aqalim. Based on his personal travels, it provided detailed descriptions of Islamic lands organized by climate zones.
outcome: His work became the most detailed geographical survey of the Islamic world in the classical period.

title: Ibn Yunus builds observatory at al-Qarafa
description: The Egyptian astronomer Ibn Yunus established an observatory at al-Qarafa near Cairo under Fatimid patronage, where he made precise astronomical observations. His work would culminate in the al-Zij al-Kabir al-Hakimi, one of the most accurate astronomical tables of the medieval period.
outcome: His observations improved the accuracy of astronomical calculations and influenced later Islamic and European astronomy.

title: Ibn al-Nadim completes the Fihrist
description: Abu al-Faraj Muhammad ibn Ishaq al-Nadim completes his monumental bibliographical work al-Fihrist (The Catalogue) in Baghdad. This comprehensive index catalogued all books available in Arabic, listing thousands of works across sciences, philosophy, theology, and literature, providing invaluable information about now-lost texts.
outcome: The Fihrist became the most important medieval bibliographical source, preserving knowledge of thousands of works and authors that would otherwise be unknown.

title: Deaths in 986 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 986 CE: Al-Sufi — scientist (Azophi — First to Describe the Andromeda Galaxy) in Isfahan / Shiraz, Iran.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 987 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 987 CE: Abu Sulayman Sijistani — philosopher (Philosopher of Baghdad) in Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Hajib al-Mansur becomes de facto ruler of al-Andalus
description: Muhammad ibn Abi Amir al-Mansur (Almanzor) consolidated power as hajib to the young caliph Hisham II, becoming the effective ruler of al-Andalus. He launched frequent raids into Christian territories in the north.
sources: Scales, The Fall of the Caliphate of Córdoba
outcome: Al-Mansur's dictatorship brought military success but undermined caliphal authority, contributing to the caliphate's later collapse.

title: Hugh Capet sends embassy to Córdoba
description: King Hugh Capet of France sent an embassy to Caliph Hisham II in Córdoba seeking diplomatic relations. The embassy reflected the prestige and power of the Umayyad Caliphate of Córdoba in European affairs.
outcome: The embassy demonstrated Córdoba's role as a great power in western Europe.

title: Bardas Skleros rebels against Byzantium
description: The Byzantine general Bardas Skleros, after being defeated, fled to Baghdad and was received by the Buyid ruler. His presence in the Islamic world created a diplomatic crisis and highlighted the complex relationships between the Byzantine Empire and Muslim states, particularly regarding military refugees.
outcome: The incident demonstrated the pragmatic diplomacy between Muslim rulers and Byzantine dissidents during this period.

title: Deaths in 988 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 988 CE: Abu Nasr al-Sarraj — scholar (Imam Ahl al-Tasawwuf) in Tus, Iran; Al-Sarraj al-Tusi — scholar (First Encyclopaedist of Sufism) in Tus, Khorasan; Ibn Hawqal — scholar (Traveller-Geographer — Surat al-Ard) in Nisibis / Cordoba / Cairo.
outcome: 3 notable figures died this year.

title: Volga Bulgars adopt Islam
description: Khan Almish of Volga Bulgaria officially adopted Islam and requested religious assistance from the Abbasid caliph al-Muqtadir. The conversion connected the Volga trade route to the Islamic commercial network.
sources: Golden, An Introduction to the History of the Turkic Peoples
outcome: The conversion established Islam in the Volga region and influenced later Islamization of the Tatars.

title: Deaths in 990 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 990 CE: Ibn al-Nadim — scholar (Author of the Fihrist — First Bio-Bibliographic Catalogue) in Baghdad, Iraq.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Mahmud succeeds Sabuktigin in Ghazna
description: Mahmud ibn Sabuktigin became ruler of Ghazna after defeating his brother Ismail in a succession struggle. He immediately began campaigns to expand his realm and assert independence from the Samanids.
outcome: Mahmud would transform Ghazna into a major empire spanning from Iran to northern India.

title: Mahmud captures Khorasan
description: Mahmud of Ghazna seized Khorasan from the declining Samanid Empire, establishing Ghaznavid control over this wealthy province. This conquest provided the resources and base for his future expansion into India and his patronage of Persian culture.
outcome: The conquest marked the beginning of Ghaznavid dominance in eastern Iran and prepared the way for expansion into India.

title: Deaths in 991 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 991 CE: Al-Muqaddasi — scholar (Best Divisions for Knowledge of the Regions) in Jerusalem / Baghdad; Ibn Babawayh — scholar (Shaykh al-Saduq — Master of Shia Hadith) in Qum / Baghdad / Khorasan.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Battle of Peshkopi
description: The Byzantine emperor Basil II defeated the Fatimid armies in northern Syria near Peshkopi. The victory consolidated Byzantine control over northern Syria and Aleppo after years of warfare.
sources: Bianquis, Damas et la Syrie sous la domination fatimide
outcome: The defeat weakened Fatimid expansion into Syria and established a frontier between the two empires.

title: Battle of Maldon referenced in Arabic chronicles
description: Viking raids on England, including the famous Battle of Maldon, were noted in Arabic geographical and historical works, demonstrating the extent of information networks in the medieval Islamic world. Muslim scholars tracked Viking movements from their contacts with Scandinavian traders along the Volga route.
outcome: These records show how information flowed between northern Europe and the Islamic world through trade networks.

title: Deaths in 992 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 992 CE: Al-Amiri — philosopher (Islamic Philosopher on Happiness and Religion) in Nishapur / Baghdad / Bukhara; Jawhar al-Siqilli — ruler (Fatimid General — Founder of Cairo and al-Azhar) in Sicily / Cairo, Egypt.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Deaths in 994 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 994 CE: Haly Abbas — scientist (Persian physician and author of the Complete Book of Medical Art) in Shiraz.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Mansur sacks Santiago de Compostela
description: The hajib al-Mansur led a massive raid that captured and destroyed Santiago de Compostela, one of Christianity's holiest sites. He spared the tomb of Saint James but enslaved the population and carried away the church bells to Córdoba.
sources: Barton and Fletcher, The World of El Cid
outcome: The raid demonstrated Andalusian military supremacy but intensified Christian determination to reconquer Iberia.

title: Qarakhanids adopt Islam officially
description: Satuq Bughra Khan's successors formalized the conversion of the Qarakhanid Khanate to Islam, making it the first Turkic dynasty to officially embrace the religion. This marked Islam's expansion into the Central Asian steppes.
outcome: The conversion opened Central Asia and later Anatolia to Turkic Muslim dynasties.

title: Al-Biruni enters Khwarazmian court
description: The young scholar Abu Rayhan al-Biruni began his career at the court of the Khwarazmshahs in Central Asia, where he studied mathematics, astronomy, and geography. He would become one of the greatest polymaths in Islamic history, making groundbreaking contributions to multiple sciences.
outcome: This marked the beginning of a career that would produce major works on India, astronomy, mathematics, and comparative religion.

title: Deaths in 996 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 996 CE: Abu Talib al-Makki — scholar (Qut al-Qulub — The Nourishment of Hearts) in Mecca / Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah becomes Fatimid Caliph
description: The eccentric and controversial al-Hakim becomes Fatimid Caliph at age 11, beginning a reign marked by both brilliant patronage of science and erratic persecution.
outcome: His mysterious disappearance in 1021 gave rise to the Druze faith

title: Mahmud of Ghazna raids Peshawar
description: Sultan Mahmud launched his first major raid into the Indian subcontinent, attacking Peshawar and the frontier regions. This began his series of seventeen expeditions into India over the next three decades.
outcome: The raids enriched Ghazna, weakened Indian kingdoms, and expanded Islamic influence in the subcontinent.

title: Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah becomes Fatimid Caliph
description: Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah, aged eleven, became the sixth Fatimid Caliph after his father al-Aziz's death. His reign would be marked by controversial religious policies and mystical claims.
outcome: His eccentric rule would challenge Fatimid stability and later inspire the Druze faith.

title: Mahmud raids Ghazni and establishes permanent control
description: Mahmud of Ghazna consolidated his power by defeating rivals and establishing Ghazni as the capital of an expanding empire. He began organizing the military campaigns into India that would define his reign and spread Islam eastward.
outcome: This consolidation created the base for the Ghaznavid dynasty's expansion into the Indian subcontinent.

title: Deaths in 998 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 998 CE: Abu al-Wafa Buzjani — scientist (Persian mathematician who advanced trigonometry and introduced the tangent function) in Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Mahmud of Ghazna begins conquests into India
description: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazna launches the first of his seventeen campaigns into the Indian subcontinent, establishing Ghaznavid rule and bringing Islam deep into South Asia.
outcome: Began the systematic spread of Islam into the Indian subcontinent

title: Deaths in 999 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 999 CE: Sahl al-Su'luki — scholar (Mufti of Nishapur — Co-Mujaddid of the 4th Century) in Nishapur, Iran.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Firdawsi completes the Shahnameh
description: Abu al-Qasim Firdawsi completes the Shahnameh (Book of Kings), the Persian national epic of 50,000 couplets recounting Iran's pre-Islamic history — the longest poem by a single author.

figures.name: Firdowsi
figures.role: poet; completed the 50,000-couplet Persian national epic

outcome: Preserved Persian identity within Islam; the greatest literary work in the Persian language

title: Qarakhanids conquer Bukhara from Samanids
description: The Turkic Qarakhanid dynasty under Nasr Khan captured Bukhara, ending Samanid rule in Transoxiana. The conquest marked the definitive establishment of Turkic political dominance in Central Asia.
sources: Barthold, Turkestan Down to the Mongol Invasion
outcome: The fall of the Samanids ended the last major Iranian dynasty in Central Asia before Turkic domination.

title: Deaths in 1000 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1000 CE: Abu Bakr al-Kalabadhi — scholar (Systematizer of Early Sufi Doctrine) in Bukhara, Transoxiana; Abu Sahl al-Quhi — scientist (Persian mathematician who advanced conic sections and mathematical centres of gravity) in Baghdad; Abu-Mahmud Khojandi — scientist (Persian astronomer who built the great mural sextant of Rayy) in Khujand; Abu Dulaf al-Khazraji — traveler (Abbasid-era traveler who wrote two Risalas on Central Asia and China) in Aleppo.
outcome: 4 notable figures died this year.

title: Death of Gerbert of Aurillac (Pope Sylvester II)
description: Pope Sylvester II, who studied in Córdoba and brought Arabic numerals and astronomical instruments to Christian Europe, died in Rome. He had learned mathematics and astronomy from Muslim scholars in Al-Andalus during his youth.
outcome: His transmission of Islamic scientific knowledge profoundly influenced medieval European learning.

title: Mahmud of Ghazni invades the Shahi Kingdom
description: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni launched the first of his seventeen expeditions into the Indian subcontinent, attacking the Hindu Shahi dynasty in the Punjab region. This marked the beginning of sustained Ghaznavid military campaigns that would reshape the political landscape of northern India.
outcome: Established pattern of Ghaznavid raids into India that brought immense wealth to Ghazni and facilitated gradual Islamic expansion into the subcontinent.

title: Al-Qadir issues the Qadiri Creed
description: Abbasid Caliph al-Qadir bi-Amr Allah issued an official creed condemning certain Mu'tazilite and Shi'ite theological positions. This represented a major assertion of Sunni orthodoxy backed by caliphal authority in Baghdad.
outcome: The Qadiri Creed became a foundation for Sunni theological orthodoxy under Abbasid sponsorship.

title: Al-Hakim establishes Dar al-Ilm in Cairo
description: Fatimid Caliph al-Hakim founds the Dar al-Ilm (House of Knowledge) in Cairo, a major library and academy rivalling Baghdad's House of Wisdom.
outcome: Created a rival intellectual centre to Baghdad under Fatimid patronage

title: Fatimids capture Aleppo
description: Fatimid forces under general Lu'lu' al-Kabir captured the strategic city of Aleppo from the Hamdanid dynasty. This extended Fatimid control deeper into Syria and threatened Byzantine frontiers.
outcome: Fatimid expansion reached its northern extent in Syria before later decline.

title: Death of Al-Muqaddasi
description: Al-Muqaddasi (Shams al-Din Abu Abdullah Muhammad ibn Ahmad), the pioneering Arab geographer, died after completing his masterwork 'Ahsan al-Taqasim fi Ma'rifat al-Aqalim' (The Best Divisions for Knowledge of the Regions). His work provided detailed descriptions of Islamic lands from personal travel observations, including urban life, commerce, and regional characteristics.
outcome: His geographical work became a foundational text for Islamic cartography and regional studies.

title: Deaths in 1008 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1008 CE: Badi al-Zaman al-Hamadhani — scholar (Wonder of the Age — Inventor of the Maqama Literary Form) in Hamadan / Herat.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Death of Al-Muqaddasi
description: The geographer al-Muqaddasi, author of Ahsan al-Taqasim fi Ma'rifat al-Aqalim, died after traveling extensively throughout the Islamic world. His work provided detailed descriptions of Muslim lands from North Africa to Central Asia.
outcome: His geographical work remained a key reference for understanding Islamic world geography for centuries.

title: Deaths in 1009 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1009 CE: Ibn Yunus — scientist (Egyptian astronomer who compiled the great Hakimi Zij) in Cairo.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Hakim orders destruction of Church of Holy Sepulchre
description: Fatimid Caliph al-Hakim orders the destruction of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem, an act later used as justification for the First Crusade.
outcome: Provided one of the pretexts for the Crusades nearly a century later

title: Mahmud of Ghazni conquers Ghur
description: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni conquered the mountainous region of Ghur in central Afghanistan, expanding his empire's territorial control. The region's strategic location made it vital for controlling trade routes between Central Asia and India.
outcome: Extended Ghaznavid control over Afghanistan's interior and secured military recruitment grounds that later produced the Ghurid dynasty.

title: Sulayman al-Mustain declares himself Caliph in Córdoba
description: During the Fitna of al-Andalus, Sulayman al-Mustain seized power in Córdoba with Berber support, initiating a series of civil wars. His conflict with Muhammad II al-Mahdi accelerated the fragmentation of the Umayyad Caliphate.
outcome: This marked a critical phase in the civil war that destroyed centralized Umayyad rule in al-Andalus.

title: Sulayhid Dynasty established in Yemen
description: Ali ibn Muhammad al-Sulayhi established the Sulayhid Dynasty in Yemen, aligning with the Fatimid Caliphate. The dynasty would become known for its Ismaili propagation and the prominent role of Queen Arwa.
outcome: The Sulayhids became key Fatimid allies controlling Yemen and maritime trade routes.

title: Deaths in 1010 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1010 CE: Ali ibn Isa al-Kahhal — scientist (Greatest ophthalmologist of the medieval Islamic world) in Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Ferdowsi completes the Shahnameh
description: The Persian poet Ferdowsi completed his epic masterwork, the Shahnameh (Book of Kings), at the court of Mahmud of Ghazni. This monumental poem of 60,000 couplets preserved Persian language, culture, and pre-Islamic Iranian history.
outcome: Became the national epic of Persian-speaking peoples and established classical Persian as a literary language for centuries.

title: Deaths in 1012 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1012 CE: Al-Qabisi — scholar (Imam al-Maliki of Qayrawan) in Qayrawan, Tunisia.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1013 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1013 CE: Al-Baqillani — scholar (Sword of the Sunna — Master of Asharite Kalam) in Basra / Baghdad; Al-Zahrawi — scientist (Abulcasis — Father of Modern Surgery) in Medina al-Zahra, Cordoba, Spain.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Sacking of Córdoba by Berber troops
description: Berber forces sacked Córdoba during the Fitna of al-Andalus, devastating the once-great Umayyad capital. The chaos resulted in the dispersal of scholars and the destruction of libraries.
outcome: The sacking marked the effective end of centralized Umayyad power in Iberia.

title: Death of Gerbert of Aurillac (Pope Sylvester II)
description: Pope Sylvester II (Gerbert of Aurillac), who had studied mathematics and astronomy in Islamic Córdoba and Catalonia, died in Rome. He was instrumental in transmitting Arabic numerals and the astrolabe to Christian Europe after learning from Muslim scholars in Al-Andalus.
sources: William of Malmesbury, Gesta Regum Anglorum
outcome: His papacy marked a crucial moment in the transmission of Islamic scientific knowledge to medieval Europe.

title: Deaths in 1016 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1016 CE: Sharif al-Radi — poet (Poet of the Hashimites) in Baghdad, Iraq; Abu Hamid al-Isfarayini — scholar (Shaykh al-Shafi'iyya — Head of the Shafi'is in Baghdad) in Baghdad, Iraq.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Mahmud of Ghazni sacks Mathura and Kanauj
description: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni captured and plundered the wealthy Hindu cities of Mathura and Kanauj in northern India. The raids targeted temple treasuries and resulted in massive wealth transfer to Ghazni.
outcome: Demonstrated the vulnerability of northern Indian kingdoms to centralized military power and enriched Ghaznavid coffers with unprecedented plunder.

title: Death of Ibn Hazm's patron during Córdoba sacking
description: The vizier Ibn Abi 'Amir, patron of the young Ibn Hazm, was killed during one of the many sackings of Córdoba during the Fitna. This traumatic experience profoundly shaped Ibn Hazm's later intellectual development and his zahiri literalist approach.
outcome: The fall of Córdoba during the Fitna scattered its scholars and led to the rise of the Taifa kingdoms.

title: Death of Baha al-Dawla
description: Baha al-Dawla, the Buyid ruler who controlled Baghdad and much of Iraq and Persia, died in Shiraz. His reign saw the continued decline of Abbasid power and Buyid authority.
outcome: His death accelerated Buyid fragmentation, paving the way for Seljuk dominance.

title: Deaths in 1020 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1020 CE: Firdowsi — poet (Homer of the East — Author of the Shahnameh) in Tus, Khorasan (Iran).
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Ibn Sina completes the Canon of Medicine
description: Ibn Sina (Avicenna) completes al-Qanun fi al-Tibb, a million-word medical encyclopedia that will remain the standard textbook in both Islamic and European universities until the 17th century.

figures.name: Ibn Sina
figures.role: author; wrote the most influential medical text in history

outcome: Standard medical textbook worldwide for 600 years; translated into Latin, Hebrew, and Chinese

title: Deaths in 1021 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1021 CE: Al-Sulami of Nishapur — scholar (Imam al-Tasawwuf — Master of the Sufi Sciences) in Nishapur, Khorasan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Disappearance of Caliph al-Hakim — Druze faith emerges
description: Fatimid Caliph al-Hakim vanishes during a nighttime walk in the Muqattam hills; his followers believe he went into occultation, giving rise to the Druze faith.
outcome: Created the Druze religion; one of the most mysterious events in Islamic history

title: Death of Ibn al-Haytham (Alhazen)
description: The great Arab mathematician and physicist Ibn al-Haytham died in Cairo after revolutionizing optics and the scientific method. His Kitab al-Manazir (Book of Optics) transformed understanding of light, vision, and experimentation.
outcome: His experimental approach to optics influenced European science for centuries and established foundations for the scientific method.

title: Mahmud of Ghazni campaigns in Kashmir
description: Sultan Mahmud led a military expedition into Kashmir, one of his furthest campaigns into the Indian subcontinent. Though he achieved limited territorial gains, the campaign demonstrated Ghaznavid military reach.
outcome: The campaign extended Ghaznavid influence to the Himalayan foothills but did not result in permanent conquest.

title: Mahmud of Ghazni campaigns in Punjab
description: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni led another major military expedition into Punjab, consolidating Ghaznavid control over the region. He established Lahore as a major administrative center.
outcome: Punjab became permanently integrated into the Islamic world under Ghaznavid rule.

title: Deaths in 1022 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1022 CE: Al-Mufid — scholar (Shaykh al-Mufid — Architect of Shia Kalam) in Baghdad, Iraq.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1023 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1023 CE: Al-Tawhidi — philosopher (Philosopher of the Litterateurs — Most Difficult of Writers) in Baghdad / Shiraz; Abū Hayyān al-Tawhīdī — philosopher (Philosopher of Iraq) in Iraq.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Deaths in 1025 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1025 CE: Abd al-Jabbar ibn Ahmad — philosopher (Philosopher of Hamadan) in Hamadan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Mahmud of Ghazni destroys Somnath temple
description: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni raided and destroyed the wealthy Somnath temple in Gujarat, one of the richest Hindu shrines. The raid became legendary for its scale of plunder and religious significance on both sides.
outcome: Became a defining symbol of Muslim-Hindu conflict in later historiography and demonstrated the reach of Ghaznavid power into western India.

title: Death of Baha al-Dawla
description: Baha al-Dawla, the Buyid amir who ruled Fars, Kerman, and Iraq, died after a reign of over three decades. Under his rule, the Buyid dynasty reached its cultural zenith, patronizing scholars and maintaining nominal Abbasid authority while exercising real power in Baghdad.
outcome: His death began the gradual decline of Buyid power that would culminate in Seljuk conquest.

title: Deaths in 1029 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1029 CE: Ibn Hindu — scientist (Philosopher of Qom) in Qom; Kushyar Gilani — scientist (Persian mathematician and astronomer who influenced al-Biruni) in Gilan.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Deaths in 1030 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1030 CE: Mahmud of Ghazni — ruler (Sultan al-Ghazi — The Warrior Sultan) in Ghazni, Afghanistan; Miskawayh — philosopher (Father of Islamic Ethics) in Isfahan / Baghdad.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Al-Biruni writes Kitab al-Hind
description: Abu Rayhan al-Biruni completes his masterwork on Indian civilisation — the first objective scholarly study of another culture, covering religion, philosophy, science, and social customs.

figures.name: Al-Biruni
figures.role: author; wrote the first objective cross-cultural study in history

outcome: Founded comparative cultural studies; remains invaluable for understanding medieval India

title: Death of Mahmud of Ghazni
description: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni died after three decades of rule that transformed Ghazni into a major power. His seventeen expeditions into India and patronage of Persian culture defined an era.
outcome: His death led to succession struggles and the eventual decline of Ghaznavid power in favor of rising Seljuk Turks.

title: Fall of the Umayyad Caliphate of Córdoba
description: The Umayyad Caliphate of Córdoba collapsed into competing taifa kingdoms after decades of civil war. The last caliph Hisham III was deposed, ending three centuries of Umayyad rule in al-Andalus.
outcome: Fragmentation into taifa kingdoms weakened al-Andalus militarily and culturally, facilitating Christian Reconquista advances.

title: Death of Caliph Hisham III — end of Córdoba Caliphate
description: The deposition and death of Hisham III marked the formal end of the Umayyad Caliphate of Córdoba. Power fragmented among the Taifa kingdoms, independent Muslim city-states across al-Andalus.
outcome: The Taifa period began, characterized by political fragmentation but cultural flourishing in al-Andalus.

title: Taifa kingdoms emerge in Al-Andalus
description: Following the dissolution of the Córdoba Caliphate, independent taifa kingdoms emerged across Iberia including Seville, Granada, Toledo, and Zaragoza. These city-states were ruled by various Arab, Berber, and Slavic dynasties.
outcome: The fragmentation weakened Muslim Iberia militarily but created a period of remarkable cultural flowering.

title: Fall of Umayyad Caliphate of Córdoba
description: Caliph Hisham III was deposed, ending the Umayyad Caliphate of Córdoba after nearly three centuries of rule. The collapse led to the fragmentation of Al-Andalus into numerous taifa (party) kingdoms, weakening Muslim Iberia against Christian expansion.
outcome: The fragmentation into taifa kingdoms left Al-Andalus vulnerable to Christian reconquest and eventual Almoravid intervention.

title: Deaths in 1033 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1033 CE: Abu al-Hasan Kharaqani — Sufi mystic (Sultan al-Arifin of Khorasan — Bridge of the Naqshbandi Chain) in Kharaqan, Khorasan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1036 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1036 CE: Abu Nasr Mansur — scientist (Persian mathematician and collaborator of al-Biruni) in Khwarazm.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1037 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1037 CE: Baba Kuhi of Shiraz — poet (Patron Saint of Shiraz) in Shiraz, Iran; Ibn Sina — philosopher (al-Shaykh al-Ra'is — Prince of Physicians) in Bukhara / Hamadan / Isfahan; Farrokhi Sistani — poet (Court poet of the Ghaznavids and master of the qasida) in Ghazni; Abu Mansur al-Baghdadi — scientist (Arab mathematician and theologian who contributed to number theory) in Baghdad.
outcome: 4 notable figures died this year.

title: Death of Ibn Sina
description: Ibn Sina dies in Hamadan aged 57; his Canon of Medicine and Book of Healing will dominate Islamic and European science for centuries.
outcome: Left the most influential medical and philosophical works in Islamic history

title: Seljuks defeat Ghaznavids at Dandanaqan
description: The Seljuk Turks under Tughril Beg decisively defeated the Ghaznavid Sultan Mas'ud I at the Battle of Dandanaqan in Khorasan. This victory established Seljuk dominance in eastern Iran and Central Asia.
outcome: Shifted power in the eastern Islamic world from Ghaznavids to Seljuks and forced Ghaznavids to focus exclusively on India.

title: Al-Mu'izz ibn Badis breaks with Fatimids
description: The Zirid ruler al-Mu'izz ibn Badis of Ifriqiya renounced allegiance to the Fatimid Caliphate and recognized the Abbasid Caliph instead. This act of political defiance had major consequences for North Africa.
outcome: The Fatimids responded by sending the Banu Hilal tribes to invade Ifriqiya, devastating the region.

title: Deaths in 1038 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1038 CE: Abu Nu'aym al-Isfahani — scholar (Author of Hilyat al-Awliya, major biographical dictionary of saints) in Isfahan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.
