title: Deaths in 1040 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1040 CE: Ibn al-Haytham — scientist (Father of Optics — Founder of the Scientific Method) in Basra / Cairo.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1044 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1044 CE: Sharif al-Murtada — scholar (Major Shia theologian and literary scholar of Baghdad) in Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi arrives in Baghdad
description: The great hadith scholar al-Khatib al-Baghdadi settled permanently in Baghdad after travels across the Islamic world. He began compiling his monumental Tarikh Baghdad (History of Baghdad), documenting thousands of scholars.
outcome: His biographical dictionary became the model for Islamic biographical literature and preserved knowledge of Baghdad's scholarly tradition.

title: Ibn al-Athir al-Baghdadi writes astronomical treatise
description: The astronomer Ibn al-Athir al-Baghdadi composed his major work on astronomical instruments and calculations in Baghdad. His work synthesized Greek and Islamic astronomical traditions.
outcome: His work contributed to the transmission of astronomical knowledge to later medieval scholars.

title: Death of Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi
description: The renowned hadith scholar al-Khatib al-Baghdadi died in Baghdad after compiling Tarikh Baghdad, a monumental biographical dictionary. His work documented thousands of scholars associated with Baghdad.
outcome: His methodology for hadith criticism became foundational for Islamic biographical literature.

title: Deaths in 1048 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1048 CE: Al-Biruni — scientist (Father of Comparative Anthropology and Geology) in Khwarezm / Ghazni.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Death of Al-Biruni
description: Al-Biruni dies after a lifetime of groundbreaking work in astronomy, mathematics, geography, mineralogy, and comparative religion.

figures.name: Al-Biruni
figures.role: died; pioneering polymath and cross-cultural scholar

outcome: Left over 140 works spanning every scientific discipline

title: Al-Bakri begins writing on West African trade
description: The Andalusian geographer al-Bakri began compiling his Book of Roads and Kingdoms, documenting trans-Saharan trade routes and African kingdoms. Though writing from Córdoba, his accounts of Ghana and other states became foundational sources.
outcome: Provided crucial documentation of pre-Islamic and Islamic African kingdoms and their gold trade networks.

title: Nasir Khusraw begins his journey
description: Persian poet and Ismaili philosopher Nasir Khusraw embarked on his famous seven-year journey through the Islamic world. He traveled through Iran, Arabia, Egypt, and Syria, documenting his observations in his Safarnama.
outcome: His travelogue became a crucial primary source for 11th-century Islamic civilization and Fatimid Egypt.

title: Al-Zarqali begins astronomical observations in Toledo
description: The astronomer Abu Ishaq al-Zarqali began his systematic astronomical observations in Toledo, later producing the Toledan Tables. His instruments included the improved astrolabe known as the safiha.
sources: Samsó, Islamic Astronomy and Medieval Spain
outcome: His astronomical tables influenced European astronomy for centuries after being translated into Latin.

title: Nasir Khusraw begins his journey
description: Nasir Khusraw, Persian poet, philosopher and Ismaili missionary, began his famous seven-year journey through the Islamic world after a spiritual crisis. His travels took him from his native Khurasan through Iran, Arabia, Syria, Palestine, and Egypt, which he documented in his 'Safarnama' (Book of Travels).
outcome: His travelogue became one of the most important firsthand accounts of 11th-century Islamic civilization and Fatimid Egypt.

title: Deaths in 1049 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1049 CE: Abu Sa'id Abu al-Khayr — poet (Pir-i Khorasan — First Sufi Quatrain Poet) in Mayhana, Khorasan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Almoravid movement founded by Abdallah ibn Yasin
description: The Berber scholar Abdallah ibn Yasin founded the Almoravid (al-Murabitun) reform movement among Sanhaja tribes in the Sahara. The movement combined strict Maliki jurisprudence with military expansion.
outcome: Launched a movement that would conquer Morocco and al-Andalus, reunifying western Islamic territories under puritanical reform.

title: Death of Ibn Hazm of Córdoba
description: The Andalusian polymath Ibn Hazm died near Seville after a life of political turbulence and prolific scholarship. He authored over 400 works including the Zahiri legal text al-Muhalla and the literary masterpiece Tawq al-Hamama.
outcome: Ibn Hazm became the most prominent representative of the Zahiri school of Islamic jurisprudence.

title: Deaths in 1054 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1054 CE: Fakhruddin Gurgani — poet (Author of Vis and Ramin, earliest Persian romance epic) in Gorgan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Seljuk Turks capture Baghdad
description: Tughril Beg's Seljuk Turks capture Baghdad, replacing the Shia Buyids as protectors of the Sunni Abbasid Caliph and establishing Sunni Turkish dominance across the eastern Islamic world.
outcome: Began the era of Turkish Sunni dominance; Seljuks became the most powerful Muslim dynasty

title: Death of Al-Mawardi
description: The jurist and political theorist al-Mawardi died in Baghdad after completing his influential works on Islamic governance. His writings systematized the relationship between caliphs and sultans.
outcome: His political theory legitimized the Seljuk-Abbasid power-sharing arrangement.

title: Almoravids conquer Awdaghust
description: Almoravid forces under Yahya ibn Umar captured Awdaghust, a major trading city controlling trans-Saharan gold routes. The conquest gave the Almoravids control over West African trade networks.
outcome: Established Almoravid economic power and disrupted the Ghana Empire's control of Saharan trade.

title: Abdallah ibn Yasin killed in battle
description: Abdallah ibn Yasin, founder of the Almoravid movement, was killed fighting the Barghawata Berbers in Morocco. His death came as the Almoravids were consolidating power in the western Maghreb.
outcome: Leadership passed to Yusuf ibn Tashfin, who transformed the Almoravids into an empire.

title: Death of Ibn Gabirol
description: The Jewish poet and philosopher Solomon ibn Gabirol died in Valencia after composing his philosophical work Fons Vitae and numerous Hebrew poems. He wrote in the Andalusian Arabic philosophical tradition.
outcome: His neoplatonic philosophy influenced both Jewish and Christian medieval thought.

title: Deaths in 1058 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1058 CE: Al-Mawardi — scholar (Greatest Political Theorist of Medieval Islam) in Basra / Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Mawardi writes The Ordinances of Government
description: Al-Mawardi completes al-Ahkam al-Sultaniyyah, the foundational text of Islamic constitutional law defining the rights and duties of the Caliph.

figures.name: Al-Mawardi
figures.role: author; wrote the foundational text of Islamic constitutional law

outcome: Defined Islamic political theory for all subsequent centuries

title: Al-Basasiri seizes Baghdad for Fatimids
description: The Turkish general al-Basasiri captured Baghdad and proclaimed the Fatimid Caliph al-Mustansir, deposing the Abbasid Caliph al-Qa'im. The Seljuks under Tughril Beg retook the city within a year.
outcome: The brief Fatimid control of Baghdad marked the high point of Fatimid political ambitions in Iraq.

title: Zirid ruler Al-Mu'izz renounces Fatimids
description: Al-Mu'izz ibn Badis of the Zirid Dynasty in Ifriqiya formally broke with the Fatimid Caliphate, shifting allegiance to the Abbasids. He ordered Fatimid symbols removed and Abbasid khutba recited.
outcome: The Fatimids retaliated by sending Banu Hilal tribes into North Africa, devastating the region.

title: Normans capture Messina from Muslims
description: Norman forces under Roger de Hauteville captured Messina, beginning the final phase of Norman conquest of Sicily. The campaign gradually displaced Muslim rule that had lasted over two centuries.
sources: Geoffrey Malaterra, De Rebus Gestis Rogerii
outcome: The conquest marked the beginning of Muslim Sicily's end as an Islamic polity.

title: Normans capture Messina from Muslims
description: Roger I of Hauteville and his Norman forces captured the strategic Sicilian city of Messina from its Muslim rulers, marking a major step in the Norman conquest of Sicily. The fall of Messina opened the way for systematic Norman conquest of the island from the Kalbid dynasty.
sources: Geoffrey Malaterra, De Rebus Gestis Rogerii
outcome: The conquest began the transformation of Sicily from a Muslim to a Christian polity, though Islamic culture persisted for decades.

title: Yusuf ibn Tashfin founds Marrakesh
description: The Almoravid leader Yusuf ibn Tashfin established Marrakesh as the new Almoravid capital in Morocco. The city became a major center linking sub-Saharan Africa with the Mediterranean world.
outcome: Marrakesh became the political and commercial heart of the Almoravid Empire and remains a major Moroccan city.

title: Al-Qushayri completes al-Risala al-Qushayriyya
description: The Sufi master Abu al-Qasim al-Qushayri completed his influential treatise on Sufism in Nishapur. The Risala systematized Sufi terminology, biography, and practice, becoming a foundational text of Islamic mysticism.
outcome: The treatise became one of the most widely studied texts of Sufi theory and practice.

title: Deaths in 1064 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1064 CE: Ibn Hazm — scholar (al-Andalusi — Polymath of Córdoba) in Córdoba / Jativa, al-Andalus.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Nizam al-Mulk founds the Nizamiyya madrasas
description: The Seljuk vizier Nizam al-Mulk establishes the Nizamiyya network of madrasas across the Islamic world, with the Baghdad Nizamiyya becoming the most prestigious university of its era.

figures.name: Nizam al-Mulk
figures.role: founder; created the madrasa system that educated the Islamic world

outcome: Created the institutional model of Islamic higher education that spread globally

title: Seljuks conquer Armenia and Georgia
description: Seljuk forces under Alp Arslan invaded the Armenian and Georgian kingdoms, devastating Ani and other cities. These campaigns opened Anatolia to Turkic migration and Islamic expansion.
outcome: Weakened Christian states in the Caucasus and set stage for Turkish settlement of Anatolia after Manzikert.

title: Death of Ibn Butlan in Antioch
description: The Christian Arab physician Ibn Butlan died in Antioch after a career spanning Baghdad, Cairo, and Constantinople. His medical handbook Taqwim al-Sihha became widely used across the Islamic world and medieval Europe.
outcome: His work on dietetics and health maintenance influenced medical practice for centuries.

title: Death of Ibn Hazm
description: The Zahiri scholar Ibn Hazm of Córdoba died in Huelva after producing over 400 works including al-Muhalla and Tawq al-Hamama. His literalist legal methodology challenged Maliki orthodoxy in Iberia.
outcome: His comparative religious studies and legal theory influenced Islamic thought despite his controversial views.

title: Al-Qushayri completes al-Risala al-Qushayriyya
description: Abu'l-Qasim al-Qushayri completed his influential 'al-Risala al-Qushayriyya' (Epistle on Sufism) in Nishapur, systematically presenting Sufi doctrine and practice for the first time. The work reconciled Sufism with orthodox Sunni theology, defending mysticism against its critics.
outcome: The Risala became one of the foundational texts of Sunni Sufism and remains widely studied today.

title: Al-Bakri completes Book of Roads and Kingdoms
description: The Andalusian geographer Abu Ubayd al-Bakri completed his comprehensive geography of the known world in Córdoba. Though he never traveled to West Africa, his descriptions of Ghana and trans-Saharan trade routes became invaluable historical sources.
outcome: His work preserved crucial information about 11th-century West African kingdoms and trade networks.

title: Deaths in 1071 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1071 CE: Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi — scholar (Greatest historian of Baghdad and pioneer of hadith methodology) in Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Battle of Manzikert — Seljuks defeat Byzantium
description: Sultan Alp Arslan's Seljuk army captures Byzantine Emperor Romanos IV at Manzikert, opening Anatolia to Turkish settlement and beginning the transformation of Asia Minor into Turkey.
outcome: Opened Anatolia to Turkish settlement; led directly to the Ottoman Empire

title: Deaths in 1072 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1072 CE: Al-Qushayri — scholar (Defender of Orthodox Sufism) in Nishapur, Iran; Ali al-Hujwiri — scholar (Data Ganj Bakhsh — Giver of Spiritual Treasures) in Ghazni / Lahore; Alp Arslan — ruler (Great Lion — Victor of Manzikert) in Marv / Baghdad.
outcome: 3 notable figures died this year.

title: Death of Sultan Alp Arslan
description: The Seljuk Sultan Alp Arslan, victor of Manzikert, was assassinated while campaigning in Transoxiana. His son Malik Shah succeeded him under the guidance of vizier Nizam al-Mulk.
outcome: His death transferred power to Malik Shah during the Seljuk Empire's zenith.

title: Al-Juwayni completes Ghiyath al-Umam
description: The Shafi'i theologian Imam al-Haramayn al-Juwayni completed his political treatise on governance and the imamate in Nishapur. The work addressed the crisis of legitimate authority during Seljuk domination of the Abbasid Caliphate.
outcome: His political theory influenced later Islamic discussions of legitimate governance and state authority.

title: Almoravids conquer Ghana Empire
description: Almoravid forces under Abu Bakr ibn Umar captured Kumbi Saleh, capital of the Ghana Empire. The conquest disrupted traditional West African power structures and accelerated Islamization of the Sahel.
outcome: Marked the decline of Ghana's hegemony and facilitated the rise of Islamic successor states in West Africa.

title: Seljuks conquer Damascus
description: The Seljuk Turks under Atsiz ibn Uvaq captured Damascus from the Fatimids, ending nearly a century of Fatimid rule in southern Syria. This extended Seljuk control over most of Syria and Palestine.
outcome: Damascus became a major Seljuk city and remained under Sunni rule thereafter.

title: Seljuks capture Jerusalem from Fatimids
description: The Seljuk general Atsiz ibn Uvak conquered Jerusalem from Fatimid control. The city changed hands multiple times in subsequent years amid Seljuk-Fatimid rivalry.
outcome: Destabilized Levantine politics and disrupted Christian pilgrimage, contributing to factors that sparked the First Crusade.

title: Seljuks capture Nicaea from Byzantines
description: Following their victory at Manzikert, the Seljuk Turks under Süleyman ibn Qutalmish captured the Byzantine city of Nicaea in northwestern Anatolia. Süleyman established it as the capital of the Sultanate of Rum.
outcome: Nicaea became the first capital of the Seljuk Sultanate of Rum in Anatolia.

title: Seljuks capture Mecca and Medina
description: Seljuk forces brought the Hejaz including Mecca and Medina under their authority, ending Fatimid influence over the holy cities. The khutba was changed from Fatimid to Abbasid names.
outcome: Seljuk control of the holy cities enhanced Sunni prestige and Abbasid legitimacy.

title: Al-Juwayni completes Ghiyath al-Umam
description: Imam al-Haramayn al-Juwayni completed his work 'Ghiyath al-Umam fi'l-Tiyath al-Zulam' (The Succor of Nations in the Engulfing Darkness) on Islamic political theory and jurisprudence. The treatise addressed the legitimacy of the caliphate and governance during times of political fragmentation under Seljuk rule.
outcome: His political theology influenced later Islamic constitutional thought and his student al-Ghazali's political ideas.

title: Deaths in 1078 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1078 CE: Mu'ayyad fi'l-Din al-Shirazi — scholar (Islamic Philosophy Scholar of Shiraz) in Shiraz.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Malik Shah establishes Jalali calendar
description: Seljuk Sultan Malik Shah commissioned Omar Khayyam and other astronomers to reform the Persian calendar. The resulting Jalali calendar was more accurate than the Gregorian calendar introduced five centuries later.
outcome: Demonstrated Islamic astronomical sophistication and provided an accurate solar calendar still used in modified form in Iran.

title: Death of Al-Juwayni
description: The influential Ash'ari theologian Imam al-Haramayn al-Juwayni died in Nishapur. His students included al-Ghazali, and his synthesis of theology and jurisprudence shaped Sunni orthodoxy.
outcome: His theological method influenced the development of later Ash'ari thought through his famous student al-Ghazali.

title: Death of Peter Damian
description: Cardinal Peter Damian, who had corresponded with Muslim scholars and advocated understanding of Islamic philosophy, died in Faenza. His writings showed knowledge of Islamic thought transmitted through Spain.
outcome: His work exemplified early intellectual engagement between Christian and Islamic scholars.

title: Al-Mu'tamid of Seville requests Almoravid aid
description: The Taifa king al-Mu'tamid ibn Abbad of Seville appealed to Yusuf ibn Tashfin for military assistance against Alfonso VI of Castile. This invitation ultimately led to Almoravid conquest of al-Andalus.
outcome: This fateful decision saved al-Andalus from Christian conquest but ended the independence of the Taifa kingdoms.

title: Seljuks capture Antioch
description: Seljuk forces under Sulayman ibn Qutalmish captured Antioch from the Byzantines, establishing the Sultanate of Rum. The city became a major Seljuk stronghold in Anatolia.
outcome: The conquest extended Seljuk power to the Mediterranean and threatened Byzantine Anatolia.

title: Al-Zarqali constructs Toledan Tables
description: Abu Ishaq Ibrahim al-Zarqali (Azarquiel) completed the Toledan Tables in Toledo, providing unprecedented astronomical data based on observations made with improved astrolabes of his own design. These tables corrected Ptolemaic errors and provided accurate calculations for planetary positions.
outcome: The Toledan Tables became the basis for the Alfonsine Tables and profoundly influenced European astronomy for centuries.

title: Deaths in 1085 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1085 CE: Imam al-Juwayni — scholar (Imam al-Haramayn — Teacher of Al-Ghazali) in Nishapur / Mecca / Medina.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Toledo falls to Christian Reconquista
description: Alfonso VI of Castile captures Toledo, the former Visigothic capital, beginning the major phase of the Christian Reconquista and opening Arabic libraries to European scholars.
outcome: Toledo's Arabic libraries sparked the Latin translation movement that fuelled the European Renaissance

title: Christians capture Toledo
description: Alfonso VI of León-Castile captured Toledo, the former Visigothic capital, from its Muslim ruler al-Qadir. The fall of this major city shocked the Taifa kingdoms and accelerated calls for Almoravid intervention.
outcome: Toledo's fall marked a turning point in the Reconquista and prompted Almoravid intervention in Iberia.

title: Battle of Sagrajas (Zallaqa)
description: Yusuf ibn Tashfin led Almoravid forces from Morocco to al-Andalus, defeating Alfonso VI of Castile at Sagrajas near Badajoz. The victory halted Christian advances and reunified Muslim Spain under Almoravid control.
outcome: Temporarily reversed Christian Reconquista momentum and established Almoravid dominance over Iberian taifa kingdoms.

title: Qadi Iyad born in Ceuta
description: The future hadith scholar and Maliki jurist Qadi Iyad was born in Ceuta, later becoming famous for his work al-Shifa on the Prophet's virtues. He would become a leading authority in Islamic jurisprudence.
outcome: His scholarship bridged Andalusian and Maghrebi Islamic learning traditions.

title: Deaths in 1087 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1087 CE: Al-Zarqali — scientist (Arzachel — Most Influential Astronomer of the 11th Century) in Toledo / Cordoba, Spain.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1088 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1088 CE: Nasir Khusraw — philosopher (Hujjat al-Khorasan — Ismaili Philosopher-Poet) in Khorasan / Cairo / Badakhshan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1089 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1089 CE: Khwaja Abdullah Ansari — Sufi mystic (Pir-i Herat — Elder of Herat) in Herat, Afghanistan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Completion of Friday Mosque of Isfahan
description: The Seljuks completed major renovations to the Friday Mosque of Isfahan under Nizam al-Mulk and Malik Shah, including innovative four-iwan design. The mosque became a model for Persian Islamic architecture.
sources: Blair and Bloom, The Art and Architecture of Islam
outcome: The architectural innovations influenced mosque design throughout the Islamic world.

title: Deaths in 1090 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1090 CE: Al-Sarakhsi — scholar (Sun of the Imams — Greatest Hanafi Jurist) in Sarakhs / Fergana, Central Asia.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Ghazali begins his spiritual crisis
description: Abu Hamid al-Ghazali, the most famous professor at Baghdad's Nizamiyya, suffers a spiritual crisis, abandons his prestigious career, and embarks on a decade of wandering and Sufi practice.

figures.name: Al-Ghazali
figures.role: abandoned his career at its peak to seek spiritual truth

outcome: His crisis and return produced the Ihya — the most influential work in Islamic history after the Quran

title: Hassan-i Sabbah captures Alamut Castle
description: The Ismaili missionary Hassan-i Sabbah seized Alamut fortress in the Elburz Mountains of northern Iran. The fortress became headquarters of the Nizari Ismaili state and the legendary Assassins.
outcome: Established a powerful Ismaili state that challenged Seljuk authority and influenced regional politics for over 150 years.

title: Almoravids depose al-Mu'tamid of Seville
description: Yusuf ibn Tashfin deposed al-Mu'tamid ibn Abbad, the poet-king of Seville who had invited him to Spain. Al-Mu'tamid was exiled to Aghmat in Morocco, where he died in captivity.
outcome: The Almoravids annexed the remaining Taifa kingdoms, ending their political independence.

title: Completion of Friday Mosque of Isfahan
description: The Great Seljuk reconstruction of the Friday Mosque (Masjid-i Jami) of Isfahan was completed under the patronage of vizier Nizam al-Mulk and Sultan Malik Shah. The mosque featured revolutionary four-iwan plan and monumental brick domes that established Persian architectural principles for centuries.
outcome: The mosque's four-iwan design became the standard template for Iranian mosques and madrasas throughout the Islamic world.

title: Deaths in 1091 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1091 CE: Wallada bint al-Mustakfi — poet (Andalusian princess-poet and literary salon hostess) in Cordoba.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Normans complete conquest of Sicily from Muslims
description: Roger I of Sicily captured Noto, the last Muslim stronghold on the island, completing the Norman conquest of Sicily. Two centuries of Islamic rule ended, though Muslim communities remained under Norman protection.
sources: Geoffrey Malaterra, De Rebus Gestis Rogerii
outcome: Ended Muslim political control of Sicily but preserved Islamic cultural and scientific influences under tolerant Norman rule.

title: Normans capture Noto — complete conquest of Sicily
description: Roger I of Sicily captured Noto, the last Muslim stronghold on the island, completing the Norman conquest begun decades earlier. This ended over two centuries of Islamic rule in Sicily.
sources: Geoffrey Malaterra, De rebus gestis Rogerii
outcome: Norman Sicily developed a unique multicultural society blending Latin, Greek, and Arabic influences.

title: Normans capture Malta from Arabs
description: Roger I of Sicily captured Malta from its Muslim rulers, completing Norman control of the central Mediterranean. The island had been under Islamic rule since 870.
sources: Geoffrey Malaterra, De Rebus Gestis Rogerii
outcome: Malta's conquest ended two centuries of Arab-Islamic rule in the central Mediterranean.

title: Deaths in 1092 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1092 CE: Nizam al-Mulk — ruler (Khwaja Buzurg — The Great Vizier) in Tus / Baghdad, Iran.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Assassination of Nizam al-Mulk
description: The great Seljuk vizier Nizam al-Mulk was assassinated by an Ismaili Assassin near Sihna. His death shortly before Sultan Malik Shah's demise precipitated succession crises and Seljuk fragmentation.
outcome: Removed the administrative genius holding the Seljuk Empire together, leading to decades of political instability.

title: Death of Malik Shah
description: Seljuk Sultan Malik Shah died in Baghdad shortly after the assassination of his vizier Nizam al-Mulk. His death triggered succession struggles that fragmented Seljuk unity.
outcome: The Seljuk Empire fragmented into competing regional dynasties after his death.

title: Deaths in 1094 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1094 CE: Al-Bakri — scholar (Greatest Andalusian Geographer) in Cordoba / Seville, Spain.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Mustansir Billah dies — Nizari-Musta'li schism
description: The death of Fatimid Caliph al-Mustansir triggered a succession dispute between his sons Nizar and al-Musta'li. The conflict split Ismaili Islam into rival Nizari and Musta'li branches that persist today.
outcome: Created permanent division in Ismaili Islam between Nizaris (including Assassins) and Musta'lis (including Bohras).

title: El Cid captures Valencia
description: The Castilian warrior Rodrigo Díaz de Vivar, known as El Cid, captured Valencia after a lengthy siege. He ruled the city independently from both Christian and Muslim powers until his death.
outcome: El Cid's conquest of Valencia became legendary in both Christian and Muslim sources.

title: Al-Ghazali appointed to Nizamiyya Baghdad
description: Abu Hamid al-Ghazali was appointed professor at the prestigious Nizamiyya madrasa in Baghdad at age 34. His lectures attracted hundreds of students before his spiritual crisis.
outcome: His appointment marked the height of his scholarly career before his spiritual transformation.

title: Pope Urban II calls the First Crusade
description: Pope Urban II calls Christian Europe to arms at the Council of Clermont, launching the First Crusade to recapture Jerusalem from Muslim control.
outcome: Began two centuries of Crusader warfare that reshaped the entire Mediterranean world

title: Al-Ghazali completes The Incoherence of the Philosophers
description: The theologian al-Ghazali finished Tahafut al-Falasifa while teaching at the Nizamiyya madrasa in Baghdad. The work critiqued Aristotelian philosophy and challenged the reconciliation of reason and revelation.
outcome: Shifted Islamic intellectual discourse toward greater skepticism of Greek philosophy and elevation of mystical knowledge.

title: Al-Ghazali leaves Baghdad for Damascus
description: After his spiritual crisis and resignation from the Nizamiyya madrasa, al-Ghazali left Baghdad and began his wandering ascetic period. He spent time in Damascus, Jerusalem, and Mecca before returning to Tus.
outcome: This period of spiritual retreat profoundly influenced al-Ghazali's integration of Sufism into mainstream Sunni thought.

title: Death of Al-Mu'tamid of Seville in exile
description: Al-Mu'tamid ibn Abbad, the poet-king of Seville deposed by the Almoravids, died in exile in Aghmat, Morocco. His poetry mourning his lost kingdom became famous in Arabic literature.
sources: Ibn Bassam, al-Dhakhira fi Mahasin Ahl al-Jazira
outcome: His tragic end symbolized the demise of the taifa kingdoms' cultural golden age.

title: Crusaders capture Jerusalem
description: The First Crusade captures Jerusalem after a bloody siege, massacring the Muslim and Jewish inhabitants and establishing the Crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem.
outcome: Shocked the Muslim world; Jerusalem lost to Christendom for 88 years until Saladin

title: Fatimids briefly retake Jerusalem before Crusader conquest
description: Fatimid forces under al-Afdal Shahanshah recaptured Jerusalem from the Seljuks weeks before the First Crusade arrived. The Fatimids lost the city to Crusaders after a brutal siege the same year.
outcome: Brief Fatimid control ended with Crusader massacre and establishment of Latin Kingdom, shocking the Muslim world.

title: Crusaders massacre Muslims in Jerusalem
description: Crusader forces massacred the Muslim and Jewish populations of Jerusalem after capturing the city, with tens of thousands killed. The al-Aqsa Mosque and Dome of the Rock were desecrated and converted.
outcome: The massacre traumatized the Islamic world and motivated subsequent jihad movements to recapture Jerusalem.

title: Deaths in 1101 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1101 CE: Mahmud al-Kashgari — scholar (Father of Turkic Linguistics — Diwan Lughat al-Turk) in Kashgar, Central Asia.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1106 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1106 CE: Yusuf ibn Tashfin — ruler (Amir al-Muslimin — Commander of the Muslims) in Marrakesh, Morocco.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Almoravid conquest of Zaragoza
description: Almoravid forces under Yusuf ibn Tashfin's successor captured Zaragoza from local Muslim rulers, consolidating North African Berber control over al-Andalus. This marked the temporary halt of Christian reconquest in the northeast Iberian Peninsula.
outcome: Extended Almoravid dominance in Iberia for another generation before Almohad rise.

title: Death of Yusuf ibn Tashfin
description: Yusuf ibn Tashfin, founder of the Almoravid dynasty and ruler of North Africa and Al-Andalus, died at an advanced age in Marrakesh. His reign unified Muslim Spain under Berber rule and temporarily halted the Christian Reconquista.
outcome: His death marked the beginning of Almoravid decline as succession struggles weakened the dynasty.

title: Deaths in 1111 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1111 CE: Al-Ghazali — scholar (Hujjat al-Islam — Proof of Islam) in Tus / Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Death of Al-Ghazali
description: Al-Ghazali dies in Tus after completing the Ihya Ulum al-Din, which reconciles Sunni orthodoxy with Sufism and reshapes Islamic thought permanently.

figures.name: Al-Ghazali
figures.role: died; author of the Ihya, the most influential Islamic book after the Quran

outcome: His Ihya reconciled orthodoxy with mysticism; shaped all subsequent Sunni Islam

title: Christians capture Zaragoza
description: Alfonso I of Aragon conquered Zaragoza after a long siege, ending over four centuries of Muslim rule in the city. This represented a major territorial loss for Muslims in northeastern al-Andalus.
outcome: Permanent Christian control of the Ebro valley and major blow to Muslim Iberia.

title: Death of Sultan Muhammad I Tapar of Great Seljuk Empire
description: Sultan Muhammad I Tapar, who ruled the Great Seljuk Empire and briefly unified much of Persia and Iraq, died in Isfahan. His death led to the fragmentation of Seljuk power among competing princes and atabegs.
outcome: The Great Seljuk Empire fragmented into regional powers controlled by atabegs.

title: Death of Sultan Muhammad I Tapar of Great Seljuk Empire
description: Sultan Muhammad I Tapar, the Great Seljuk ruler who maintained unity during a period of fragmentation, died in Isfahan. His reign saw continued patronage of scholars and attempts to maintain Seljuk authority over rival regional powers.
outcome: His death accelerated the fragmentation of the Great Seljuk Empire into competing regional sultanates.

title: Death of Sultan Ahmad Sanjar's father Malik-Shah I
description: Malik-Shah I, the powerful Seljuk Sultan who ruled from 1072-1092, had already died in 1092, but his son Ahmad Sanjar became the last great Seljuk ruler, consolidating power over Khorasan in 1118. Sanjar's assumption of supreme Seljuk authority marked the beginning of the final phase of unified Seljuk power before fragmentation.
outcome: Sanjar's reign represented the last period of Seljuk centralized authority before the dynasty's collapse.

title: Battle of Didgori
description: Georgian King David IV decisively defeated a much larger Seljuk army under Ilghazi, securing Georgian independence. The battle ended Seljuk attempts to dominate the Caucasus region.
outcome: Established Georgian power and restricted Seljuk expansion northward.

title: Death of Al-Hariri — author of Maqamat
description: Abu Muhammad al-Qasim al-Hariri, author of the Maqamat al-Hariri, died in Basra. His collection of fifty picaresque tales featuring the eloquent vagabond Abu Zayd became one of the most celebrated works of Arabic literature.
outcome: The Maqamat became a cornerstone of Arabic literary education and inspired centuries of illuminated manuscripts.

title: Al-Turtushi writes Siraj al-Muluk
description: Abu Bakr al-Turtushi, Maliki jurist from Tortosa, completed his major work on political governance and Islamic ethics. The work became influential in Andalusian and North African Islamic thought on statecraft.
outcome: The work influenced Islamic political theory and governance principles for centuries.

title: Deaths in 1122 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1122 CE: Al-Baghawi — scholar (Muhyi al-Sunna — Reviver of the Sunnah) in Bagh, Khorasan, Iran; Al-Hariri of Basra — poet (Author of the Maqamat, masterpiece of Arabic prose literature) in Basra.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Omar Khayyam dies — poet, mathematician, astronomer
description: Omar Khayyam dies in Nishapur; his calendar reform is more accurate than the Gregorian, his algebra advanced cubic equations, and his Rubaiyat became the most translated Persian poem.
outcome: His calendar was more accurate than the Gregorian; his poetry became world-famous through FitzGerald

title: Death of Al-Hariri — author of Maqamat
description: Abu Muhammad al-Qasim ibn Ali al-Hariri, author of the Maqamat al-Hariri, died in Basra. His work of rhymed prose became one of the most celebrated examples of Arabic literary art and was widely copied and illustrated.
outcome: The Maqamat influenced Arabic literature for centuries and became a standard text for teaching eloquent Arabic.

title: Deaths in 1123 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1123 CE: Ahmad Ghazali — Sufi mystic (Imam al-Mahabba — Imam of Love) in Tus / Qazvin.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Conquest of Tyre by Venetian-Crusader alliance
description: The important Mediterranean port city of Tyre fell to Crusader forces with Venetian naval support after a prolonged siege. The loss significantly weakened Muslim control of the Levantine coast.
outcome: The fall of Tyre gave Crusaders nearly complete control of the Eastern Mediterranean coastline.

title: Deaths in 1124 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1124 CE: Hasan-i Sabbah — ruler (Old Man of the Mountain — Founder of the Nizari Ismaili State) in Qom / Alamut, Iran.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1125 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1125 CE: Sharaf al-Zaman al-Marwazi — scientist (Persian physician and zoologist who authored the Nature of Animals) in Merv.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1127 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1127 CE: Ibn al-Sīd al-Baṭalyawsī — philosopher (Philosopher of Badajoz) in Badajoz.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Imad al-Din Zangi begins Muslim counter-crusade
description: The Turkish atabeg Imad al-Din Zangi unifies Mosul and Aleppo, beginning the organised Muslim military response to the Crusader states.
outcome: Began the Muslim counter-crusade that Nur al-Din and Saladin would complete

title: Death of Zangi's father Aq-Sunqur
description: Aq-Sunqur al-Hajib, atabeg of Aleppo and father of Imad al-Din Zangi, was assassinated in a palace intrigue. His death created instability that eventually allowed his son Zangi to rise to power.
outcome: Set stage for Zangi dynasty that would lead Muslim counter-crusade.

title: Almohad conquest of Tlemcen
description: The Almohad forces under Abd al-Mu'min captured Tlemcen from the Almoravids, extending their control over western Algeria. This victory marked a critical step in the Almohad expansion across the Maghreb.
outcome: Tlemcen became an important Almohad base for further conquests eastward.

title: Deaths in 1130 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1130 CE: Ibn Tumart — reformer (Al-Mahdi — The Almohad Prophet) in Sus, Morocco.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Almohad movement founded by Ibn Tumart
description: Muhammad ibn Tumart declared himself the Mahdi and founded the Almohad movement in the Atlas Mountains, preaching strict monotheism and reform. His successor Abd al-Mu'min would build this into an empire spanning North Africa and Iberia.
outcome: Launched religious-political movement that replaced Almoravids and reformed Maghrebi Islam.

title: Almohad conquest of Tlemcen
description: The Almohad movement under Abd al-Mu'min captured Tlemcen from Almoravid control, expanding their authority in the Maghrib. This conquest was part of the systematic Almohad campaign to replace the Almoravid dynasty.
outcome: The fall of Tlemcen accelerated Almoravid collapse and strengthened Almohad control over North Africa.

title: Death of Al-Mahdawi, Quranic scholar
description: Ahmad ibn Ammar al-Mahdawi, prominent Maliki scholar and expert in Quranic readings (qira'at), died in Valencia. He was known for his expertise in the seven canonical readings of the Quran.
outcome: His work preserved Andalusian traditions of Quranic recitation during the Reconquista period.

title: Deaths in 1131 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1131 CE: Omar Khayyam — poet (Philosopher-Poet of the Rubaiyat) in Nishapur, Khorasan, Iran; Sana'i of Ghazna — poet (Hakim al-Ghayb — Father of Sufi Masnavi Poetry) in Ghazna, Afghanistan.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Deaths in 1134 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1134 CE: Abu al-Salt — scientist (Andalusian polymath and author of works on medicine, astronomy and music) in Denia.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Construction of Qutb Minar begins
description: Qutb al-Din Aibak, first Sultan of Delhi, began construction of the Qutb Minar tower to commemorate the Muslim conquest of Delhi. The minaret remains the tallest brick tower in the world.
outcome: Iconic symbol of Islamic architecture in India and Delhi Sultanate power.

title: Death of Al-Zamakhshari
description: Mahmud ibn Umar al-Zamakhshari, the renowned Mu'tazili scholar and author of the Quranic commentary al-Kashshaf, died in Khwarazm. His tafsir became influential despite his rationalist theological positions.
outcome: His Quranic commentary influenced both Sunni and Shi'a scholarship for centuries despite theological controversies.

title: Death of Al-Zamakhshari
description: Mahmud al-Zamakhshari, Persian Mu'tazili scholar, died in Khwarazm after completing his famous Quranic commentary al-Kashshaf. Despite his Mu'tazili theology, the work became influential across all Sunni schools for its linguistic analysis.
outcome: His tafsir influenced Islamic exegesis despite theological controversies over his Mu'tazili views.

title: Deaths in 1138 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1138 CE: Ibn Bajja — philosopher (Avempace — First Great Andalusian Philosopher) in Saragossa / Seville / Fez; Arwa al-Sulayhi — ruler (Longest-reigning queen of Yemen and Ismaili patron) in Jibla.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Death of al-Zamakhshari
description: Mahmud al-Zamakhshari, Persian Mu'tazilite scholar and author of the influential Quran commentary al-Kashshaf, died in Khwarazm. His rational approach to Quranic exegesis influenced Islamic thought for centuries despite theological controversies.
outcome: His tafsir remained influential across Sunni and Shi'a scholarship despite Mu'tazilite decline.

title: Battle of Shaizar
description: Zangi of Mosul and Nur al-Din defeated Byzantine emperor John II Komnenos besieging Shaizar in northern Syria. The Byzantine withdrawal allowed Zangi to consolidate Muslim control over the region.
outcome: The Byzantine failure marked the end of serious attempts to reclaim northern Syria.

title: Deaths in 1140 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1140 CE: Yusuf al-Hamadani — Sufi mystic (Qutb al-Aqtab — Teacher of Yesevi and Ghujdawani) in Hamadan / Merv.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Death of Al-Shahrastani
description: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Karim al-Shahrastani, author of Kitab al-Milal wa al-Nihal (The Book of Religions and Philosophical Sects), died in Khurasan. His comprehensive survey of religious and philosophical systems became a foundational work in comparative religion.
outcome: His systematic study of religions influenced Islamic approaches to understanding non-Muslim belief systems.

title: Deaths in 1142 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1142 CE: Abu Hafs al-Nasafi — scholar (Central Asian Hanafi jurist and creed author) in Samarkand.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: First Latin translation of the Quran completed
description: Robert of Ketton completed the first full Latin translation of the Quran, commissioned by Peter the Venerable of Cluny in Toledo. Though deeply flawed and polemical in purpose, it marked the beginning of direct European engagement with Islamic texts.
sources: Thomas Burman, Reading the Qur'an in Latin Christendom
outcome: Despite its inaccuracies, it became the primary means by which European Christians encountered Islamic scripture for centuries.

title: Deaths in 1144 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1144 CE: Al-Zamakhshari — philosopher (Philosopher of Zamakhshar) in Zamakhshar.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Zangi recaptures Edessa from Crusaders
description: Zangi captures the Crusader County of Edessa, the first Crusader state to fall, triggering the Second Crusade.
outcome: First major Muslim victory against Crusaders; triggered the Second Crusade

title: Fall of Tripoli to Normans
description: Norman Crusaders under Raymond II of Tripoli completed the conquest of Tripoli after a long siege, establishing the Crusader County of Tripoli. The Banu Ammar dynasty that ruled the city was destroyed.
outcome: Established last of the major Crusader states in the Levant.

title: Death of Al-Shahrastani
description: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Karim al-Shahrastani, author of Kitab al-Milal wa al-Nihal (Book of Sects and Creeds), died in Khorasan. His systematic study of religious and philosophical movements remains a fundamental source for Islamic heresiography.
outcome: His work became the standard reference for understanding Islamic and non-Islamic belief systems throughout medieval Islam.

title: Almohads capture Marrakesh
description: Abd al-Mu'min ibn Ali captured Marrakesh from the Almoravids, ending their dynasty after ruling North Africa and al-Andalus for nearly a century. The Almohad Caliphate now controlled the Maghreb.
outcome: Established Almohad hegemony over western Islamic world for next century.

title: Deaths in 1148 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1148 CE: Abu Bakr ibn al-Arabi — scholar (Leading Andalusian Maliki jurist and student of al-Ghazali) in Seville.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Failure of Second Crusade at Damascus
description: Mu'in al-Din Unur successfully defended Damascus against the Second Crusade led by European kings. The failed siege strengthened Muslim resolve and damaged Crusader-Byzantine relations.
outcome: Marked turning point showing limits of Crusader power in Syria.

title: Deaths in 1149 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1149 CE: Al-Qadi Iyad — scholar (Imam al-Maghrib — Author of Al-Shifa) in Ceuta / Granada / Marrakesh.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Death of Qadi Iyad
description: Qadi Iyad ibn Musa, author of Al-Shifa bi-ta'rif huquq al-Mustafa, died in Marrakesh. His work on the Prophet Muhammad's rights and virtues became one of the most revered texts in Islamic devotional literature.
outcome: Al-Shifa became mandatory reading in Maliki circles and remains widely studied across the Muslim world.

title: Death of Qadi Iyad
description: Abu al-Fadl Iyad ibn Musa, the Maliki jurist and author of al-Shifa bi-ta'rif huquq al-Mustafa, died in Marrakesh. His work on the rights and virtues of Prophet Muhammad became one of the most revered texts in Islamic devotional literature.
outcome: Al-Shifa became mandatory reading in Maliki circles and shaped Muslim devotion to the Prophet.

title: Deaths in 1153 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1153 CE: Al-Shahrastani — philosopher (Muhammad al-Shahrastānī) in Shahristan District.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Al-Idrisi creates the most accurate world map
description: The Sicilian-Arab geographer al-Idrisi completes the Tabula Rogeriana for the Norman King Roger II of Sicily, the most accurate world map of the medieval period.

figures.name: Al-Idrisi
figures.role: cartographer; created the most accurate medieval world map

outcome: Most accurate world map for three centuries; used by European explorers

title: Nur al-Din captures Damascus
description: Nur al-Din Zangi unified Syria by taking Damascus peacefully after Mu'in al-Din's death, creating a strong Muslim state surrounding Crusader territories. This unification proved crucial for later Muslim victories.
outcome: Created unified Syrian state that became base for Saladin's later campaigns.

title: Deaths in 1155 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1155 CE: Al-Khazini — scientist (Physicist who wrote the Book of the Balance of Wisdom on mechanics and hydrostatics) in Merv.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Battle of Granica
description: Seljuk Sultan Sanjar was defeated and captured by the Oghuz Turks in Central Asia, ending Seljuk Great Sultanate's effective power. The defeat fragmented Seljuk territories into rival successor states.
outcome: Marked end of Great Seljuk Empire and rise of regional Turkish dynasties.

title: Alfonso VII of Castile dies — Reconquista stalls
description: Alfonso VII, Christian emperor of León and Castile who had pressed the Reconquista southward, died while returning from campaign. His death and the division of his kingdom among his sons temporarily slowed Christian advances in Iberia.
outcome: The division of the kingdom gave Almohad and Muslim taifa states a respite from Christian pressure.

title: Death of Ahmad Sanjar and collapse of Great Seljuk authority
description: Sultan Ahmad Sanjar died after years of captivity by Ghuzz Turkmen tribes who had defeated him in 1153. His death marked the effective end of unified Seljuk authority, leading to fragmentation into regional dynasties.
outcome: The Seljuk Empire fragmented into regional powers including the Khwarazmians, Syrian Seljuks, and Anatolian Sultanate of Rum.

title: Deaths in 1160 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1160 CE: Ibn al-Qalanisi — scholar (Damascene chronicler of the First Crusade) in Damascus.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Death of Abd al-Qadir al-Gilani
description: Abd al-Qadir al-Gilani, founder of the Qadiriyya Sufi order, died in Baghdad. His teachings and order spread across the Islamic world, becoming one of the largest and most influential Sufi brotherhoods.
outcome: Qadiriyya order became one of most widespread Sufi tariqas globally.

title: Death of Ibn Bajjah's successor Ibn Tufayl begins writing Hayy ibn Yaqzan
description: Ibn Tufayl, Andalusian polymath and court physician to the Almohad ruler, composed his famous philosophical novel Hayy ibn Yaqzan. The work explored natural theology and reason through the story of a child raised by a gazelle on an isolated island.
outcome: The philosophical novel influenced both Islamic and later European Enlightenment thought on natural religion and reason.

title: Deaths in 1162 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1162 CE: Ibn Zuhr — scientist (Avenzoar — Greatest Clinical Physician of Islamic Spain) in Seville, Spain.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Death of Ibn Zuhr (Avenzoar)
description: Abu Marwan Abd al-Malik ibn Zuhr, known in Latin as Avenzoar, died in Seville. The distinguished physician pioneered experimental surgery and wrote al-Taysir, which influenced medieval European medicine.
outcome: His medical works, translated into Latin, shaped European medical practice for centuries.

title: Death of Usama ibn Munqidh
description: Usama ibn Munqidh, Arab knight, poet, and author of Kitab al-I'tibar (Book of Learning by Example), died in Damascus. His memoirs provide invaluable firsthand accounts of Crusader-Muslim interactions and 12th-century Levantine life.
outcome: His writings remain a primary source for understanding the cultural and military dynamics of the Crusader period.

title: Deaths in 1165 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1165 CE: Al-Idrisi — scholar (al-Sharif al-Idrisi — World's Greatest Cartographer) in Ceuta / Palermo, Sicily; Abu'l-Barakat al-Baghdadi — scientist (Philosopher and physicist who challenged Aristotelian mechanics) in Baghdad.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Death of Abd al-Mu'min
description: Abd al-Mu'min ibn Ali, founder of the Almohad Caliphate and conqueror of North Africa and al-Andalus, died after ruling for 33 years. His son Abu Ya'qub Yusuf succeeded him, continuing Almohad expansion.
outcome: Established stable dynastic succession for Almohad Caliphate.

title: Death of Abd al-Qadir al-Gilani
description: Abd al-Qadir al-Gilani, the Hanbali scholar and Sufi master who founded the Qadiriyya order, died in Baghdad. His teachings spread throughout the Islamic world and the Qadiriyya became one of the most widespread Sufi orders.
outcome: The Qadiriyya order spread from West Africa to Southeast Asia becoming one of Islam's major Sufi brotherhoods.

title: Death of Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani
description: Abd al-Qadir al-Jilani, founder of the Qadiriyya Sufi order, died in Baghdad after decades of teaching and spiritual guidance. His order became one of the most widespread Sufi tariqas, spreading from North Africa to Southeast Asia.
outcome: The Qadiriyya order became one of Islam's most geographically extensive Sufi networks.

title: Deaths in 1166 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1166 CE: Abdul Qadir al-Jilani — founder (Ghawth al-A'zam — The Greatest Intercessor) in Gilan / Baghdad; Ahmed Yesevi — founder (Pir-i Turkistan — Father of Turkish Sufism) in Sayram / Turkestan.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Death of Abd al-Mu'min
description: Abd al-Mu'min, first Almohad caliph and successor to Ibn Tumart, died after consolidating Almohad control over North Africa and Al-Andalus. His reign transformed the Almohad movement from a religious reform into a major empire.
outcome: His death initiated succession challenges but the Almohad Empire remained dominant for decades.

title: Deaths in 1167 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1167 CE: Al-Sam'ani — scholar (Author of al-Ansab, the great dictionary of Arabic genealogical surnames) in Merv.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1168 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1168 CE: Abu Najib Suhrawardi — Sufi mystic (Founder of the Suhrawardi Path — Uncle and Teacher of Shihab al-Din) in Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Fall of Fatimid Caliphate in Egypt
description: The Fatimid caliph al-Adid died in Cairo, and Saladin abolished the Shi'a Fatimid Caliphate, restoring Sunni allegiance to the Abbasid caliph in Baghdad. This ended over two centuries of Ismaili Shi'a rule in Egypt.
outcome: Egypt returned to Sunni Islam under Ayyubid rule and became the base for Saladin's empire.

title: Saladin overthrows the Fatimid Caliphate
description: Salah al-Din al-Ayyubi (Saladin) abolishes the Fatimid Caliphate in Egypt, restoring Sunni Islam and founding the Ayyubid dynasty.

figures.name: Salahuddin Ayyubi
figures.role: overthrew the Fatimids; restored Sunni Islam in Egypt

outcome: Ended 260 years of Shia rule in Egypt; united Egypt and Syria against the Crusaders

title: Ghurid conquest of Ghazni
description: Ghiyath al-Din Muhammad and his brother Mu'izz al-Din captured Ghazni from the Ghaznavids, establishing Ghurid supremacy in Afghanistan. This opened the path for Muslim expansion into northern India.
outcome: Shifted Islamic power center in Afghanistan and enabled later Delhi Sultanate.

title: Saladin's khutba in Cairo names Abbasid caliph
description: Saladin ordered the Friday khutba in Cairo to be delivered in the name of the Abbasid caliph al-Mustadi instead of the Fatimid caliph, formally ending Fatimid legitimacy. This act symbolically reunited Egypt with the Sunni Muslim world.
outcome: The symbolic restoration of Abbasid authority legitimized Saladin's rule and united Sunni Muslims against the Crusaders.

title: Saladin's khutba in Cairo names Abbasid caliph
description: Saladin ordered the Friday khutba in Cairo to be delivered in the name of the Abbasid caliph al-Mustadi, replacing the Fatimid Ismaili formula. This act formally ended Fatimid caliphal authority and restored Sunni orthodoxy in Egypt.
outcome: This symbolic act consolidated Saladin's legitimacy and realigned Egypt with the Sunni Abbasid caliphate.

title: Almohad conquest of Seville
description: The Almohads under Abu Yaqub Yusuf completed their conquest of Seville from local Andalusian rulers. This consolidated Almohad control over southern Iberia and marked their expansion into al-Andalus.
outcome: Seville became a major Almohad administrative center and cultural capital in Iberia.

title: Ghurid conquest of Ghazni
description: Ghiyath al-Din Muhammad and his brother Mu'izz al-Din (Muhammad of Ghor) captured Ghazni from the Ghaznavids, establishing Ghurid dominance in Afghanistan. This conquest positioned the Ghurids to expand into India.
outcome: The conquest ended the Ghaznavid dynasty and made the Ghurids the dominant power in Afghanistan and the gateway to India.

title: Deaths in 1174 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1174 CE: Nur al-Din Zangi — ruler (Al-Malik al-Adil — The Just King) in Mosul / Aleppo / Damascus.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Death of Nur al-Din
description: Nur al-Din Zangi, ruler of Syria and champion of jihad against Crusaders, died unexpectedly in Damascus. His death created a power vacuum that allowed Saladin to emerge as leader of Muslim resistance.
outcome: Enabled Saladin's rise to power and unification of Egypt and Syria.

title: Death of Nur al-Din
description: Nur al-Din Zangi, the Zangid ruler who unified Syria and laid the groundwork for Muslim resistance to the Crusades, died in Damascus. His death created a power vacuum that Saladin would exploit to establish the Ayyubid dynasty.
outcome: His death enabled Saladin to consolidate control over Syria and Egypt, unifying Muslim forces against the Crusaders.

title: Death of Nur al-Din Zangi
description: Nur al-Din Mahmud Zangi, architect of Muslim unity against the Crusaders, died in Damascus. His death created a power vacuum that allowed his subordinate Saladin to take control of Syria and Egypt.
outcome: His death enabled Saladin's rise and the unification of Egypt and Syria under Ayyubid rule.

title: Battle of Myriokephalon
description: Seljuk Sultan Kilij Arslan II defeated Byzantine Emperor Manuel I Komnenos in Anatolia, ending Byzantine hopes of reconquering central Anatolia. The battle secured Turkish dominance in Asia Minor.
outcome: Permanently established Turkish control of Anatolian interior.

title: Muhammad of Ghor conquers Multan
description: Mu'izz al-Din Muhammad of Ghor captured Multan from the Ismaili Qarmatian rulers, bringing this strategic Indus valley city under Ghurid control. This conquest opened the path for further Ghurid expansion into the Indian subcontinent.
outcome: Multan became a Ghurid base for invasions into the Gangetic plain and northern India.

title: Deaths in 1176 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1176 CE: Ibn Asakir — historian (Hafiz al-Sham — Greatest Historian of Damascus) in Damascus, Syria.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Saladin takes Damascus
description: Saladin entered Damascus and took control of Syria from the young Zangid heir, unifying Egypt and Syria under Ayyubid rule. This consolidation created the power base for his later campaigns against the Crusaders.
outcome: United Egypt and Syria under single ruler for first time since Fatimids.

title: Battle of Myriokephalon
description: The Seljuk Turks of Rum under Kilij Arslan II decisively defeated Byzantine Emperor Manuel I Komnenos in Anatolia. This battle permanently ended Byzantine hopes of reconquering central Anatolia from Turkish control.
outcome: The Byzantine defeat secured Turkish dominance in Anatolia and marked the end of major Byzantine offensives in the region.

title: Battle of Montgisard — Saladin's defeat
description: Saladin suffered a major defeat at Montgisard near Ramla when Baldwin IV of Jerusalem, despite being a leper, led a smaller Crusader force in a surprise attack. The Ayyubid army was routed and Saladin barely escaped.
outcome: The defeat temporarily checked Saladin's expansion but did not prevent his eventual conquest of Jerusalem.

title: Deaths in 1178 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1178 CE: Shuhda al-Katiba — scholar (Fakhr al-Nisa — Pride of Women) in Baghdad, Iraq.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Battle of Marj Ayyun
description: Baldwin IV of Jerusalem defeated Saladin at Marj Ayyun, temporarily halting Ayyubid expansion. Despite being afflicted with leprosy, the young king personally led the charge that routed Saladin's forces.
outcome: Demonstrated Crusader resilience but did not prevent Saladin's ultimate triumph.

title: Battle of Montgisard — Saladin's defeat
description: Baldwin IV of Jerusalem, despite suffering from leprosy, led a surprise attack that routed Saladin's larger army near Montgisard. Saladin barely escaped with his life in one of his worst defeats.
outcome: The unexpected defeat delayed Saladin's plans to recapture Jerusalem by nearly a decade.

title: Kilij Arslan II defeats Manuel Komnenos at Myriokephalon
description: The Seljuk Sultan of Rum, Kilij Arslan II, ambushed and defeated the Byzantine Emperor Manuel I Komnenos at the mountain pass of Myriokephalon. The victory secured Seljuk Anatolia against Byzantine reconquest.
outcome: The battle permanently established Seljuk Turkish control over central Anatolia.

title: Deaths in 1179 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1179 CE: Abdul Khaliq Ghujdawani — Sufi mystic (Founder of Silent Dhikr in the Khwajagan Chain) in Ghujduwan, Uzbekistan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Death of Hildegard of Bingen — Muslim-Christian exchange
description: Hildegard of Bingen, the German abbess whose medical writings showed influence from Arabic medical texts translated in Toledo, died. Her works reflected the transmission of Islamic medical knowledge to Christian Europe.
outcome: Her works exemplified how Islamic scientific knowledge was being absorbed into medieval European scholarship.

title: Construction of the Giralda tower begins in Seville
description: The Almohads began construction of the monumental minaret of the Great Mosque of Seville, known as the Giralda. Designed as both a religious and symbolic structure, it showcased Almohad architectural ambition.
outcome: The Giralda became an iconic example of Almohad architecture and survives today as part of Seville Cathedral.

title: Deaths in 1180 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1180 CE: Al-Samawal al-Maghribi — scientist (Jewish-born mathematician who advanced algebra before converting to Islam) in Baghdad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1182 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1182 CE: Ahmad al-Rifai — founder (Imam al-Ariffin — Founding Pole of the Rifaiyya) in Wasit, Iraq; Ahmed ar-Rifa'i — Sufi mystic (Founder of the Rifa'iyya Sufi order) in Basra.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Conquest of Aleppo by Saladin
description: Saladin peacefully took control of Aleppo from the Zangid dynasty after the death of al-Salih Ismail, unifying Syria under Ayyubid rule. This consolidation gave Saladin control of resources needed to confront the Crusader states.
outcome: The unification of Muslim Syria under Saladin made possible the subsequent reconquest of Jerusalem.

title: Conquest of Aleppo by Saladin
description: Saladin peacefully acquired Aleppo after negotiations with the Zangid ruler Imad al-Din Zangi II. This acquisition consolidated Ayyubid control over Syria and encircled the Crusader states.
outcome: Control of Aleppo gave Saladin strategic depth and resources necessary for his later campaigns against the Crusaders.

title: Construction of Kutubiyya Mosque completed
description: The Kutubiyya Mosque in Marrakesh, commissioned by Almohad Caliph Abu Ya'qub Yusuf, was completed with its iconic 77-meter minaret. The mosque exemplified Almohad architectural style and remains Marrakesh's most famous landmark.
outcome: Became architectural model for later Moroccan and Andalusian mosques.

title: Death of Usama ibn Munqidh
description: Usama ibn Munqidh, the Arab-Syrian knight, poet, and author of Kitab al-I'tibar (Book of Learning by Example), died in Damascus. His memoirs provided invaluable insights into Muslim-Crusader relations and 12th-century Syrian society.
outcome: His memoirs became a primary source for understanding the cultural dynamics of the Crusader period.

title: Battle of Marj Ayyun
description: Saladin defeated a combined Crusader-Byzantine force near Marj Ayyun in southern Lebanon. This victory demonstrated Ayyubid military superiority and set the stage for the conquest of Jerusalem.
outcome: The victory strengthened Saladin's position and demoralized Crusader forces before Hattin.

title: Deaths in 1185 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1185 CE: Ibn Tufayl — philosopher (Abubacer — Author of the First Philosophical Novel) in Cadiz / Marrakesh.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: First Battle of Tarain
description: Muhammad of Ghor was defeated by Prithviraj Chauhan, the Rajput ruler of Delhi and Ajmer, at Tarain near Thanesar. Muhammad was wounded and barely escaped with his life back to Afghanistan.
outcome: Despite this defeat, Muhammad returned the following year to decisively defeat Prithviraj and establish Muslim rule in northern India.

title: Death of Ibn Mardanish, last independent ruler of Valencia
description: Muhammad ibn Sad ibn Mardanish, known to Christians as Rey Lobo, died after decades of maintaining Valencia's independence from both Almohads and Christians. His death led to Almohad annexation of eastern al-Andalus.
outcome: His death ended the last significant independent Muslim taifa state in eastern Iberia.

title: Saladin recaptures Jerusalem — Battle of Hattin
description: Saladin crushes the Crusader army at Hattin and recaptures Jerusalem after 88 years of Crusader rule, treating the defeated with mercy that contrasted sharply with the Crusaders' 1099 massacre.

figures.name: Salahuddin Ayyubi
figures.role: commander; recaptured Jerusalem with famous mercy

outcome: Recaptured Jerusalem for Islam; Saladin's mercy became legendary worldwide

title: Deaths in 1188 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1188 CE: Usama ibn Munqidh — scholar (Crusader-era memoirist and author of Kitab al-I'tibar) in Shaizar.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1189 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1189 CE: Anvari — poet (Master of the Persian panegyric qasida) in Balkh.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Third Crusade launched after Hattin
description: European kings including Richard I of England, Philip II of France, and Frederick Barbarossa embarked on the Third Crusade to recapture Jerusalem from Saladin. The massive expedition represented Christendom's response to the Muslim reconquest.
outcome: The Third Crusade recaptured coastal cities but failed to retake Jerusalem from Saladin.

title: Third Crusade launched after Hattin
description: European monarchs including Richard I of England, Philip II of France, and Frederick Barbarossa launched the Third Crusade in response to Saladin's capture of Jerusalem. This massive expedition sought to reclaim the Holy City for Christendom.
outcome: The Third Crusade failed to retake Jerusalem but resulted in a negotiated peace that allowed Christian pilgrimage.

title: Deaths in 1191 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1191 CE: Yahya Suhrawardi (Maqtul) — philosopher (Shaykh al-Ishraq — Master of Illumination) in Sohrevard / Aleppo.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Battle of Arsuf
description: Richard I of England defeated Saladin's army at Arsuf during the Third Crusade, breaking the Ayyubid momentum. Despite tactical defeat, Saladin maintained strategic control and prevented Crusader reconquest of Jerusalem.
sources: Baha al-Din ibn Shaddad, Sirat Salah al-Din
outcome: Tactical setback but Saladin retained Jerusalem and negotiated favorable peace.

title: Battle of Acre — Crusader victory
description: After a prolonged siege, Crusader forces under Richard I and Philip II captured Acre from Saladin's garrison, establishing a key base for the Third Crusade. Saladin was forced to negotiate the surrender of the city.
outcome: Acre remained the capital of the Crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem until 1291.

title: Battle of Acre — Crusader victory
description: After a two-year siege, the Crusaders under Richard I and Philip II captured the strategic port city of Acre from Saladin's forces. The fall of Acre was a significant setback for the Ayyubids during the Third Crusade.
outcome: Acre became the Crusader capital of the Kingdom of Jerusalem for the next century.

title: First Battle of Tarain
description: Prithviraj Chauhan of the Rajput Chahamana dynasty defeated Muhammad of Ghor in northern India near Tarain. This initial victory temporarily halted Ghurid expansion into the Indian subcontinent.
outcome: The defeat forced Muhammad to regroup, but he would return the following year with a larger army.

title: Battle of Arsuf
description: Richard I of England defeated Saladin's forces at Arsuf during the Third Crusade as the Crusaders marched from Acre to Jaffa. Despite tactical defeat, Saladin's strategic position remained strong as he retained Jerusalem.
outcome: Richard's victory allowed the Crusaders to secure the coast but failed to lead to Jerusalem's recapture.

title: Battle of Arsuf
description: Richard I of England defeated Saladin's forces at Arsuf during the Third Crusade, breaking Muslim attempts to prevent the Crusader march to Jaffa. Despite the tactical victory, Richard could not retake Jerusalem.
sources: Baha al-Din ibn Shaddad, al-Nawadir al-Sultaniyya
outcome: The battle demonstrated Crusader military superiority in open combat but failed to achieve strategic objectives.

title: Muhammad of Ghor invades India
description: Mu'izz al-Din Muhammad of Ghor defeated Prithviraj Chauhan at the Second Battle of Tarain, opening northern India to Muslim rule. This victory laid foundations for the Delhi Sultanate.
outcome: Established permanent Muslim political presence in North India.

title: Second Battle of Tarain
description: Muhammad of Ghor decisively defeated Prithviraj Chauhan at Tarain, capturing and executing the Rajput king. This victory opened northern India to Muslim conquest and established the foundations for the Delhi Sultanate.
outcome: Muslim rule was permanently established in northern India, transforming the subcontinent's political landscape.

title: Second Battle of Tarain
description: Muhammad of Ghor decisively defeated Prithviraj Chauhan in a rematch at Tarain, opening northern India to Muslim conquest. Prithviraj was captured and executed, ending Rajput dominance in the region.
outcome: This victory established Muslim political dominance in northern India and paved the way for the Delhi Sultanate.

title: Qutb al-Din Aibak captures Delhi
description: Qutb al-Din Aibak, a Turkish slave-general of Muhammad of Ghor, captured Delhi and established it as a major Muslim stronghold in northern India. This conquest laid the foundation for the Delhi Sultanate.
outcome: Delhi became the capital of Muslim India and remained so for centuries under successive sultanates.

title: Deaths in 1193 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1193 CE: Salahuddin Ayyubi — warrior (Al-Nasir — Defender of the Faith; Liberator of Jerusalem) in Tikrit / Cairo / Damascus.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Death of Saladin
description: Salah al-Din Yusuf ibn Ayyub died in Damascus at age 55, having recaptured Jerusalem and unified Muslim resistance to Crusades. His death led to division of Ayyubid territories among his sons and brothers.
outcome: Ayyubid empire fragmented but dynasty continued ruling Egypt and Syria for decades.

title: Qutb al-Din Aibak captures Delhi
description: Qutb al-Din Aibak, general of Muhammad of Ghor, captured Delhi from the Rajputs and made it the capital of Ghurid territories in India. This marked the beginning of Delhi as the center of Muslim power in the subcontinent.
outcome: Delhi became the capital of successive Muslim sultanates ruling India for over three centuries.

title: Death of Saladin
description: Salah al-Din Yusuf ibn Ayyub (Saladin), founder of the Ayyubid dynasty and reconqueror of Jerusalem, died in Damascus. His death led to the division of the Ayyubid realm among his sons and brothers.
outcome: Despite fragmentation, the Ayyubid dynasty continued to rule Egypt and Syria for several generations.

title: Battle of Chandawar
description: Muhammad of Ghor and his commander Qutb al-Din Aibak defeated the Gahadavala king Jayachandra at Chandawar near Kannauj. The victory opened the Gangetic plain to Ghurid expansion and Muslim rule in northern India.
outcome: The battle secured Muslim control over the critical Gangetic heartland of North India.

title: Battle of Chandawar
description: Muhammad of Ghor defeated Jaichand, the ruler of Kannauj, at Chandawar near Firozabad, establishing Muslim control over the Gangetic plain. Jaichand was killed in battle and his kingdom annexed.
outcome: The victory extended Muslim rule to Bihar and Bengal, bringing the richest regions of India under Ghurid control.

title: Battle of Chandawar
description: Muhammad of Ghor defeated the Gahadavala king Jayachandra near Chandawar, securing control over the Gangetic plain. This victory extended Ghurid dominance to the heartland of northern India.
outcome: The Ghurid conquest of the Gangetic plain completed the Muslim domination of northern India.

title: Battle of Alarcos
description: Almohad Caliph Abu Yusuf Ya'qub al-Mansur decisively defeated Castilian King Alfonso VIII at Alarcos in al-Andalus. This victory temporarily halted Christian Reconquista and restored Muslim military prestige in Iberia.
outcome: Gave Almohads decade of dominance before Las Navas de Tolosa reversed fortunes.

title: Death of Ibn Zuhr (Avenzoar)
description: Abu Marwan ibn Zuhr, the Andalusian physician and author of al-Taysir, died in Seville. His medical writings influenced both Islamic and European medicine, particularly through collaborations with Ibn Rushd.
outcome: His clinical descriptions and therapeutic approaches influenced medical practice in both Islamic and Christian Europe.

title: Battle of Alarcos
description: The Almohad caliph Abu Yusuf al-Mansur decisively defeated Alfonso VIII of Castile at Alarcos in central Spain. The victory halted Christian advances and reasserted Muslim power in Iberia.
outcome: The Almohad victory delayed Christian Reconquista for nearly two decades until the Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa.

title: Battle of Alarcos
description: The Almohad caliph Abu Yusuf Yaqub al-Mansur decisively defeated Alfonso VIII of Castile at Alarcos in central Iberia. This victory temporarily halted Christian advances and reasserted Muslim military dominance in Al-Andalus.
outcome: The Almohad victory delayed the Reconquista but proved temporary as Christian forces would recover within two decades.

title: Deaths in 1198 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1198 CE: Abu Madyan — Sufi mystic (Shu'ayb al-Andalusi — Patron Saint of Tlemcen) in Seville / Bejaia, Algeria; Ibn Rushd — philosopher (al-Mu'allim al-Thalith — The Great Commentator) in Córdoba / Marrakesh.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Death of Ibn Rushd (Averroes)
description: Ibn Rushd dies in Marrakesh; his commentaries on Aristotle will ignite the intellectual revolution in medieval Europe that leads to Aquinas and the Renaissance.

figures.name: Ibn Rushd
figures.role: died; his Aristotle commentaries transformed European philosophy

outcome: His work reached Europe as 'Averroes' and sparked the Scholastic revolution

title: Death of Ibn Rushd (Averroes)
description: Ibn Rushd died in Marrakesh after years of disgrace under Almohad authorities who condemned his philosophical writings. His Aristotelian commentaries profoundly influenced both Islamic and European medieval thought.
outcome: His works shaped European scholasticism despite rejection by some Muslim scholars.

title: Death of Ibn Rushd (Averroes)
description: Abu al-Walid Muhammad ibn Rushd, the Andalusian philosopher and physician known in the West as Averroes, died in Marrakesh. His commentaries on Aristotle profoundly influenced both Islamic and European philosophy.
outcome: His works sparked the Averroist movement in medieval Europe and shaped the reception of Aristotelian philosophy.

title: Death of Ibn Rushd (Averroes)
description: Abu al-Walid ibn Rushd, known in Europe as Averroes, died in Marrakesh after years of disgrace under Almohad persecution. His philosophical commentaries on Aristotle profoundly influenced both Islamic and European scholastic thought.
outcome: His works became foundational to European scholasticism and sparked centuries of debate on reason and revelation.

title: Death of Ibn Rushd (Averroes)
description: Abu al-Walid Muhammad ibn Ahmad ibn Rushd, known in the West as Averroes, died in Marrakesh. The great Andalusian polymath had profoundly influenced both Islamic philosophy and medieval European scholasticism through his commentaries on Aristotle.
sources: Ibn Abi Usaybi'a, Uyun al-Anba fi Tabaqat al-Atibba
outcome: His works continued to shape both Islamic and Christian philosophical traditions for centuries.

title: Deaths in 1201 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1201 CE: Ibn al-Jawzi — scholar (Imam al-Wa'iz — The Great Preacher of Baghdad) in Baghdad, Iraq; Abu'l-Faraj ibn al-Jawzi — philosopher (Ibn al-Jawzi (c) in Baghdad.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Death of Al-Qadi al-Fadil
description: Abd al-Rahim al-Baysani, known as al-Qadi al-Fadil, Saladin's chief secretary and advisor, died in Cairo. He was one of the most influential administrators in Ayyubid Egypt and a master of Arabic prose.
outcome: His administrative reforms and literary style influenced chancery practices across the Islamic world for generations.

title: Deaths in 1204 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1204 CE: Abu al-Abbas as-Sabti — Sufi mystic (Sidi Bel Abbas) in Ceuta under Muslim rule.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Death of Maimonides in Egypt
description: Moses ben Maimon (Maimonides), the great Jewish philosopher and physician who served at Saladin's court, died in Fustat, Egypt. His medical writings and philosophical works, written largely in Arabic, influenced Muslim, Jewish, and Christian scholars.
outcome: His synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy and religious thought shaped interfaith intellectual discourse.

title: Death of Salah al-Din's brother al-Adil I proclaimed Sultan
description: Al-Adil I (al-Malik al-Adil Sayf al-Din) consolidates power over the Ayyubid realm after years of maneuvering following Salah al-Din's death. He ruled from Damascus and Cairo, reuniting much of the Ayyubid territories under his direct control.
outcome: Al-Adil's reign stabilized the Ayyubid state and maintained relative peace with the Crusaders until his death in 1218.

title: Deaths in 1206 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1206 CE: al-Jazari — scientist (Father of Robotics and Mechanical Engineering) in Upper Mesopotamia / Diyarbakir, Turkey; Muhammad of Ghor — ruler (Ghurid sultan who established Muslim rule in northern India) in Ghazni.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Delhi Sultanate founded
description: Qutb al-Din Aibak, a former Ghurid slave-general, establishes the Delhi Sultanate, beginning over three centuries of Muslim rule in northern India.
outcome: Began 320 years of Muslim rule in India; paved the way for the Mughal Empire

title: Genghis Khan unifies the Mongol tribes
description: Temüjin is proclaimed Genghis Khan (Universal Ruler), unifying the Mongol tribes into the military force that will devastate the Islamic world.
outcome: Created the military machine that would destroy Baghdad and reshape the Islamic world

title: Death of Qutb al-Din Aibak
description: Qutb al-Din Aibak, founder of the Mamluk dynasty in Delhi and former slave commander, died in a polo accident in Lahore. His death led to a succession crisis in the young sultanate.
outcome: His brief reign established the precedent of Turkish Mamluk rule in northern India.

title: Death of Muhammad of Ghor
description: Sultan Mu'izz al-Din Muhammad of Ghor, who had conquered much of northern India and established Muslim rule in Delhi, was assassinated at Dhamiak. His death led to the succession of his slave-general Qutb al-Din Aibak and the consolidation of the Delhi Sultanate.
outcome: His death marked the transition from Ghurid to independent sultanate rule in India.

title: Death of Al-Malik al-Adil's rival al-Afdal
description: Al-Afdal ibn Salah al-Din, the eldest son of Saladin who had ruled Damascus, dies after being politically sidelined by his uncle al-Adil. His death ended the succession disputes that had plagued the Ayyubid dynasty since Saladin's death in 1193.
sources: Abu al-Fida, al-Mukhtasar fi Akhbar al-Bashar
outcome: Consolidated Ayyubid power under al-Adil's branch of the family.

title: Deaths in 1207 CE
