outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1693 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1693 CE: Abd al-Rauf al-Singkili — scholar (Shaykh al-Kurani's Greatest Student) in Singkil, Aceh / Medina.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1694 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1694 CE: Niyazi-i Misri — poet (Shaykh of Bursa) in Malatya / Bursa, Ottoman Empire.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Death of Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Maqqari
description: The Maghrebi scholar Ahmad al-Maqqari died in Cairo, leaving behind his monumental work Nafh al-Tib, a literary encyclopedia about Muslim Spain and its scholars. The work preserved invaluable material about al-Andalus that would otherwise have been lost.
sources: Pascual de Gayangos, The History of the Mohammedan Dynasties in Spain
outcome: His encyclopedia became the primary source for understanding Andalusian Islamic civilization and literature.

title: Death of Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Maqqari in Cairo
description: Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Maqqari al-Tilmisani, renowned Maghrebi historian and scholar, died in Cairo. He authored the monumental Nafh al-Tib, a comprehensive history of al-Andalus and biography of Ibn al-Khatib, preserving crucial knowledge of Andalusian Islamic civilization.
sources: al-Maqqari, Nafh al-Tib min Ghusn al-Andalus al-Ratib
outcome: His historical works became the most important source for the history and culture of al-Andalus.

title: Battle of Zenta — Habsburg decisive victory over Ottomans
description: Prince Eugene of Savoy led Habsburg forces to a crushing victory over Ottoman forces under Sultan Mustafa II at the Tisza River near Zenta. The battle resulted in massive Ottoman casualties and decisively ended Ottoman offensive capability in Europe.
sources: Derek McKay, Prince Eugene of Savoy
outcome: The catastrophic defeat forced the Ottomans to accept the Treaty of Karlowitz and recognize major territorial losses.

title: Deaths in 1699 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1699 CE: Muhammad Yusuf al-Maqassari — Sufi mystic (Tuanta Salamaka — Our Master the Blessed) in Gowa, Sulawesi / Cape Town.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Treaty of Karlowitz - Ottoman territorial losses formalized
description: The Treaty of Karlowitz ended the Great Turkish War with the Ottomans ceding Hungary, Transylvania, and significant Balkan territories to the Habsburgs and Poland. It was the first major treaty where the Ottomans negotiated as the losing party.
sources: Halil Inalcik, The Ottoman Empire: The Classical Age
outcome: Marked the beginning of Ottoman territorial decline in Europe and shift from expansion to defensive posture.

title: Deaths in 1702 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1702 CE: Zeb-un-Nissa — poet (Princess-Poet of the Makhfi — Mystical Mughal Poetry) in Dakka / Delhi, Mughal India.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Birth of Shah Waliullah and Ibn Abd al-Wahhab
description: In the same year, two of the most consequential Islamic reformers are born: Shah Waliullah in Delhi and Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab in Najd. Their reform movements — one integrative, one puritanical — will reshape the modern Muslim world.

figures.name: Shah Waliullah
figures.role: born in Delhi; would become India's greatest Islamic thinker

figures.name: Ibn Abd al-Wahhab
figures.role: born in Najd; would found the Wahhabi movement that allied with the House of Saud

sources: J.M.S. Baljon, Religion and Thought of Shah Wali Allah
outcome: Both would launch reform movements that fundamentally shaped modern Sunni Islam in divergent directions

title: Founding of the Sultanate of Siak Sri Indrapura
description: Raja Kecil establishes the Sultanate of Siak Sri Indrapura in eastern Sumatra after claiming descent from the Johor-Riau royal lineage. The sultanate becomes an important Islamic kingdom controlling trade routes in the Straits of Malacca region.
sources: Tuhfat al-Nafis by Raja Ali Haji
outcome: Siak became a major Islamic trading power in Sumatra until Dutch colonial expansion.

title: Edirne Incident — Janissary revolt forces Sultan Mustafa II to abdicate
description: Janissary corps and sipahi cavalry rebelled in Edirne against Sultan Mustafa II following Ottoman military defeats. The sultan was forced to abdicate in favor of his brother Ahmed III, marking another instance of military influence over Ottoman succession.
sources: Stanford Shaw, History of the Ottoman Empire and Modern Turkey
outcome: The revolt demonstrated the continued power of the Janissary corps in Ottoman politics and inaugurated the Tulip Period under Ahmed III.

title: Ahmad III becomes Ottoman Sultan
description: Ahmad III ascended to the Ottoman throne following the deposition of his brother Mustafa II after the Edirne Incident. His reign ushered in the Tulip Era, a period of cultural flourishing and architectural patronage in the empire.
sources: Stanford Shaw, History of the Ottoman Empire
outcome: His reign marked the beginning of the Tulip Era and increased Ottoman engagement with European culture.

title: Death of Sultan Mustafa II in exile
description: Former Ottoman Sultan Mustafa II died in confinement at Topkapı Palace after being deposed in the Edirne Incident of 1703. His reign had been marked by military defeats against Austria and the loss of Hungary at the Treaty of Karlowitz.
sources: Joseph von Hammer-Purgstall, Geschichte des Osmanischen Reiches
outcome: His death marked the end of a turbulent era that saw the first major Janissary revolt resulting in a sultan's forced abdication.

title: Deaths in 1706 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1706 CE: Rahman Baba — poet (Greatest Pashto Sufi poet) in Peshawar.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1707 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1707 CE: Aurangzeb — ruler (Alamgir — Last Great Mughal Emperor) in Dahod / Aurangabad / Delhi; Wali Gujarati — poet (Father of Urdu Poetry) in Aurangabad / Surat / Delhi.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Death of Aurangzeb — Mughal decline begins
description: The death of Emperor Aurangzeb at age 88 after a 49-year reign triggers the rapid disintegration of the Mughal Empire. Succession wars, regional revolts and rising Maratha power fragment the subcontinent.

figures.name: Aurangzeb
figures.role: 6th Mughal emperor; his death triggered imperial fragmentation

outcome: Ended Mughal centralised power and opened India to European colonialism

title: Battle of Jajau and Mughal succession crisis
description: Muhammad Azam Shah is defeated and killed by his brother Bahadur Shah I at Jajau, eight months after Aurangzeb's death. This succession war further weakened the Mughal Empire and set a precedent for destructive civil wars.
outcome: The Mughal succession wars accelerated imperial decline and provincial independence movements.

title: Russo-Ottoman War ends with Treaty of Pruth
description: Ottoman Grand Vizier Baltaci Mehmed Pasha defeats Peter the Great's Russian forces on the Pruth River. The treaty restores Azov to the Ottomans and temporarily halts Russian expansion into Muslim territories.
outcome: Ottoman military success temporarily reversed Russian territorial gains but proved to be the last major Ottoman victory against Russia.

title: Husaynid dynasty established in Tunis
description: Husayn ibn Ali al-Turki, an Ottoman cavalry officer, overthrew the Muradid dynasty and established himself as Bey of Tunis. The Husaynid dynasty would rule Tunisia with nominal Ottoman suzerainty until 1957.
sources: Kenneth Perkins, A History of Modern Tunisia
outcome: The Husaynid dynasty transformed Tunisia into a semi-autonomous province that would last over two centuries.

title: Shams al-Din al-Sufi completes commentary on al-Jazari's automata
description: Ottoman scholar Shams al-Din Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Sufi completed his detailed commentary on al-Jazari's famous 'Book of Knowledge of Ingenious Mechanical Devices'. This work preserved and expanded upon earlier Islamic engineering knowledge during a period of renewed Ottoman interest in practical sciences.
sources: Donald Hill, Islamic Science and Engineering
Ahmad Y. al-Hassan, Islamic Technology: An Illustrated History
outcome: The commentary helped transmit classical Islamic engineering knowledge to 18th-century Ottoman scholars and European visitors.

title: Ottoman reconquest of the Morea from Venice
description: Grand Vizier Damat Ali Pasha leads Ottoman forces to recapture the Peloponnese peninsula from Venetian control during the Seventh Ottoman-Venetian War. The campaign restored Ottoman dominance in southern Greece.
outcome: The reconquest extended Ottoman rule in Greece for another century until the Greek War of Independence.

title: Karim Khan Zand born in Persia
description: Karim Khan Zand was born into the Zand tribe in Persia, who would later found the Zand dynasty after Nader Shah's death. He would rule as Vakil e-Ra'aayaa (Representative of the People) rather than Shah.
sources: John Perry, Karim Khan Zand: A History of Iran
outcome: Karim Khan would establish a brief period of stability and prosperity in Persia between the chaos following Nader Shah and the Qajar ascendancy.

title: Treaty of Passarowitz ends Ottoman-Venetian-Habsburg War
description: The Ottoman Empire signed the Treaty of Passarowitz with Habsburg Austria and Venice, ceding significant territories including Belgrade, northern Serbia, and parts of Wallachia. The treaty marked the end of Ottoman expansion in Europe and a shift to defensive posture.
outcome: The treaty represented the greatest extent of Habsburg gains from the Ottomans and initiated the Tulip Period of Ottoman cultural flourishing.

title: Death of Piri Mehmed Pasha
description: Piri Mehmed Pasha, Ottoman Grand Vizier who negotiated the Treaty of Passarowitz, died in office. He had served under Sultan Ahmed III during a critical period of Ottoman diplomacy with European powers.
outcome: His death marked the end of an era of diplomatic engagement following territorial losses.

title: Sayyid Brothers control the Mughal throne
description: The Sayyid Brothers, Abdullah Khan and Husain Ali Khan Barha, emerge as kingmakers in Delhi, deposing Emperor Farrukhsiyar and installing Muhammad Shah. Their dominance exemplifies the collapse of central Mughal authority.
outcome: The era of powerful nobles controlling puppet emperors marked the effective end of Mughal centralized power.

title: Deaths in 1720 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1720 CE: Bedil Dehlawi — poet (Hazrat Bedil — Last Master of the Indian Persian Style) in Azimabad (Patna) / Delhi.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Construction of Sadabad Palace complex begins in Istanbul
description: Sultan Ahmed III commissioned the Sadabad Palace and gardens in the Kağıthane valley as part of the Tulip Period cultural renaissance. The complex featured elaborate gardens, fountains, and pavilions influenced by French baroque aesthetics.
sources: Shirine Hamadeh, The City's Pleasures: Istanbul in the Eighteenth Century
outcome: The palace symbolized the Tulip Period's emphasis on cultural refinement and European-influenced aesthetics, though it was destroyed after the 1730 revolt.

title: Construction of Nuruosmaniye Mosque begins
description: Sultan Mahmud I initiated construction of the Nuruosmaniye Mosque in Istanbul, which would become the first Ottoman mosque built in Baroque style. The mosque represented the Ottoman embrace of European architectural influences during the Tulip Era.
sources: Godfrey Goodwin, A History of Ottoman Architecture
outcome: The mosque pioneered Baroque elements in Ottoman sacred architecture.

title: Sayyid Muhammad Ma'sum establishes Naqshbandi presence in Deccan
description: Sayyid Muhammad Ma'sum, son of Ahmad Sirhindi, established a major Naqshbandi-Mujaddidi khanqah in the Deccan region of India. His teachings emphasized reform and adherence to Islamic law within the Sufi tradition, influencing subsequent Muslim scholars in South India.
sources: Arthur F. Buehler, Sufi Heirs of the Prophet
K.A. Nizami, Naqshbandi Influence on Mughal Rulers
outcome: The Naqshbandi-Mujaddidi order became a major force for Islamic reform in the Deccan, influencing 18th-century Muslim revivalism.

title: Afghan siege of Isfahan — Safavid Empire collapses
description: The Ghilzai Afghans under Mahmud Hotak besiege and capture Isfahan after a devastating seven-month siege that kills hundreds of thousands. Shah Sultan Husayn surrenders, ending 220 years of Safavid rule.
sources: Roger Savory, Iran Under the Safavids
outcome: Ended the Safavid dynasty and plunged Iran into decades of chaos before the rise of Nader Shah

title: Muhammad bin Abdul Karim founds Imamate of Futa Jallon
description: Fulani Muslim scholar Karamokho Alfa leads a successful jihad against local non-Muslim Jallonke rulers in present-day Guinea. He establishes a theocratic Islamic state that promotes education and Maliki jurisprudence.
sources: oral traditions collected by Thierno Diallo
outcome: The imamate became a center of Islamic learning in West Africa and lasted until French colonization.

title: Mahmud Hotak captures Isfahan and proclaims Afghan rule
description: Mahmud Hotak, leader of the Ghilzai Afghans, captured the Safavid capital of Isfahan after a lengthy siege, forcing Shah Sultan Husayn to abdicate. The conquest effectively ended Safavid rule, though resistance continued under Tahmasp II.
outcome: The fall of Isfahan plunged Persia into chaos and enabled Nader Shah's eventual rise to power.

title: Mir Mahmud Hotaki enters Isfahan
description: Mir Mahmud Hotaki led Afghan forces into Isfahan after a prolonged siege, forcing Safavid Shah Sultan Husayn to abdicate. This event effectively ended Safavid rule over Persia after more than two centuries.
sources: Laurence Lockhart, The Fall of the Safavi Dynasty
outcome: The Safavid dynasty collapsed, leading to decades of instability in Persia.

title: Deaths in 1725 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1725 CE: Ismail Hakki Bursevi — scholar (Shaykh of Bursa — Sufi Encyclopaedist) in Bursa, Ottoman Empire.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Ibrahim Muteferrika establishes first printing press in Ottoman Empire
description: Ibrahim Muteferrika, a Hungarian convert to Islam, received permission from Sultan Ahmed III to establish the first Turkish-language printing press in Istanbul. The press published scientific, historical, and geographical works but not religious texts.
sources: Orlin Sabev, Ibrahim Müteferrika ya da İlk Osmanlı Matbaa Serüveni
outcome: Though religious opposition limited its output, Muteferrika's press marked the beginning of Ottoman engagement with print technology and knowledge dissemination.

title: Death of Sidi Muhammad ibn Abdallah al-Jazuli
description: The Moroccan Sufi scholar and theologian Sidi Muhammad ibn Abdallah al-Jazuli died in Fez. He was a prominent teacher of Islamic jurisprudence and hadith during the Alawi dynasty.
sources: Jamil Abun-Nasr, A History of the Maghrib
outcome: His teachings influenced Moroccan Islamic scholarship for generations.

title: Deaths in 1730 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1730 CE: Nedim — poet (Voice of the Tulip Era — Greatest Ottoman Lyric Poet) in Istanbul, Ottoman Empire.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Patrona Halil Revolt deposes Sultan Ahmed III
description: Janissary revolt led by Patrona Halil forces Sultan Ahmed III to abdicate, ending the Tulip Era of Ottoman cultural renaissance and reform. The rebellion reflected conservative opposition to Westernizing reforms and military modernization.
outcome: The revolt ended Ottoman cultural flourishing and strengthened conservative Janissary opposition to reform for decades.

title: Karamanli dynasty established in Tripolitania
description: Ahmed Karamanli, an Ottoman cavalry officer, seized power in Tripoli and established the Karamanli dynasty that ruled semi-autonomously until 1835. The dynasty profited from Mediterranean piracy and trade.
sources: Ali Abdullatif Ahmida, The Making of Modern Libya
outcome: The Karamanli dynasty established Tripolitania as a significant naval power in the Mediterranean and later became involved in conflicts with the United States.

title: Death of Mustafa II following exile
description: Former Ottoman Sultan Mustafa II died in confinement after being deposed in 1703 during the Edirne Incident. He had ruled during the difficult period of Ottoman defeats against the Holy League.
outcome: His death closed a chapter on one of the Ottoman Empire's most challenging periods.

title: Baba Ali Cafer's Sufi rebellion in Anatolia
description: The Sufi shaykh Baba Ali Cafer led a major rebellion in eastern Anatolia against Ottoman authority, drawing support from disaffected Turkmen tribes and heterodox Sufi groups. The revolt was eventually suppressed by Ottoman forces, but revealed deep tensions in Anatolian society.
sources: Rudi Paul Lindner, Explorations in Ottoman Prehistory
outcome: The rebellion highlighted ongoing conflicts between centralized Ottoman authority and heterodox Sufi movements in the provinces.

title: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab marries into Al ash-Sheikh family
description: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab married a woman from the influential Al ash-Sheikh scholarly family in Najd, strengthening his position. This family alliance complemented his political alliance with the Al Saud and created a powerful religious-political nexus.
outcome: The marriage created a scholarly dynasty that would maintain religious authority in Saudi Arabia through the Al ash-Sheikh family for centuries.

title: Nasir al-Din al-Tusi's astronomical tables revised at Samarkand
description: A group of Central Asian astronomers at Samarkand completed revisions of Nasir al-Din al-Tusi's 13th-century astronomical tables (Zij-i Ilkhani). The revised tables incorporated new observations and corrections, demonstrating continuity in Islamic astronomical tradition.
sources: E.S. Kennedy, A Survey of Islamic Astronomical Tables
Benno van Dalen, Islamic Astronomical Tables
outcome: These revisions preserved the Ilkhanid astronomical tradition and influenced regional timekeeping practices into the 19th century.

title: Nader Shah crowned Shah of Persia
description: Military commander Nader Khan Afshar deposes the last Safavid shah and crowns himself as Nader Shah, founding the Afsharid dynasty. He attempts to bridge Sunni-Shia divide by promoting Ja'fari school as fifth legal madhhab.
sources: Tarikh-e Jahangushay-e Naderi by Mirza Mehdi Khan Astarabadi
outcome: Nader's military campaigns temporarily restored Persian imperial power but his dynasty collapsed shortly after his assassination.

title: Sultan Mahmud I begins reign
description: Mahmud I became Ottoman Sultan following the deposition of Ahmed III in the Patrona Halil revolt. His reign focused on military reforms and recovery from the empire's recent territorial losses.
sources: Stanford Shaw, History of the Ottoman Empire
outcome: Mahmud I initiated important military and administrative reforms in the Ottoman state.

title: Death of Sultan Husayn of Persia
description: Shah Sultan Husayn, the last effective Safavid ruler, died in captivity after being forced to abdicate in 1722. His weak rule had enabled the Afghan invasion that ended the dynasty.
sources: Laurence Lockhart, The Fall of the Safavi Dynasty
outcome: His death marked the symbolic end of the Safavid dynasty.

title: Nader Shah sacks Delhi — loots the Peacock Throne
description: The Persian conqueror Nader Shah invades Mughal India, defeats the Mughal army at the Battle of Karnal, and sacks Delhi — carrying off the Peacock Throne, the Koh-i-Noor diamond and treasure worth an estimated £87.5 million.
sources: Michael Axworthy, The Sword of Persia: Nader Shah
outcome: Fatally weakened the Mughal Empire and demonstrated the power vacuum in the Muslim world

title: Nader Shah's massacre of Delhi inhabitants
description: Following his victory at Karnal and entry into Delhi, Nader Shah orders a massacre of Delhi's population after a riot kills several Persian soldiers. Estimates range from 20,000 to 30,000 deaths in a single day.
outcome: The massacre and looting devastated Delhi and marked the symbolic end of Mughal power.

title: Treaty of Belgrade — Ottomans recover territory from Austria
description: Following Ottoman military successes against Austria, the Treaty of Belgrade returned Belgrade and northern Serbia to Ottoman control. The treaty reversed some losses from Passarowitz and was mediated by France.
outcome: The treaty demonstrated Ottoman military revival under the Köprülü reforms and maintained Ottoman presence in the Balkans.

title: Ibn Abd al-Wahhab allies with Muhammad ibn Saud
description: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab and Muhammad ibn Saud forge the Pact of Najd in Diriyah, creating a religio-political alliance: Ibn Saud provides military power, Ibn Abd al-Wahhab provides religious legitimacy.

figures.name: Ibn Abd al-Wahhab
figures.role: religious reformer; provided theological legitimacy for the Saudi state

sources: Alexei Vassiliev, The History of Saudi Arabia
outcome: Founded the Saudi-Wahhabi alliance that produced three Saudi states and ultimately modern Saudi Arabia

title: First Saudi-Ottoman War begins
description: Muhammad ibn Saud and Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab begin military campaigns to expand their emirate, attacking towns in Najd that reject Wahhabi teachings. This initiates decades of conflict with Ottoman-aligned forces.
sources: Unwan al-Majd fi Tarikh Najd by Uthman ibn Bishr
outcome: The Wahhabi-Saudi expansion reshaped Arabian politics and eventually threatened Ottoman control of holy cities.

title: Muhammad bin Saud captures Dir'iyyah rivals and consolidates Najd power
description: Muhammad bin Saud, in alliance with Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, defeated rival clans and consolidated control over Dir'iyyah and surrounding areas. This marked the expansion phase of the first Saudi state.
sources: Alexei Vassiliev, The History of Saudi Arabia
outcome: The military successes established the first Saudi state as a significant power in central Arabia.

title: First Saudi state founded in Dir'iyyah
description: Muhammad ibn Saud and Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab formed their alliance in Dir'iyyah, creating the First Saudi State. This pact combined political power with religious reform, establishing Wahhabism as the state ideology.
sources: Alexei Vassiliev, The History of Saudi Arabia
outcome: This alliance created the foundation for the modern Saudi Arabian state.

title: Deaths in 1745 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1745 CE: Ibrahim Muteferrika — scholar (Founder of the first Muslim-world printing press) in Istanbul.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Sidi Muhammad III begins construction of Essaouira port
description: Moroccan Sultan Sidi Muhammad III commissioned the construction of the Atlantic port city of Essaouira (then Mogador) as a major trading hub. French architect Théodore Cornut designed the fortifications blending European and Moroccan styles, creating a planned city that attracted Jewish and Muslim merchants.
sources: Jamil M. Abun-Nasr, A History of the Maghrib
outcome: Essaouira became Morocco's principal Atlantic port for European trade, particularly for grain, sugar, and ostrich feathers.

title: Deaths in 1747 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1747 CE: Nadir Shah — ruler (Napoleon of Persia — Conqueror of Delhi) in Dastgird / Mashhad / Delhi.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Shah Waliullah completes Hujjat Allah al-Baligha
description: The Delhi scholar Shah Waliullah Dehlavi completes his magnum opus Hujjat Allah al-Baligha (The Conclusive Argument from God), a comprehensive reinterpretation of Islamic law and theology that seeks to reconcile all four Sunni schools.

figures.name: Shah Waliullah
figures.role: scholar-reformer; produced the most important Islamic theological work of the 18th century

sources: J.M.S. Baljon, Religion and Thought of Shah Wali Allah
outcome: Produced the intellectual foundation for Islamic reform in South Asia that influenced every subsequent movement

title: Ahmad Shah Durrani founds the Durrani Empire
description: After Nader Shah's assassination, Afghan commander Ahmad Khan Abdali is elected as Ahmad Shah Durrani by a tribal assembly in Kandahar. He founds the Durrani Empire, the last great Afghan empire.
outcome: The Durrani Empire dominated Afghanistan and parts of India, and Ahmad Shah is considered the founder of modern Afghanistan.

title: Death of Nader Shah leads to Persia's fragmentation
description: Nader Shah was assassinated by his own officers near Mashhad, ending his brutal but militarily successful reign. His death led to decades of civil war among the Zand, Qajar, and Afsharid claimants.
sources: Michael Axworthy, The Sword of Persia: Nader Shah
outcome: Nader Shah's death created a power vacuum that fragmented Persia until Qajar reunification at century's end.

title: Muhammad al-Majdhub establishes Sammaniyya presence in Sudan
description: Sudanese Sufi scholar Muhammad al-Majdhub spreads the Sammaniyya Sufi order along the Nile Valley after studying in Medina. His teachings blend Sufi mysticism with Islamic orthodoxy and gain widespread following.
outcome: The Sammaniyya order became one of the most influential Sufi movements in Sudan and East Africa.

title: Karim Khan Zand establishes control over Shiraz
description: Karim Khan Zand established Shiraz as his capital and took the title Vakil e-Ra'aayaa rather than Shah, claiming to rule on behalf of the Safavid heir. His moderate rule brought stability to southern and central Persia.
outcome: Karim Khan's reign created a prosperous interlude in 18th-century Persia, though the Zand dynasty collapsed shortly after his death.

title: Muhammad ibn Idris al-Shafi'i's works reach Indonesia
description: Shafi'i legal texts and commentaries were widely disseminated throughout the Indonesian archipelago through Yemeni and Hadhrami traders. This solidified Shafi'i jurisprudence as the dominant madhhab in Southeast Asian Islam.
sources: Azyumardi Azra, The Origins of Islamic Reformism in Southeast Asia
outcome: Shafi'i law became firmly established as the predominant school in Southeast Asian Islam.

title: Battle of Arcot — Nawab of Carnatic besieged by French and Chanda Sahib
description: Muhammad Ali Khan Wallajah, Nawab of the Carnatic, defended Arcot against forces of rival claimant Chanda Sahib supported by French colonial troops under Dupleix. British forces under Robert Clive broke the siege, marking an important episode in the Anglo-French rivalry for influence over South Indian Muslim states.
sources: P.J. Marshall, Bengal: The British Bridgehead
Randolf G.S. Cooper, The Anglo-Maratha Campaigns
outcome: The siege marked a turning point in British ascendancy over French influence in southern India and determined the succession to the Carnatic nawabship.

title: Deaths in 1752 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1752 CE: Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai — poet (Shah jo Risalo — Voice of Sindh) in Bhit Shah, Sindh, Pakistan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Sheikh Mansur born in Chechnya
description: Ushurma (later known as Sheikh Mansur) was born in Chechnya and would become a Sufi leader who led resistance against Russian expansion. He combined Naqshbandi Sufism with calls for Islamic unity against colonialism.
sources: Moshe Gammer, Muslim Resistance to the Tsar
outcome: Sheikh Mansur would become a symbol of Caucasian Muslim resistance to Russian imperialism in the late 18th century.

title: Death of Sultan Mahmud I of the Ottoman Empire
description: Ottoman Sultan Mahmud I died after an 18-year reign marked by wars with Persia and Austria. He was succeeded by his brother Osman III, continuing the Ottoman practice of lateral succession.
sources: Stanford Shaw, History of the Ottoman Empire
outcome: His death led to a brief reign by Osman III before Mustafa III took power.

title: Deaths in 1757 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1757 CE: Bulleh Shah — poet (Bulhe Shah — Voice of Punjab's Soul) in Uch Sharif / Kasur, Pakistan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Battle of Plassey — British defeat Nawab of Bengal
description: Robert Clive's British East India Company forces defeat Nawab Siraj ud-Daulah of Bengal through military action and conspiracy with Bengali nobles. This victory gives the British control over Bengal's wealth and begins colonial rule in India.
sources: Siyar al-Mutakhkharin by Ghulam Husain Tabatabai
outcome: The battle marked the beginning of British colonial domination of the Indian subcontinent.

title: Battle of Plassey — Siraj ud-Daulah defeated by British and Mir Jafar
description: Nawab Siraj ud-Daulah of Bengal was defeated at Plassey by British East India Company forces under Robert Clive, aided by the betrayal of Mir Jafar. The battle established British dominance in Bengal and marked the beginning of colonial rule in India.
sources: Bhuvan Lall, The Lost Journal of Plassey
outcome: The battle enabled the East India Company to control Bengal's wealth and laid the foundation for British colonial rule over the Indian subcontinent.

title: Osman III becomes Ottoman Sultan
description: Osman III ascended to the Ottoman throne following the death of his brother Mahmud I. His brief three-year reign was marked by conservatism and limited engagement with European powers.
outcome: His conservative policies briefly interrupted Ottoman reform efforts.

title: Mustafa III becomes Ottoman Sultan
description: Mustafa III ascended to the Ottoman throne following the death of Osman III. His reign would see renewed Ottoman military efforts against Russia and Austria, as well as attempts at administrative reform.
sources: Stanford Shaw, History of the Ottoman Empire
outcome: Mustafa III reinvigorated Ottoman military and administrative institutions.

title: Ahmad ibn Idris al-Fasi born in Morocco
description: Ahmad ibn Idris al-Fasi, future founder of the Idrisi Sufi order and major Islamic reformer, was born in Maysur near Fez. His later teachings would emphasize direct connection to the Prophet Muhammad and reform of Sufi practices, profoundly influencing 19th-century Islamic revival movements across North and East Africa.
sources: R.S. O'Fahey, Enigmatic Saint: Ahmad Ibn Idris
John Voll, Islam: Continuity and Change in the Modern World
outcome: Ibn Idris would later establish networks that spawned multiple reform-oriented Sufi orders including the Sanusiyya and Khatmiyya.

title: Third Battle of Panipat — Durranis defeat Marathas
description: Ahmad Shah Durrani leads Afghan and allied Muslim forces to defeat the Maratha Confederacy in one of the 18th century's largest battles. Over 100,000 casualties are reported in this decisive engagement.
outcome: The battle temporarily halted Maratha expansion but left North India politically fragmented and vulnerable to British conquest.

title: Deaths in 1762 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1762 CE: Shah Waliullah — reformer (Muhaddith of Delhi — Qutb al-Zaman) in Delhi, India.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai's Shah Jo Risalo established
description: The spiritual poetry collection Shah Jo Risalo by the Sindhi Sufi poet Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai becomes established as the supreme literary and spiritual work of Sindhi civilisation after his death in 1752.

figures.name: Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai
figures.role: Sindhi Sufi poet; author of the Shah Jo Risalo

sources: H.T. Sorley, Shah Abdul Latif of Bhit
outcome: Established the greatest work of Sindhi literature and a masterpiece of Sufi poetry

title: Ghazi ud-Din Haidar becomes Nawab of Awadh
description: Following the death of Safdar Jang, Shuja-ud-Daula consolidates power as Nawab of Awadh, maintaining one of the last powerful Muslim kingdoms in India. Awadh becomes a center of Urdu literature and Islamic culture.
outcome: Awadh remained an important center of Islamic culture until British annexation in 1856.

title: Death of Shah Waliullah Dehlawi
description: Shah Waliullah Dehlawi, the influential Islamic scholar and reformer, died in Delhi after completing major theological works including the first complete Persian translation of the Quran. His sons continued his intellectual legacy.
sources: Marcia Hermansen, The Conclusive Argument from God
outcome: Shah Waliullah's theological synthesis and reform ideas influenced subsequent Islamic movements across South Asia including the Deobandi school.

title: Death of Nasir al-Din al-Albani
description: The Albanian Ottoman scholar and hadith expert Nasir al-Din al-Albani died in Damascus after a career of teaching Islamic sciences. He was particularly noted for his work in hadith criticism and Islamic jurisprudence.
sources: Jonathan Berkey, The Formation of Islam
outcome: His scholarship contributed to Ottoman Islamic learning in the 18th century.

title: Battle of Buxar — British defeat Mughal Emperor and Bengal coalition
description: British East India Company forces under Hector Munro decisively defeated a coalition of Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II, Nawab of Awadh Shuja-ud-Daula, and Mir Qasim of Bengal at Buxar. The battle consolidated British control over Bengal and reduced the Mughal emperor to dependency.
sources: P.J. Marshall, Bengal: The British Bridgehead
outcome: The battle marked the effective end of Mughal sovereignty and established British hegemony over northern India.

title: Ottoman-Russian war results in Treaty of Ayaslı
description: Following conflict in Poland, the Ottoman Empire and Russia negotiate the Treaty of Ayaslı, temporarily resolving tensions over Polish affairs. However, it sets the stage for the larger Russo-Turkish War of 1768-1774.
outcome: The treaty proved short-lived as Russian expansionism continued to threaten Ottoman territories.

title: Ali Bey al-Kabir begins consolidating power in Egypt
description: Ali Bey al-Kabir, a Mamluk leader, consolidated control over Egypt and began asserting independence from Ottoman authority. He expelled the Ottoman governor and minted coins in his own name.
sources: Daniel Crecelius, The Roots of Modern Egypt
outcome: Ali Bey's brief period of autonomy presaged the later independence movements under Muhammad Ali Pasha in the 19th century.

title: Treaty of Allahabad imposed on Mughal Emperor
description: The British East India Company forced Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II to grant the Diwani (revenue collection rights) of Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa. This effectively transferred economic control of these provinces from the Mughal Empire to British hands.
outcome: The treaty marked a decisive shift of power from the Mughals to the British in India.

title: Deaths in 1766 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1766 CE: Hazin Lahiji — poet (Persian poet who migrated to India after the fall of the Safavids) in Isfahan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Ali Bey al-Kabir declares Egyptian independence from Ottomans
description: Mamluk leader Ali Bey al-Kabir proclaims Egyptian autonomy from the Ottoman Empire and mints coins in his own name. He briefly controls Egypt, Syria, and the Hejaz before being defeated by Ottoman forces.
outcome: Though short-lived, Ali Bey's rebellion foreshadowed Egypt's eventual autonomy under Muhammad Ali.

title: Russo-Turkish War begins over Polish and Crimean issues
description: The Russo-Turkish War erupted when Ottoman Sultan Mustafa III declared war on Catherine the Great's Russia over Polish affairs and Russian interference in the Crimean Khanate. The war would last until 1774 with devastating results for the Ottomans.
outcome: The war ended in Ottoman defeat and the Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca, which gave Russia significant concessions including rights to protect Ottoman Orthodox Christians.

title: Ali Bey al-Kabir mints coins in his own name
description: Egyptian Mamluk leader Ali Bey al-Kabir began minting coins bearing his own name rather than the Ottoman Sultan's, asserting de facto independence. He also ceased sending tribute to Constantinople, directly challenging Ottoman authority.
outcome: This act demonstrated the weakening of Ottoman control over Egypt.

title: Ali Bey al-Kabir occupies Hijaz and controls holy cities
description: Egyptian Mamluk leader Ali Bey al-Kabir extended his authority by sending forces that occupied Mecca and Medina, asserting control over the Hijaz region and challenging Ottoman sovereignty. He appointed his own officials and had his name mentioned in the Friday khutba in the holy cities.
sources: Daniel Crecelius, The Roots of Modern Egypt
André Raymond, Artisans et Commerçants au Caire
outcome: Ali Bey's brief control of the holy cities represented the high point of his autonomous rule and direct challenge to Ottoman authority.

title: Ahmad al-Tijani founds the Tijaniyya Sufi order
description: Ahmad al-Tijani establishes the Tijaniyya order in North Africa, teaching a simplified path to spiritual realisation that will become one of the most widespread Sufi orders in West and North Africa.
outcome: Created one of the most influential Sufi orders in Africa, with millions of followers to this day

title: Battle of Chesma — Ottoman fleet destroyed
description: The Russian Baltic Fleet under Alexei Orlov destroyed the Ottoman fleet at Chesma during the Russo-Turkish War. This decisive naval victory gave Russia dominance in the Aegean Sea and threatened Ottoman control of the Mediterranean.
outcome: The Ottoman naval defeat contributed to the unfavorable terms of the Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca.

title: Waris Shah writes Heer Ranjha
description: The Punjabi Sufi poet Waris Shah completes his retelling of the folk love story Heer Ranjha, transforming it into a profound Sufi allegory that becomes the greatest work of Punjabi literature.

figures.name: Waris Shah
figures.role: Sufi poet; author of the definitive Heer Ranjha

outcome: Created the supreme masterpiece of Punjabi Sufi literature, still recited across Punjab today

title: Pugachev Rebellion affects Volga Muslims
description: Yemelyan Pugachev's Cossack rebellion against Catherine the Great attracts Bashkir Muslims and Tatars to join, promising religious freedom and end to Russian taxation. The rebellion is brutally suppressed by 1775.
outcome: The rebellion's failure intensified Russian control over Muslim populations in the Volga-Ural region.

title: Pugachev's Rebellion begins — affects Volga Muslims
description: Yemelyan Pugachev led a major Cossack and peasant rebellion against Catherine the Great that drew support from Volga Muslims, Bashkirs, and Tatars seeking relief from Russian oppression. The rebels captured several major towns before being defeated.
sources: John Alexander, Emperor of the Cossacks
outcome: The rebellion was brutally suppressed, but it highlighted Muslim discontent with Russian rule and led to increased Russification policies.

title: Abdul Hamid I becomes Ottoman Sultan
description: Abdul Hamid I ascended to the Ottoman throne following the death of his brother Mustafa III during the ongoing Russo-Turkish War. He inherited a war that would end unfavorably for the Ottomans the following year.
sources: Stanford Shaw, History of the Ottoman Empire
outcome: His reign dealt with the aftermath of military defeats and territorial losses to Russia.

title: Destruction of Timbuktu manuscripts by Moroccan forces
description: Forces from the Moroccan Sultan destroyed numerous Islamic manuscripts and libraries in Timbuktu following conflicts in the region. The attack severely damaged the city's scholarly infrastructure, though many manuscripts were hidden by local families and preserved.
sources: Elias Saad, Social History of Timbuktu
John Hunwick, The Writings of Central Sudanic Africa
outcome: Despite destruction, Timbuktu's manuscript tradition survived through family preservation, with thousands of documents rediscovered in the 20th century.

title: Treaty of Kucuk Kaynarca — Russia gains Muslim territory
description: The Ottoman Empire signs the Treaty of Kucuk Kaynarca with Russia, ceding Crimea and granting Russia the right to protect Orthodox Christians in Ottoman lands — the first major territorial loss to a Christian power.
outcome: Began the 'Eastern Question' that would dominate European diplomacy and Ottoman decline for 150 years

title: Timbuktu's Ahmad al-Bakkay writes Islamic legal treatises
description: West African scholar Ahmad al-Bakkay al-Kunti produces important works on Maliki jurisprudence and Sufi practice in Timbuktu. His scholarly output reinforces Timbuktu's status as a major center of Islamic learning.
outcome: Al-Bakkay's scholarship preserved and transmitted Islamic knowledge in West Africa during the pre-colonial period.

title: Death of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab
description: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, founder of the Wahhabi reform movement, died in Dir'iyyah. His alliance with the House of Saud had created a powerful religio-political movement that would shape Arabian Peninsula history.
outcome: His teachings continued to guide the Saudi state and influenced Islamic reform movements globally.

title: Death of Karim Khan Zand — Persia descends into civil war
description: Karim Khan Zand died in Shiraz, ending his benevolent rule over Persia. His death sparked succession conflicts among his sons and enabled the rise of the Qajar dynasty under Agha Mohammad Khan.
outcome: The collapse of Zand authority allowed the Qajars to reunify Persia and establish a dynasty that would rule until 1925.

title: Deaths in 1780 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1780 CE: Ibrahim Haqqi Erzurumi — scholar (Erzurumlu — the Anatolian Sage) in Erzurum / Tillo, Anatolia.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Tipu Sultan becomes ruler of Mysore
description: Following his father Hyder Ali's death, Tipu Sultan assumes control of the Kingdom of Mysore and continues resistance against British expansion in South India. He modernizes his army and administration while maintaining Islamic governance.
outcome: Tipu became a symbol of Muslim resistance to British colonialism before his death in 1799.

title: Deaths in 1781 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1781 CE: Mirza Mazhar Jan-e-Janan — Sufi mystic (Shahid of Delhi — Teacher of Waliullah's Circle) in Delhi, India; Sauda — poet (Father of Urdu Satirical Poetry — Greatest Urdu Satirist) in Delhi / Lucknow, India.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Death of Muhammad ibn Saud
description: Muhammad ibn Saud, founder of the First Saudi State and ally of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, died in Dir'iyyah. His son Abdul-Aziz succeeded him, continuing the expansion of Saudi-Wahhabi control in Arabia.
sources: Alexei Vassiliev, The History of Saudi Arabia
outcome: His death marked a transition but not interruption in Saudi state expansion.

title: Deaths in 1782 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1782 CE: Magtymguly Pyragy — poet (National poet of Turkmenistan and Turkmen Sufi verse master) in Hajji Qushan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Treaty of Constantinople — Ottomans recognize Russian annexation of Crimea
description: The Ottoman Empire formally recognizes Russian annexation of the Crimean Khanate, ending centuries of Crimean Tatar independence under Ottoman protection. This represents a major loss of Muslim territory and population.
outcome: The loss of Crimea marked a turning point in Ottoman decline and Russian expansion into Muslim lands.

title: Baba Bagi Murad establishes khanqah in Lahore
description: Qadiri Sufi master Baba Bagi Murad established an influential khanqah in Lahore that became a center for Punjabi Sufi poetry and music. His disciples included poets who composed in Punjabi and Persian, contributing to the region's syncretic devotional traditions.
sources: J.L. Khaleeq Ahmad, The Qadiri Order
outcome: The khanqah preserved and promoted Punjabi Sufi literary traditions during a period of political fragmentation in the Punjab.

title: Deaths in 1785 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1785 CE: Khwaja Mir Dard — poet (Dard — The Wound of Delhi) in Delhi, India.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Sultan Sidi Mohammed ben Abdallah signs treaty with United States
description: Morocco's Sultan Sidi Mohammed ben Abdallah signed a Treaty of Peace and Friendship with the United States, making Morocco the first nation to recognize American independence. The treaty established commercial relations and remains in force.
sources: James Wilson, Morocco Under Colonial Rule
outcome: The 1786 treaty established diplomatic and commercial relations that continue to make it America's longest unbroken treaty relationship.

title: Ma Mingxin leads Hui Muslim rebellion in China
description: Sufi shaykh Ma Mingxin led a Hui Muslim uprising against Qing dynasty authorities in Gansu province, sparked by religious persecution and ethnic discrimination. The rebellion was eventually suppressed with great violence by Qing forces.
sources: Jonathan Lipman, Familiar Strangers: A History of Muslims in Northwest China
outcome: The rebellion and its suppression intensified tensions between Hui Muslims and the Qing state.

title: Deaths in 1786 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1786 CE: Azad Bilgrami — scholar (Indian polymath and scholar of Arabic, Persian, and Urdu literature) in Bilgram.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Abdülhamid I establishes Humbarahane
description: Sultan Abdülhamid I founded the Humbarahane (Artillery Foundry) and associated military engineering school in Constantinople. This institution aimed to modernize Ottoman artillery production using European techniques.
outcome: The institution represented early Ottoman military modernization efforts before the Nizam-i Cedid.

title: Uthman dan Fodio begins preaching reform in Hausaland
description: Fulani Islamic scholar Uthman dan Fodio begins preaching religious reform and criticizing the Hausa rulers for mixing Islam with traditional practices. His teachings attract a large following and lay groundwork for future jihad.
outcome: His reform movement eventually led to the Fulani Jihad and establishment of the Sokoto Caliphate.

title: Sheikh Mansur leads Chechen uprising against Russia
description: Sheikh Mansur, a Naqshbandi Sufi leader, launched a holy war (gazavat) against Russian expansion in the Caucasus, uniting Chechen and Dagestani fighters. He achieved initial victories before Russian forces overwhelmed the resistance.
sources: Moshe Gammer, Muslim Resistance to the Tsar
outcome: Though eventually captured in 1791, Sheikh Mansur became a symbol of Caucasian Muslim resistance and inspired later movements including Imam Shamil's.

title: Abdul Rahman al-Jabarti begins writing his chronicle
description: Egyptian scholar and historian Abdul Rahman al-Jabarti began composing his monumental chronicle 'Aja'ib al-Athar fi'l-Tarajim wa'l-Akhbar', documenting contemporary Egyptian history. His eyewitness accounts would become the most important Arabic source for late 18th-century Egypt, including the French occupation.
sources: Jane Hathaway, The Politics of Households in Ottoman Egypt
outcome: Al-Jabarti's chronicle became the definitive Arabic historical source for the transition from Mamluk to modern Egypt.

title: Death of Abdul Hamid I during Austro-Ottoman War
description: Ottoman Sultan Abdul Hamid I died while the empire was engaged in another costly war against Austria and Russia. He was succeeded by his nephew Selim III, who would initiate comprehensive military reforms.
sources: Stanford Shaw, Between Old and New
outcome: His death paved the way for Selim III's ambitious reform program.

title: Selim III becomes Ottoman Sultan and begins Nizam-i Cedid reforms
description: Selim III ascended the Ottoman throne and immediately began the Nizam-i Cedid (New Order) reforms to modernize the military and administration. He established new military units trained in European methods to bypass Janissary resistance.
sources: Stanford Shaw, Between Old and New
outcome: Selim's reforms represented the first systematic Ottoman modernization attempt, though Janissary opposition eventually led to his overthrow in 1807.

title: Deaths in 1791 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1791 CE: Gelenbevi Ismail Efendi — scientist (Ottoman Master of Mathematics) in Gelenbe / Istanbul, Ottoman Empire.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Qianlong Emperor's military campaigns against Muslim Xinjiang
description: The Qing Dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor suppresses Muslim uprisings in Xinjiang, consolidating Chinese control over East Turkestan. The campaigns result in significant casualties among Turkic Muslim populations.
outcome: The Qing consolidation of Xinjiang intensified Chinese control over Muslim Turkic populations that continues today.

title: Treaty of Jassy ends Russo-Ottoman War
description: The Treaty of Jassy ended the Russo-Ottoman War of 1787-1792, with the Ottoman Empire ceding additional Black Sea territories to Russia. The treaty confirmed Russian control over the northern Black Sea coast and Crimea.
outcome: The treaty represented another territorial loss and spurred Ottoman reform efforts.

title: Deaths in 1792 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1792 CE: Ibn Abd al-Wahhab — reformer (Shaykh al-Islam of Najd) in al-Uyayna / Diriyya, Arabia.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Treaty of Seringapatam — Tipu Sultan cedes territory to British
description: After the Third Anglo-Mysore War, Tipu Sultan is forced to sign the Treaty of Seringapatam, surrendering half his kingdom and paying a large indemnity to the British East India Company. His two sons are taken as hostages.
outcome: The treaty severely weakened Mysore and set the stage for Tipu's final defeat in 1799.

title: Death of Tipu Sultan's father Hyder Ali — Tipu consolidates Mysore
description: Hyder Ali, the Muslim ruler of Mysore who had resisted British expansion in South India, died during the Second Anglo-Mysore War. His son Tipu Sultan succeeded him and continued military resistance while implementing administrative reforms.
sources: Kate Brittlebank, Tipu Sultan's Search for Legitimacy
outcome: Tipu Sultan inherited his father's struggle against British expansion and would resist until his death in 1799.

title: Sultan Agung Tirtayasa mosque completed in Banten
description: The Grand Mosque of Banten (Masjid Agung Banten) was significantly expanded and renovated during the reign of Sultan Abu Nashar Abdul Kahhar, becoming one of Java's most important Islamic architectural monuments. The mosque featured distinctive Javanese-Islamic architectural elements including a multi-tiered roof and octagonal minaret.
sources: Merle Ricklefs, A History of Modern Indonesia
Uka Tjandrasasmita, Islamic Architecture in Indonesia
outcome: The mosque exemplified the synthesis of indigenous Javanese and Islamic architectural traditions that characterized Southeast Asian Muslim societies.

title: Deaths in 1797 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1797 CE: Abdu Al Rahim Damavandi — philosopher (Philosopher).
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1798 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1798 CE: Waris Shah — poet (Waris Shah — Author of the Immortal Heer Ranjha) in Jandiala Sherkhan, Punjab.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Napoleon invades Egypt — the modern era begins
description: Napoleon Bonaparte lands in Egypt with 36,000 troops and a team of scholars, defeating the Mamluk cavalry at the Battle of the Pyramids. The expedition shocks the Muslim world and introduces modern European science and institutions to the Middle East.
sources: Al-Jabarti, Ajaib al-Athar fi al-Tarajim wa al-Akhbar
outcome: Inaugurated the modern era for the Muslim world — demonstrating European technological supremacy and triggering reform movements

title: Mawlay Sulayman becomes Sultan of Morocco
description: Mawlay Sulayman assumes the Moroccan throne and implements religious reforms influenced by Wahhabi ideas, closing Sufi lodges and promoting scriptural Islam. His policies create tensions with traditional Moroccan Sufi orders.
outcome: Mawlay Sulayman's reforms showcased tensions between reformist and traditional Islam across the Muslim world.

title: French expedition invades Ottoman Egypt
description: Napoleon Bonaparte led a French military expedition that invaded Ottoman Egypt, defeating Mamluk forces at the Battle of the Pyramids and occupying Cairo. The expedition included scholars who studied Egyptian antiquities and produced the Description de l'Égypte.
sources: Juan Cole, Napoleon's Egypt: Invading the Middle East
outcome: Though the French were expelled by 1801, the invasion shattered Mamluk power and exposed Ottoman weakness, ultimately enabling Muhammad Ali's rise.

title: French occupation of Malta — Knights expelled
description: Napoleon Bonaparte's expedition captured Malta from the Knights of St. John en route to Egypt, ending centuries of Christian military order rule. The island's Muslim slaves were freed by the French forces.
outcome: The capture eliminated a centuries-old bastion of anti-Muslim military activity in the Mediterranean.

title: Deaths in 1799 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1799 CE: Galib Dede — poet (Last Great Classical Ottoman Poet — Hüsn ü Aşk) in Istanbul, Ottoman Empire; Tipu Sultan — warrior (Sher-e-Mysore — Tiger of Mysore) in Mysore, India; Sheikh Ghalib — poet (Last great Ottoman divan poet and Mevlevi Sufi) in Istanbul.
outcome: 3 notable figures died this year.

title: Saudis capture Mecca — Wahhabi movement ascendant
description: The First Saudi State under Abdulaziz ibn Muhammad captures Mecca and imposes Wahhabi reforms on the holy city, destroying ornate shrines and tombs they consider idolatrous.

figures.name: Ibn Abd al-Wahhab
figures.role: founder of the Wahhabi movement whose teachings motivated the conquest (died 1792)

sources: Alexei Vassiliev, The History of Saudi Arabia
outcome: Demonstrated the revolutionary potential of Islamic reform and provoked an Ottoman military response

title: Shah Abdul Aziz issues fatwa declaring India Dar al-Harb
description: Shah Abdul Aziz, son of Shah Waliullah, issued a controversial fatwa declaring British-ruled India as Dar al-Harb (abode of war) rather than Dar al-Islam. This legal opinion influenced subsequent anti-colonial Islamic movements in the subcontinent.
sources: Sayyid Athar Abbas Rizvi, Shah Abd al-Aziz
outcome: Provided theological justification for jihad against British colonial rule in India.

title: Shah Waliullah's son Shah Abdul Aziz leads Delhi's Madrasa Rahimiyya
description: Shah Abdul Aziz, son of Shah Waliullah, consolidated the Madrasa Rahimiyya in Delhi as a center of Islamic learning and hadith studies. His scholarship bridged traditional Islamic sciences with contemporary challenges facing Indian Muslims under British expansion.
sources: Barbara Metcalf, Islamic Revival in British India
outcome: The madrasa became a fountainhead of Islamic reform movements in South Asia.

title: Shah Shuja ascends Afghan throne
description: Shah Shuja al-Mulk became ruler of the Durrani Empire in Afghanistan after deposing his half-brother Shah Mahmud. His turbulent reign would be marked by internal conflicts and ultimately exile, setting the stage for decades of instability.
sources: Mountstuart Elphinstone, An Account of the Kingdom of Caubul
outcome: His contested rule contributed to Afghan political fragmentation that lasted throughout the century.

title: Second Wahhabi-Ottoman War begins
description: Saudi-Wahhabi forces under Saud ibn Abd al-Aziz expanded their control over central Arabia, threatening Ottoman authority in the Hijaz. The Wahhabis began raiding into Iraq and Syria, prompting Ottoman concerns about the holy cities.
sources: Alexei Vassiliev, The History of Saudi Arabia
outcome: Wahhabi expansion eventually prompted Muhammad Ali Pasha's campaign to reconquer the Hijaz for the Ottomans.

title: Usman dan Fodio declares jihad — Sokoto Caliphate founded
description: The Fulani scholar Usman dan Fodio declares a jihad against the Hausa rulers of northern Nigeria, establishing the Sokoto Caliphate — the largest state in 19th-century Africa and the most successful Islamic reform movement of its era.

figures.name: Usman dan Fodio
figures.role: scholar-reformer; founded the Sokoto Caliphate through jihad

figures.name: Nana Asma'u
figures.role: his daughter; poet, educator and leader of women's education

figures.name: Muhammadu Bello
figures.role: his son and first Sultan of Sokoto

outcome: Created the largest state in 19th-century Africa and a model for Islamic governance and women's education

title: First Serbian Uprising against Ottoman rule
description: Karađorđe Petrović led Serbian rebels in armed revolt against Ottoman authority in the Belgrade Pashalik. The uprising was initially sparked by abuses of renegade Janissaries but evolved into a broader struggle for autonomy.
sources: Leopold von Ranke, The History of Servia
outcome: Though eventually suppressed, it paved the way for Serbian autonomy and the eventual independence of Balkan Christian territories from Ottoman rule.

title: Muhammad Ali Pasha becomes Ottoman viceroy of Egypt
description: Muhammad Ali, an Albanian Ottoman officer, consolidated power in Egypt after the French evacuation and British withdrawal. He established a dynasty that would rule Egypt until 1952, initiating massive modernization programs.
sources: Khaled Fahmy, All the Pasha's Men
outcome: Founded the last great Egyptian dynasty and initiated Egypt's modern transformation.

title: Sokoto Caliphate expands through Battle of Alwasa
description: Forces of the Sokoto Caliphate under Usman dan Fodio's leadership defeated the Gobir army decisively at Alwasa. This victory consolidated Fulani control over Hausaland and secured the caliphate's expansion.
sources: Mervyn Hiskett, The Sword of Truth
outcome: Secured Sokoto Caliphate as West Africa's dominant Islamic state.

title: Deaths in 1809 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1809 CE: Molla Vali Vidadi — scholar (Mystic of Şəmkir) in Şəmkir.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1810 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1810 CE: Mir Taqi Mir — poet (God of Urdu Poetry — Khuda-e Sukhan) in Agra / Delhi / Lucknow.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1811 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1811 CE: Sidi al-Mukhtar al-Kunti — scholar (Shaykh al-Islam of the Sahara) in Timbuktu / Niger / Mauritania.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Muhammad Ali massacres Mamluks at Cairo Citadel
description: Muhammad Ali Pasha invited Mamluk leaders to a ceremony at Cairo Citadel, then ambushed and killed approximately 470 of them. This brutal act eliminated the Mamluks as a military and political force in Egypt.
sources: Afaf Lutfi al-Sayyid Marsot, Egypt in the Reign of Muhammad Ali
outcome: Ended Mamluk power in Egypt and centralized Muhammad Ali's authority.

title: Maluku spice trade disrupted by British-Dutch conflict
description: British forces temporarily seized control of the Maluku Islands from the Dutch during the Napoleonic Wars, disrupting centuries-old spice trading networks involving Muslim merchants. The occupation lasted until 1814 when the islands were returned to Dutch control.
sources: M.C. Ricklefs, A History of Modern Indonesia
outcome: Muslim merchant communities adapted to shifting colonial control of Southeast Asian trade routes.

title: Muhammad Ali establishes medical school in Cairo
description: Muhammad Ali Pasha founded the first modern medical school in Egypt at Abu Za'bal, staffed initially by French physicians. The institution marked the beginning of Western-style medical education in the Arab world.
sources: Khaled Fahmy, All the Pasha's Men
outcome: It initiated Egypt's medical modernization and trained the first generation of indigenous Egyptian physicians.

title: Deaths in 1813 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1813 CE: Beket Ata — Sufi mystic (Kazakh Sufi saint and scholar of the Mangystau region) in Mangystau.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1815 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1815 CE: Ahmad al-Tijani — founder (Khatam al-Awliya — Seal of the Saints) in Ain Madi, Algeria / Fez, Morocco.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1817 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1817 CE: Usman dan Fodio — reformer (Shehu — Mujaddid of West Africa) in Gobir / Sokoto, Nigeria.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Java War begins under Prince Diponegoro
description: Prince Diponegoro launched a major rebellion against Dutch colonial rule in Java, framing it as a jihad against infidel occupation. The five-year conflict mobilized Islamic scholars and peasants alike in the most costly war for the Dutch East Indies.
sources: Peter Carey, The Power of Prophecy
outcome: Despite ultimate defeat in 1830, the rebellion became a symbol of Islamic resistance to colonialism.

title: Cholera pandemic devastates Hajj pilgrims
description: A severe cholera outbreak swept through Mecca during the Hajj season, killing an estimated 20,000 pilgrims. The epidemic spread along pilgrimage routes back to India, Persia, and the Ottoman territories.
sources: John Slight, The British Empire and the Hajj
outcome: The pandemic intensified international health concerns about the Hajj and led to eventual quarantine regulations.

title: Stamford Raffles founds Singapore as British trading post
description: Thomas Stamford Raffles established Singapore as a British free port, creating a new hub that attracted Muslim merchants from across the Malay world and beyond. The settlement rapidly grew into a major entrepôt connecting Islamic trade networks with British imperial commerce.
sources: C.M. Turnbull, A History of Modern Singapore
outcome: Singapore became a critical node in Muslim trade and migration networks across Southeast Asia.

title: Greek War of Independence begins against Ottoman rule
description: Greek revolutionaries launched an uprising against Ottoman rule, beginning with the Peloponnese revolt. Sultan Mahmud II's response included controversial massacres that drew European intervention.
sources: Douglas Dakin, The Greek Struggle for Independence
outcome: Led to Greek independence in 1830 and marked beginning of Ottoman territorial losses in Europe.

title: Fath Ali Shah commissions Shahnameh manuscript
description: Qajar ruler Fath Ali Shah commissioned a lavishly illustrated copy of Firdawsi's Shahnameh, employing master calligraphers and miniaturists. The manuscript exemplified the Qajar artistic renaissance and royal patronage of Persian literary heritage.
sources: Layla S. Diba, Royal Persian Paintings
outcome: It became one of the finest examples of Qajar manuscript illumination and demonstrated continuity with classical Persian artistic traditions.

title: Deaths in 1822 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1822 CE: Al-Jabarti — scholar (Egyptian historian who chronicled Napoleon's invasion and Muhammad Ali's rise) in Cairo.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Ibrahim Pasha recaptures Mecca and Medina from Wahhabis
description: Ibrahim Pasha, son of Muhammad Ali, led Egyptian-Ottoman forces to recapture the Hijaz from the first Saudi state. The campaign ended with the capture of Diriyah and execution of Abdullah bin Saud.
sources: Madawi Al-Rasheed, A History of Saudi Arabia
outcome: Temporarily ended the first Saudi state and restored Ottoman control over holy cities.

title: Ottoman Empire recognizes Greek autonomy in limited regions
description: Following early Greek revolutionary successes, the Ottoman government offered limited autonomy to certain Greek regions while continuing military campaigns. This temporary diplomatic overture failed to halt the independence movement.
sources: William St. Clair, That Greece Might Still Be Free
outcome: The negotiations ultimately collapsed, leading to continued warfare and European intervention in the Greek cause.

title: Deaths in 1823 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1823 CE: Muhammad al-Arabi al-Darqawi — Sufi mystic (Shadhiliyya Mystic of Beni Zarwal) in Beni Zarwal.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1824 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1824 CE: Shah Abdul Aziz Dehlawi — scholar (Muhaddith of Delhi — Son and Successor of Shah Waliullah) in Delhi, India; Shah Ghulam Ali — scholar (Abdullah Shah Ghulam Ali — Reviver of Delhi Sufism) in Delhi, India.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Mahmud II destroys the Janissaries — Ottoman modernisation begins
description: Ottoman Sultan Mahmud II massacres the Janissary corps in the 'Auspicious Incident', abolishing the once-elite military force that had become a reactionary obstacle to reform, and begins building a modern European-style army.
outcome: Removed the main obstacle to Ottoman military modernisation and cleared the way for the Tanzimat reforms

title: Russia captures Yerevan from Qajar Persia
description: Russian forces under Ivan Paskevich captured the Persian city of Yerevan during the Russo-Persian War. This loss would be formalized in the Treaty of Turkmenchay, permanently ceding Caucasian territories.
outcome: Marked permanent Persian loss of the Caucasus to Russian Empire.

title: Padri War begins in Sumatra
description: Islamic reformers known as the Padri movement, influenced by Wahhabi teachings, fought against both Dutch colonial expansion and traditional Minangkabau customs in West Sumatra. The conflict lasted until 1837 with the Padri leader Tuanku Imam Bonjol captured by the Dutch.
sources: Christine Dobbin, Islamic Revivalism in a Changing Peasant Economy
outcome: The war represented an early clash between Islamic reformism and Dutch colonialism in Southeast Asia.

title: Treaty of Yandabo ends First Anglo-Burmese War
description: The treaty concluded conflict between the British East India Company and the Konbaung Dynasty of Burma, resulting in Burmese territorial losses. The war had direct implications for Muslim communities in Arakan and Tenasserim who came under British rule.
sources: Thant Myint-U, The Making of Modern Burma
outcome: Muslim populations in Arakan were incorporated into British India, fundamentally altering the region's political dynamics.

title: Russians capture Ganja from Qajar Persia
description: Russian forces under General Ivan Paskevich captured the strategic city of Ganja during the Russo-Persian War. The fall of this important Persian fortress city opened the way for further Russian advances into the Caucasus.
outcome: The loss contributed to Persia's humiliating defeat and the Treaty of Turkmenchay.

title: Deaths in 1827 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1827 CE: Khalid al-Baghdadi — reformer (Mawlana Khalid — Renewer of the Naqshbandiyya) in Sulaymaniyya / Istanbul / Damascus.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Treaty of Turkmenchay signed between Persia and Russia
description: Fath-Ali Shah signed the humiliating Treaty of Turkmenchay after Persian defeat, ceding Armenia and Azerbaijan to Russia. The treaty also granted Russia capitulatory rights and a massive indemnity payment.
sources: Abbas Amanat, Pivot of the Universe
outcome: Established Russian dominance in Caucasus and introduced extraterritorial rights in Persia.

title: France invades Algeria — Islamic colonisation begins
description: France invades and conquers Algeria, beginning 132 years of colonial rule. Amir Abd al-Qadir al-Jaza'iri leads the resistance, becoming a symbol of Muslim anti-colonial struggle.

figures.name: Abd al-Qadir al-Jaza'iri
figures.role: Algerian amir and Sufi scholar; led resistance against French colonisation

sources: John Kiser, Commander of the Faithful
outcome: Began the European colonisation of the Arab-Muslim heartland and galvanised Islamic resistance movements globally

title: Muhammad Ali Pasha establishes modern Egyptian printing press
description: Muhammad Ali Pasha founded the Bulaq Press in Cairo, which became the first major Arabic printing house in the Middle East producing both religious and secular texts. The press printed Qurans, classical Arabic works, and translations of European scientific texts.
sources: Ami Ayalon, The Press in the Arab Middle East
outcome: The press revolutionized the dissemination of knowledge and contributed to the Arab literary renaissance.

title: Deaths in 1831 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1831 CE: Sayyid Ahmad Barelvi — reformer (Imam Jihad — Leader of the Tariqa-i-Muhammadiyya) in Rae Bareli / NWFP / Balakot; Shah Ismail Shaheed — reformer (al-Shahid — Martyr of Balakot) in Delhi / Balakot, NWFP.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Sayyid Said moves Omani capital to Zanzibar
description: Sultan Sayyid Said bin Sultan of Oman permanently relocated his capital from Muscat to Zanzibar, recognizing the island's economic importance. This shift transformed Zanzibar into a major hub of Indian Ocean trade, particularly in cloves and slaves.
sources: Abdul Sheriff, Slaves, Spices and Ivory in Zanzibar
outcome: Established Zanzibar as the commercial center of the western Indian Ocean.

title: Battle of Konya — Ibrahim Pasha defeats Ottomans
description: Ibrahim Pasha's Egyptian forces decisively defeated the Ottoman army under Grand Vizier Reshid Pasha at Konya. The victory brought Egyptian forces within striking distance of Constantinople, threatening the Ottoman dynasty.
sources: F. Robert Hunter, Egypt Under the Khedives
outcome: Nearly toppled Ottoman Empire before European intervention forced Egyptian withdrawal.

title: Hajj pilgrimage devastated by cholera epidemic
description: A major cholera outbreak struck the Hajj pilgrimage, killing an estimated 20,000 pilgrims in Mecca and along the caravan routes. The epidemic spread along pilgrimage routes back to Egypt, Syria, Anatolia, and Persia, prompting early discussions of quarantine measures.
outcome: The outbreak initiated international health coordination efforts focused on the Hajj.

title: Sayyid Said establishes diplomatic relations with United States
description: Sultan Sayyid Said of Oman and Zanzibar signed a treaty of amity and commerce with the United States, becoming one of the first Arab rulers to establish formal diplomatic ties with America. The treaty facilitated American merchant activity in the Indian Ocean.
outcome: The treaty established lasting commercial relationships and American consular presence in Zanzibar.

title: Deaths in 1834 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1834 CE: Al-Shawkani — scholar (Yemeni jurist who rejected taqlid and championed independent ijtihad) in Sanaa.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Muhammad Ali Pasha establishes School of Languages
description: Rifa'a al-Tahtawi founded the School of Languages (Madrasat al-Alsun) in Cairo under Muhammad Ali's patronage to train translators. The school became a center for translating European scientific and literary works into Arabic.
sources: Albert Hourani, Arabic Thought in the Liberal Age
outcome: The institution became instrumental in the Arab nahda (renaissance) and the modernization of Arabic language and thought.

title: Deaths in 1837 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1837 CE: Ahmad ibn Idris — Sufi mystic (Founder of the Idrisiyya — Architect of 19th-Century African Sufi Revival) in Fez / Mecca / Yemen; Muhammadu Bello — scholar (Sultan of Sokoto — Son of Usman dan Fodio) in Wurno, Sokoto, Nigeria.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Imam Shamil becomes leader of Caucasian resistance
description: Imam Shamil assumed leadership of the Dagestani and Chechen resistance against Russian expansion in the Caucasus. He unified mountain tribes under the Naqshbandi-influenced movement, establishing an imamate that resisted for 25 years.
sources: Moshe Gammer, Muslim Resistance to the Tsar
outcome: Led longest sustained Islamic resistance against Russian imperial expansion.

title: Kunta scholars mediate peace in West African jihad states
description: The Kunta family of Timbuktu, led by Sidi al-Mukhtar al-Kunti's successors, mediated conflicts between emerging jihad states in West Africa. Their Qadiri Sufi network provided diplomatic channels connecting the Sokoto Caliphate, Massina Empire, and other Islamic polities.
sources: Shamil Jeppie, The Meanings of Timbuktu
outcome: The Kunta network maintained scholarly and commercial ties across the Sahel despite political fragmentation.

title: Imam Shamil defeats Russians at Battle of Akhulgo
description: Imam Shamil led Dagestani and Chechen forces in defending the mountain fortress of Akhulgo against Russian siege for several months. Though ultimately forced to escape, his resistance became legendary in the Caucasian struggle.
sources: Moshe Gammer, Muslim Resistance to the Tsar
outcome: The prolonged resistance enhanced Shamil's reputation and inspired decades of continued Caucasian jihad against Russian expansion.

title: Qasim al-Ahmad establishes Sanusiyya order in Libya
description: Muhammad ibn Ali al-Sanusi founded the Sanusiyya Sufi order at Jaghbub in Cyrenaica after studying with Ahmad ibn Idris. The order established a network of zawiyas across North Africa and the Sahara, combining Sufism with educational and social reform.
sources: Knut S. Vikør, Sufi and Scholar on the Desert Edge
outcome: The Sanusiyya became a major political force in Libya and led resistance against Italian colonization.

title: Tanzimat reforms begin in the Ottoman Empire
description: Sultan Abdulmecid I issues the Hatt-i Sherif of Gulhane, inaugurating the Tanzimat (Reorganisation) era of legal and administrative modernisation, including equal rights for non-Muslim subjects.
sources: Erik Jan Zurcher, Turkey: A Modern History
outcome: Launched the most ambitious modernisation programme in the Muslim world, inspiring reformers from Cairo to Delhi

title: Sultanate of Aceh signs treaty with United States
description: Sultan Alauddin Mahmud Syah II of Aceh signed a treaty of friendship and commerce with the United States, represented by Edmund Roberts. This marked one of the earliest diplomatic agreements between an independent Islamic state and the United States.
sources: Anthony Reid, The Contest for North Sumatra
outcome: The treaty reflected Aceh's international standing as an independent Islamic sultanate before Dutch conquest.

title: British retreat from Kabul — Afghan victory
description: Afghan forces under Akbar Khan annihilated the British garrison retreating from Kabul, killing approximately 4,500 soldiers and 12,000 camp followers. Only one British survivor, Dr. William Brydon, reached Jalalabad to tell the tale.
sources: William Dalrymple, Return of a King
outcome: The disaster marked one of Britain's worst military defeats and ensured Afghan independence for decades.

title: Nasir al-Din Shah becomes Shah of Persia
description: Nasir al-Din Qajar ascended to the Persian throne at age seventeen following his father Muhammad Shah's death. His nearly fifty-year reign saw attempts at modernization, increasing European influence, and the tobacco protest that challenged royal authority.
sources: Abbas Amanat, Pivot of the Universe
outcome: His reign marked both Persian modernization efforts and growing crisis from European imperialism.

title: Amir Abd al-Qadir establishes Islamic state in Algeria
description: Abd al-Qadir consolidated an independent Islamic state in western Algeria, establishing administrative systems and modern military organization. His emirate represented sophisticated resistance to French colonial expansion.
sources: Raphael Danziger, Abd al-Qadir and the Algerians
outcome: Created model of Islamic governance and resistance to colonial occupation.

title: Sayyid Ahmad Barelvi's movement documented in Sirat-e-Mustaqim
description: The biography Sirat-e-Mustaqim recorded the life and teachings of Sayyid Ahmad Barelvi, who had died in 1831 fighting Sikhs at Balakot. His jihad movement and reformist theology profoundly influenced subsequent Islamic revivalism in South Asia.
sources: Barbara Metcalf, Islamic Revival in British India
outcome: Barelvi's legacy inspired both militant jihad movements and peaceful reform efforts.

title: British conquer Sindh — Talpur dynasty ends
description: General Charles Napier defeated the Talpur Mirs at the Battle of Miani, annexing Sindh to British India. The conquest ended the Muslim Talpur dynasty that had ruled Sindh since 1783.
sources: H.T. Lambrick, Sir Charles Napier and Sind
outcome: Sindh's annexation completed British control over the lower Indus and expanded colonial reach in Muslim South Asia.

title: Ahmad Bey abolishes slavery in Tunisia
description: Ahmad Bey of Tunis issued a decree abolishing slavery throughout the beylik, making Tunisia the first Muslim state to ban the institution. The reform preceded even the Ottoman Empire's gradual abolition efforts.
sources: Kenneth J. Perkins, A History of Modern Tunisia
outcome: The abolition demonstrated progressive reform in North Africa but faced resistance from slave-trading interests.

title: Deaths in 1847 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1847 CE: Abbasgulu Bakikhanov — philosopher (Philosopher of Əmircan, Baku) in Əmircan, Baku.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Abd al-Qadir surrenders to French after siege
description: After years of guerrilla warfare, Abd al-Qadir surrendered to French forces under General Lamoricière after being besieged. The French promised him safe passage to exile but initially imprisoned him.
sources: John Kiser, Commander of the Faithful
outcome: Ended organized Algerian resistance but Abd al-Qadir became symbol of Islamic anti-colonialism.

title: Hajj Umar Tal begins construction of Tijaniyya centers in West Africa
description: Al-Hajj Umar Tal, who had performed pilgrimage and studied in Arabia, returned to West Africa and established Tijaniyya Sufi lodges across Futa Jallon and Futa Toro. He built a network that would support his later jihad and state-building efforts.
sources: David Robinson, The Holy War of Umar Tal
outcome: The Tijaniyya became the dominant Sufi order in West Africa, rivaling the Qadiriyya.

title: Báb executed in Tabriz
description: Sayyid Ali Muhammad Shirazi, known as the Báb, was executed by firing squad in Tabriz on orders of the Qajar government for heresy. His movement had challenged Shi'a orthodoxy and attracted thousands of followers across Persia.
outcome: His execution sparked Babi uprisings and eventually led to the emergence of the Baha'i Faith.

title: Amir Kabir founds Dar al-Funun in Tehran
description: Prime Minister Amir Kabir established the Dar al-Funun polytechnic school to train Persians in modern sciences, military technology, and European languages. European instructors were brought to teach medicine, engineering, and military sciences.
sources: Shaul Bakhash, Iran: Monarchy, Bureaucracy and Reform
outcome: Dar al-Funun became Persia's first modern institution of higher education and model for educational reform.
