title: Hajj Umar Tal conquers Bambara kingdom of Kaarta
description: The Tijaniyya leader Hajj Umar Tal captured Kaarta in present-day Mali, expanding his jihad state. The conquest established Tijaniyya Sufi authority over previously animist and nominally Muslim Bambara territories.
sources: David Robinson, The Holy War of Umar Tal
outcome: The victory extended the Tijaniyya-led Toucouleur Empire and intensified Islamic conversion in the Western Sudan.

title: Hajj Umar Tal declares jihad in West Africa
description: Al-Hajj Umar Tal launched a jihad from Dinguiraye, conquering territories in present-day Guinea, Senegal, and Mali to establish the Toucouleur Empire. His Tijaniyya-based state challenged both non-Muslim kingdoms and the declining Bambara Empire.
sources: David Robinson, The Holy War of Umar Tal
outcome: The Toucouleur Empire became one of the major Islamic states in West Africa until French conquest.

title: Crimean War begins — Ottomans allied with European powers
description: Ottoman Empire entered war against Russia, allied with Britain and France over disputes concerning Christian holy sites. The conflict demonstrated Ottoman dependence on European powers and accelerated reform efforts.
outcome: Temporarily halted Russian expansion but exposed Ottoman military weakness.

title: Sayyid Barghash leads failed coup in Zanzibar
description: Prince Barghash bin Said attempted to overthrow his brother Sultan Majid and seize control of Zanzibar with support from conservative factions. British intervention defeated the coup and Barghash was exiled to Bombay for two years.
sources: Abdul Sheriff, Slaves, Spices and Ivory in Zanzibar
outcome: The failed coup demonstrated British power over Zanzibari succession and regional politics.

title: Zanzibar becomes world's largest clove producer
description: Under Sultan Majid bin Said, Zanzibar achieved dominance in global clove production using plantation agriculture. The island's economy, controlled by Omani Arab elites and Indian financiers, relied heavily on enslaved East African labor.
sources: Abdul Sheriff, Slaves, Spices and Ivory in Zanzibar
outcome: Zanzibar's clove wealth made it a major center of Swahili-Islamic culture and Indian Ocean trade.

title: Khiva khanate defeats Russian expedition
description: The Khanate of Khiva under Muhammad Amin Khan successfully repelled a Russian military expedition attempting to establish control over Central Asian trade routes. The victory temporarily preserved Khiva's independence.
sources: Seymour Becker, Russia's Protectorates in Central Asia
outcome: Despite this success, Russian pressure continued and Khiva eventually became a protectorate in 1873.

title: British abolish Mughal Empire after Indian Rebellion
description: After the failed Indian Rebellion of 1857, the British formally depose the last Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar and exile him to Rangoon, ending 332 years of Mughal rule and establishing direct British Crown rule over India.
outcome: Ended Islamic political sovereignty in India and traumatised the Muslim community, spurring reform movements

title: Indian Rebellion — Muslim and Hindu soldiers revolt
description: Sepoy mutiny in Meerut spread across north India, with Bahadur Shah II reluctantly declared leader by rebels. Muslim ulama like Maulana Liaquat Ali issued fatwas supporting jihad against British rule.
outcome: Led to formal end of Mughal Empire and direct British colonial rule.

title: Ottoman Land Code promulgated
description: The Ottoman Empire issued a comprehensive land code that aimed to clarify property rights and increase agricultural productivity. The law unintentionally facilitated the consolidation of large estates and weakened communal landholding practices common in rural areas.
sources: Haim Gerber, The Social Origins of the Modern Middle East
outcome: The code transformed Ottoman agrarian relations and concentrated landholding among urban elites.

title: Deaths in 1859 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1859 CE: Muhammad al-Sanusi — Sufi mystic (Founder of the Sanusiyya — Builder of the Libyan Islamic State) in Algeria / Mecca / Cyrenaica.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Imam Shamil surrenders to Russia
description: After 25 years of resistance, Imam Shamil surrendered to Russian forces at Gunib, ending the Caucasian Imamate. He was taken to Russia where he lived in honorable captivity until his death.
sources: Moshe Gammer, Muslim Resistance to the Tsar
outcome: His surrender marked the end of organized Islamic resistance in the Caucasus and Russian consolidation of the region.

title: Deaths in 1860 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1860 CE: Al-Bajuri — scholar (Shaykh al-Azhar of His Time) in Bajur / Cairo, Egypt.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Damascus massacre — sectarian violence against Christians
description: Druze-Christian conflict in Mount Lebanon escalated into massacre of Christians in Damascus, killing thousands. Abd al-Qadir, in exile, personally protected thousands of Christians, earning international recognition.
sources: Ussama Makdisi, The Culture of Sectarianism
outcome: Led to European intervention and Ottoman administrative reforms in Lebanon.

title: Bukhara defeats Kokand in Central Asian conflict
description: The Emirate of Bukhara under Muzaffar al-Din Khan defeated the neighboring Khanate of Kokand in territorial warfare. The victory demonstrated Bukhara's military dominance among Central Asian Muslim states on the eve of Russian conquest.
outcome: Inter-khanate conflicts weakened Central Asian unity against impending Russian imperial expansion.

title: Sayyid Barghash visits Britain and France
description: Sayyid Barghash bin Said, heir to the Zanzibar throne, traveled to Britain and France where he witnessed European industrialization. This experience shaped his later modernization efforts when he became Sultan in 1870.
sources: Abdul Sheriff, Slaves, Spices and Ivory in Zanzibar
outcome: Barghash later introduced telegraphs, piped water, and other reforms to Zanzibar.

title: Deaths in 1864 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1864 CE: Muhammad al-Futi — reformer (Al-Hajj Umar Tal — Jihad of the Pen and Sword) in Futa Toro / Guinea / Mali; Nana Asma'u — scholar (Pioneer of Mass Female Education in West Africa) in Sokoto, Nigeria.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Circassian deportation from Caucasus begins
description: Russian forces expelled hundreds of thousands of Muslim Circassians from their Caucasian homeland to the Ottoman Empire following the conclusion of the Caucasian War. An estimated 400,000 to 1 million Circassians were forcibly displaced.
outcome: The mass deportation constituted one of the 19th century's largest forced migrations and permanently altered the Caucasus' demographic landscape.

title: Deoband seminary founded — Islamic education reform
description: The Darul Uloom Deoband is founded in northern India to preserve Islamic learning and produce scholars after the fall of the Mughal Empire, becoming the most influential Islamic seminary in the Sunni world after Al-Azhar.
sources: Barbara Metcalf, Islamic Revival in British India
outcome: Created a new model of Islamic education that shaped religious thought across South and Central Asia

title: Yaqub Beg establishes Islamic state in Kashgaria
description: Yaqub Beg, a Kokandi military leader, established an independent Islamic state in Xinjiang after the collapse of Qing control. His Turkic Muslim emirate briefly gained recognition from Britain and the Ottoman Empire.
sources: Kim Hodong, Holy War in China
outcome: Created brief independent Muslim state in Central Asia before Qing reconquest.

title: Ismail Gaspirali born in Crimea
description: Ismail Gaspirali (Gasprinsky) was born in Crimea and would become a leading Muslim modernist advocating educational reform among Russian Muslims. His usul-i jadid (new method) schools and newspaper Terjuman promoted pan-Turkic unity and progressive Islam.
sources: Adeeb Khalid, The Politics of Muslim Cultural Reform
outcome: Gaspirali's movement transformed Muslim education across the Russian Empire.

title: Bukhara falls to Russian conquest
description: Russian forces under General Dmitry Romanovskii captured Samarkand and defeated the Emirate of Bukhara, forcing Emir Muzaffar al-Din to accept vassal status. Ancient centers of Islamic learning came under Russian imperial control.
sources: Alexander Morrison, Russian Rule in Samarkand
outcome: The conquest marked Russian domination over Central Asian Muslim khanates and their cultural centers.

title: Deaths in 1869 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1869 CE: Mirza Ghalib — poet (Asad — Last Great Mughal Poet) in Agra / Delhi, India.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Suez Canal opens — reshapes Muslim trade routes
description: The Suez Canal is completed and opens for navigation, dramatically reducing the sea journey between Europe and Asia and making Egypt a critical strategic asset, ultimately leading to British occupation.
outcome: Transformed the geopolitics of the Muslim world by making Egypt a linchpin of global trade and European imperial strategy

title: Baha'u'llah arrives in Ottoman Acre as prisoner
description: Baha'u'llah, founder of the Baha'i Faith and former Babi, was exiled by Ottoman authorities to the prison city of Acre. His arrival marked the final stage of his life where he composed major theological works.
sources: Moojan Momen, Baha'i Faith: A Short Introduction
outcome: Established Acre as Baha'i holy site and center of new religious movement emerging from Islam.

title: Maliki scholar Muhammad al-Amin al-Shinqiti completes Hajj journey
description: The renowned Mauritanian scholar Muhammad al-Amin al-Shinqiti completed an extraordinary fourteen-year walking pilgrimage from West Africa to Mecca. He taught Islamic sciences throughout his journey and settled in Medina where he became a prominent scholar.
outcome: Al-Shinqiti's scholarship connected West African Islamic learning to the Hijaz scholarly tradition.

title: Suez Canal transforms Hajj routes
description: Following the 1869 opening of the Suez Canal, Muslim pilgrims increasingly traveled to Mecca via steamship rather than overland caravan routes. This revolutionized the Hajj, making it more accessible but also spreading diseases more rapidly.
outcome: Steam travel democratized the Hajj while internationalizing health and security concerns.

title: Nasir al-Din Shah visits Europe
description: Qajar ruler Nasir al-Din Shah became the first Iranian monarch to visit Europe, traveling to Britain, France, Belgium, and Russia. His journey exposed him to European industrialization and inspired limited modernization efforts in Persia.
sources: Abbas Amanat, Pivot of the Universe
outcome: The visit influenced Persian modernization attempts though ultimately failed to prevent European economic domination.

title: Deaths in 1873 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1873 CE: Hadi Sabzavari — philosopher (Philosopher of Sabzevar) in Sabzevar; Rifa'a at-Tahtawi — reformer (Islamic Theology Reformer of Tahta) in Tahta.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Aceh War begins against Dutch invasion
description: The Dutch launched a major invasion of the Sultanate of Aceh in northern Sumatra, beginning a conflict that would last until 1904. Sultan Mahmud Syah and later resistance leaders waged guerrilla warfare, making it the longest and costliest colonial war for the Netherlands.
sources: Anthony Reid, The Contest for North Sumatra
outcome: Acehnese resistance became a symbol of Islamic opposition to European colonialism in Southeast Asia.

title: Dutch launch second expedition against Aceh
description: The Netherlands launched a major military offensive against the Sultanate of Aceh in northern Sumatra, escalating the Aceh War. Sultan Mahmud Syah led resistance that would continue for decades despite Dutch technological superiority.
sources: Anthony Reid, The Contest for North Sumatra
outcome: The protracted war became the costliest Dutch colonial campaign and demonstrated fierce Islamic resistance in Southeast Asia.

title: Aceh Sultan Mahmud Shah signs treaty with Ottoman Empire
description: Sultan Mahmud Shah of Aceh sought Ottoman support against Dutch aggression by requesting recognition as an Ottoman vassal. Sultan Abdulaziz responded positively, though the distance prevented meaningful military assistance.
sources: Anthony Reid, The Contest for North Sumatra
outcome: The diplomatic ties symbolized pan-Islamic solidarity but could not prevent Dutch conquest of Aceh.

title: Allama Iqbal born — future poet-philosopher of Islam
description: Muhammad Iqbal is born in Sialkot, Punjab. He will become the greatest Urdu and Persian poet of the 20th century and the intellectual father of Pakistan.

figures.name: Allama Iqbal
figures.role: born in Sialkot; would become the poet-philosopher of the Islamic revival

sources: Iqbal, The Reconstruction of Religious Thought in Islam
outcome: Would produce the philosophical vision for Islamic modernity and the intellectual foundation for Pakistan

title: Sultan Abdulhamid II ascends Ottoman throne
description: Abdulhamid II became sultan during a constitutional crisis, initially accepting constitutional reforms before suspending parliament in 1878. He ruled for 33 years, emphasizing Pan-Islamic ideology and centralization.
sources: François Georgeon, Abdülhamid II: Le sultan calife
outcome: Last sultan to exercise absolute power, promoted Pan-Islamism as unifying ideology.

title: Ottoman constitution promulgated
description: Sultan Abdulhamid II promulgated the Ottoman Empire's first constitution, establishing a parliament and guaranteeing subjects' rights regardless of religion. The parliament met briefly before the Sultan suspended it in 1878, ushering in his authoritarian era.
sources: Nader Sohrabi, Revolution and Constitutionalism in the Ottoman Empire
outcome: The short-lived constitution inspired later constitutional movements across the Muslim world.

title: Sultan Abdulaziz deposed and dies mysteriously
description: Ottoman Sultan Abdulaziz was deposed by reformist ministers led by Midhat Pasha and died days later in suspicious circumstances. His nephew Murad V briefly succeeded before being replaced by Abdulhamid II.
sources: Roderic Davison, Reform in the Ottoman Empire
outcome: The crisis precipitated constitutional reforms and marked the beginning of Abdulhamid II's authoritarian reign.

title: Syed Ameer Ali publishes The Spirit of Islam
description: Syed Ameer Ali, Indian jurist and modernist thinker, published his influential work presenting Islam to Western audiences as rational and progressive. The book became widely read among English-educated Muslims and Europeans.
sources: Aziz Ahmad, Islamic Modernism in India and Pakistan
outcome: Shaped modernist Islamic discourse and Western perceptions of Islam.

title: Congress of Berlin — Ottoman Empire loses territory
description: European powers met in Berlin to revise the Treaty of San Stefano after Russo-Turkish War, stripping Ottoman control over Bulgaria, Cyprus, Bosnia, and other territories. Sultan Abdulhamid II sent Grand Vizier as Ottoman representative.
sources: William Hagen, German History in Modern Times
outcome: Formalized Ottoman territorial losses and European imperial domination.

title: Jamal al-Din al-Afghani expelled from Egypt
description: The pan-Islamist activist Jamal al-Din al-Afghani is expelled from Egypt by the British-backed Khedive after years of political agitation, teaching and newspaper publishing that galvanised the Islamic reform movement.

figures.name: Jamal al-Din al-Afghani
figures.role: pan-Islamist activist and reformer; expelled for political agitation

sources: Nikki Keddie, An Islamic Response to Imperialism
outcome: Spread pan-Islamic ideas that influenced Muhammad Abduh, Rashid Rida and every subsequent Islamic reform movement

title: Muhammad Abduh exiled to Syria
description: Muhammad Abduh, Egyptian Islamic modernist, was exiled to Syria by Khedive Tawfiq for his association with the Urabi revolt. During exile, he collaborated with Jamal al-Din al-Afghani and deepened his reformist thought.
sources: Albert Hourani, Arabic Thought in the Liberal Age
outcome: Abduh's exile period shaped his later influential reforms of Islamic education and law.

title: Ahmad Urabi emerges as Egyptian nationalist leader
description: Colonel Ahmad Urabi led Egyptian military officers in demanding constitutional reforms and opposing Turco-Circassian dominance in the army. His movement combined nationalism with Islamic reformist ideas and challenged both Khedive Tewfik and European influence.
sources: Juan Cole, Colonialism and Revolution in the Middle East
outcome: Urabi's movement led to the 1882 British occupation of Egypt after his defeat at Tel el-Kebir.

title: Deaths in 1880 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1880 CE: Muhammad Qasim Nanautavi — founder (Co-founder of Darul Uloom Deoband) in Deoband, India.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Muhammad Ahmad declares himself the Mahdi in Sudan
description: Muhammad Ahmad ibn Abd Allah declares himself the Mahdi (guided one) in Sudan and launches a revolt against Egyptian-Ottoman and British rule, establishing a theocratic state.

location.modern: Aba Island, White Nile State, Sudan

sources: P.M. Holt, The Mahdist State in the Sudan
outcome: Created the only successful anti-colonial Islamic state of the 19th century, lasting until 1898

title: Muhammad Ahmad declares himself Mahdi in Sudan
description: Muhammad Ahmad ibn Abdallah declared himself the Mahdi (divinely guided one) on Aba Island, proclaiming a mission to purify Islam and expel Egyptian-Ottoman rule from Sudan. His movement rapidly gained followers among Sudanese tribes and Sufi orders.
sources: P.M. Holt, The Mahdist State in the Sudan
outcome: The Mahdist movement would conquer Khartoum and establish an Islamic state until 1898.

title: French establish protectorate over Tunisia
description: France forced the Bey of Tunis to sign the Treaty of Bardo, establishing a French protectorate over Tunisia. The occupation followed manufactured border incidents with Algeria and European financial pressure.
outcome: Tunisia became France's first North African protectorate, initiating the scramble for the remaining Ottoman territories.

title: British military occupation of Egypt
description: Britain occupies Egypt militarily to protect the Suez Canal and suppress the Urabi revolt, beginning a 72-year occupation that transforms Egyptian politics and culture.
sources: M.W. Daly, The Cambridge History of Egypt
outcome: Brought the most important Arab country under European control, galvanising the Islamic and nationalist reform movements

title: Deaths in 1883 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1883 CE: Abd al-Qadir al-Jaza'iri — Sufi mystic (al-Amir al-Kabir — The Great Emir) in Mascara / Damascus.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Berlin Conference partitions Africa
description: European powers met in Berlin to establish rules for colonizing Africa, dividing territories without African or Muslim representation. The conference formalized European claims to Muslim-majority territories across North, West, and East Africa.
sources: Thomas Pakenham, The Scramble for Africa
outcome: The conference accelerated European colonization of Muslim Africa, fragmenting historic Islamic polities.

title: Deaths in 1885 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1885 CE: Muhammad Ahmad (the Mahdi) — founder (Founder of the Sudanese Mahdiyya movement) in Omdurman, Sudan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Muhammad Abduh publishes reformist Quranic commentary
description: Muhammad Abduh, Grand Mufti of Egypt and student of al-Afghani, begins publishing his modernist Quranic exegesis and legal opinions that argue Islam is compatible with reason, science and modernity.

figures.name: Muhammad Abduh
figures.role: Grand Mufti of Egypt; pioneer of Islamic modernism

outcome: Founded the Islamic modernist school that shaped Muslim intellectual life for the next century

title: Mahdi forces capture Khartoum — Gordon killed
description: Muhammad Ahmad's Mahdist forces captured Khartoum after a prolonged siege, killing British General Charles Gordon. The victory secured the Mahdist state's control over Sudan until British reconquest in 1898.
sources: P.M. Holt, The Mahdist State in the Sudan
outcome: Established independent Mahdist Islamic state in Sudan for thirteen years.

title: Samori Ture establishes Wassoulou Empire
description: Samori Ture consolidated his Islamic state in West Africa, creating the Wassoulou Empire centered in present-day Guinea. He implemented Islamic law, organized a professional army, and manufactured weapons locally to resist French colonial expansion.
sources: Yves Person, Samori: une révolution Dyula
outcome: The Wassoulou Empire resisted French conquest until 1898, making it one of the longest anti-colonial struggles in West Africa.

title: Deaths in 1886 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1886 CE: Abd al-Hayy al-Lucknawi — scholar (Indian Hanafi jurist and hadith critic) in Lucknow.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1887 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1887 CE: Ahmad Faris al-Shidyaq — scholar (Pioneer of modern Arabic prose and journalism) in Istanbul.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1888 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1888 CE: Namik Kemal — scholar (Father of Ottoman Turkish Literature and Nationalism) in Tekirdağ / Istanbul / Famagusta.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Ahmadiyya movement founded by Mirza Ghulam Ahmad
description: Mirza Ghulam Ahmad founded the Ahmadiyya movement in Qadian, Punjab, claiming to be a prophet and the promised Mahdi. His theological claims, especially regarding prophethood after Muhammad, sparked intense controversy among mainstream Muslims.
outcome: The Ahmadiyya spread globally but faced persecution as heretical by many Muslim authorities.

title: Tobacco Protest in Qajar Persia — first mass movement
description: Jamal al-Din al-Afghani influenced Ayatollah Mirza Hassan Shirazi to issue a fatwa against tobacco use, protesting the shah's concession to British monopoly. Mass boycott forced cancellation of the concession.
sources: Nikki Keddie, Religion and Rebellion in Iran
outcome: First successful mass protest in modern Middle East, demonstrating clerical political power.

title: Young Tunisians movement emerges
description: Educated Tunisians formed a modernist movement advocating constitutional reform and civil rights under French protectorate rule. Led by figures like Ali Bash Hamba and Abd al-Aziz al-Thaalbi, they sought to reconcile Islamic identity with modern governance.
sources: Kenneth Perkins, A History of Modern Tunisia
outcome: The movement laid foundations for Tunisian nationalism and modern political activism.

title: Sayyid Jamal al-Din al-Afghani meets with Nasir al-Din Shah
description: The pan-Islamic activist Jamal al-Din al-Afghani met with Nasir al-Din Shah in Tehran but their relationship soured over political differences. Al-Afghani was subsequently expelled from Persia after criticizing the shah's tobacco concession to Britain.
sources: Nikki Keddie, Sayyid Jamal ad-Din al-Afghani
outcome: From exile, al-Afghani helped inspire the Tobacco Protest that demonstrated popular resistance to foreign concessions.

title: Deaths in 1892 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1892 CE: Muhammad Shams-ul-Haq Azimabadi — scholar (Muhammad Shams-ul-Haq Azeemabadi) in Azimabad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Hamidian massacres of Armenians begin
description: Sultan Abdulhamid II's forces, including irregular Hamidiye cavalry, conducted massacres of Armenian populations across eastern Anatolia. Between 100,000-300,000 Armenians were killed in waves of violence through 1896.
outcome: Marked escalation of Ottoman persecution of Armenians, foreshadowing 1915 genocide.

title: Mahdist State introduces new currency system
description: The Mahdist state in Sudan, under Khalifa Abdullahi, introduced its own currency system to replace Egyptian and Turkish coins. The new monetary system reflected the state's assertion of independence and Islamic governance separate from Ottoman and Egyptian influence.
sources: P.M. Holt, The Mahdist State in the Sudan
outcome: The currency symbolized Mahdist sovereignty but the state fell to Anglo-Egyptian conquest in 1898.

title: Deaths in 1895 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1895 CE: Chiragh Ali — scholar (Indian Muslim modernist and associate of Sir Syed Ahmad Khan) in Hyderabad.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1897 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1897 CE: Jamal al-Din al-Afghani — scholar (Father of Pan-Islamism and Islamic Modernism) in Asadabad / Tehran / Cairo / Istanbul.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Mirza Ghalib's Urdu ghazals established as canon
description: By the end of the 19th century, the ghazals of Mirza Ghalib (died 1869) are established as the supreme achievement of Urdu poetry, studied and recited across the Muslim world from Istanbul to Dhaka.

figures.name: Mirza Ghalib
figures.role: the greatest Urdu poet; his work became the canon of Urdu literature

sources: Ralph Russell, Ghalib: Life and Letters
outcome: Established the gold standard for Urdu poetry that continues to define the language's literary tradition

title: Muhammad Rashid Rida begins publishing Al-Manar
description: Muhammad Rashid Rida, Syrian Islamic scholar and reformer, began publishing Al-Manar journal in Cairo. The periodical became the leading voice of Salafi modernism, reaching readers across the Muslim world.
sources: Henri Laoust, Le reformisme orthodoxe des Salafiya
outcome: Shaped modern Salafi thought and Islamic reformist discourse.

title: First Zionist Congress impacts Ottoman Palestine
description: Theodor Herzl convened the First Zionist Congress in Basel, formally launching political Zionism's goal of establishing a Jewish homeland in Ottoman Palestine. Ottoman authorities and Arab Muslim leaders began expressing concerns about Zionist land purchases and immigration.
outcome: The congress initiated organized Jewish immigration that would fundamentally transform Palestine.

title: Deaths in 1898 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1898 CE: Sayyid Ahmad Khan — scholar (Sir Syed — Father of Muslim Modernism in South Asia) in Delhi / Aligarh, British India.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1899 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1899 CE: Haji Imdadullah Muhajir Makki — Sufi mystic (Spiritual Father of Both Deoband and Barelvi) in Nanota / Mecca.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1901 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1901 CE: Khawaja Ghulam Farid — poet (Khawaja Sahib — Voice of the Cholistan Desert) in Chachran Sharif, Cholistan, Punjab.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Abdulhamid II completes the Hejaz Railway foundation
description: Ottoman Sultan Abdulhamid II launched the Hejaz Railway project to connect Damascus with Medina, facilitating pilgrimage and strengthening Ottoman control. The railway was financed through donations from Muslims worldwide and represented the last major Ottoman infrastructure achievement.
sources: Jacob Landau, The Hejaz Railway and the Muslim Pilgrimage
outcome: The railway operated until World War I when Arab forces destroyed sections during the revolt, and it was never fully restored.

title: Maktab Anbar school opens in Damascus
description: Ahmad Munif Pasha al-Aydid establishes Maktab Anbar, the first modern secondary school in Damascus, offering both traditional Islamic sciences and modern European curriculum. The school became a model for educational reform throughout Ottoman Syria and trained future nationalist leaders.
sources: Philip Khoury, Syria and the French Mandate
outcome: The school became a training ground for Arab nationalist intellectuals and officials.

title: Deaths in 1902 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1902 CE: Al-Kawakibi — scholar (Pioneer of Arab Nationalism and Anti-Despotism) in Aleppo / Cairo; Abd al-Rahman al-Kawakibi — philosopher (Philosopher of Aleppo) in Aleppo.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Deaths in 1905 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1905 CE: Muhammad Abduh — scholar (Grand Mufti of Egypt — Father of Islamic Modernism) in Mahallat Nasr / Cairo / Paris; Syed Ahmad Kabir Rifai — Sufi mystic (Qutb al-Zaman of Bengal) in Bengal, India.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Ismail Gaspirali convenes All-Russian Muslim Congress
description: Crimean Tatar intellectual Ismail Gaspirali organizes the first All-Russian Muslim Congress in Saint Petersburg to address educational and political reforms for Muslim populations under Tsarist rule. The congress established networks that would shape Muslim political consciousness across the Russian Empire.
sources: Adeeb Khalid, The Politics of Muslim Cultural Reform
outcome: Laid foundations for Muslim political organization in Russia prior to the Bolshevik Revolution.

title: Deaths in 1906 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1906 CE: Ibrahim al-Yaziji — philosopher (Nahda Philosopher of Beirut) in Beirut.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: All India Muslim League founded
description: Muslim leaders in British India found the All India Muslim League in Dhaka to protect Muslim political interests, eventually becoming the vehicle for the creation of Pakistan.
outcome: Created the political party that would demand and achieve a separate Muslim state in South Asia

title: Dinshaway Incident sparks Egyptian nationalism
description: British colonial officers killed pigeons belonging to Egyptian villagers in Dinshaway, leading to a clash in which an officer died. The subsequent harsh sentences and public executions of Egyptian villagers galvanized anti-colonial sentiment across Egypt.
sources: Peter Mansfield, The British in Egypt
outcome: The incident became a turning point in Egyptian nationalism and resistance to British occupation.

title: Deaths in 1908 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1908 CE: Qasim Amin — scholar (Pioneer of Arab Feminism — Liberation of Women) in Alexandria / Cairo, Egypt; Mirza Ghulam Ahmad — founder (Founder of the Ahmadiyya movement) in Qadian, Punjab, India.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Young Turk Revolution restores Ottoman constitution
description: The Young Turks (Committee of Union and Progress) force Sultan Abdulhamid II to restore the 1876 constitution, beginning the final phase of Ottoman modernisation that will end with the empire's dissolution.

figures.name: Namik Kemal
figures.role: intellectual forefather of the Young Turk movement (died 1888)

sources: Erik Jan Zurcher, The Unionist Factor
outcome: Inaugurated the constitutional era that led to the abolition of the Ottoman sultanate and caliphate

title: Italy invades Ottoman Libya
description: Italy launched an invasion of Ottoman Tripolitania and Cyrenaica, beginning the Italo-Turkish War. Ottoman officers including Mustafa Kemal and Enver Pasha organized Arab and Berber resistance, though the Ottomans were forced to cede Libya in 1912.
sources: E.E. Evans-Pritchard, The Sanusi of Cyrenaica
outcome: Ottoman loss of Libya marked the beginning of Italian colonial rule and inspired continued Sanusi resistance led by Omar Mukhtar.

title: Agadir Crisis escalates Franco-German tensions over Morocco
description: Germany sent the gunboat SMS Panther to Agadir in Morocco, challenging French colonial expansion and sparking an international crisis. The incident forced Sultan Abd al-Hafid to concede more territory to France, weakening Moroccan sovereignty.
sources: Eugene Rogan, The Arabs: A History
outcome: Morocco became a French protectorate in 1912, ending centuries of independent Muslim rule.

title: Muhammadiyah movement founded in Java
description: Ahmad Dahlan established Muhammadiyah in Yogyakarta as a modernist Islamic reform movement. The organization sought to purify Islamic practice and promote education, social welfare, and women's participation.
sources: Deliar Noer, The Modernist Muslim Movement in Indonesia
outcome: Muhammadiyah became one of the largest Islamic organizations in Southeast Asia with millions of members.

title: Muhammadiyah movement founded in Java
description: Ahmad Dahlan established Muhammadiyah in Yogyakarta as a modernist Islamic reform movement combining religious orthodoxy with modern education and social welfare. The organization rapidly expanded across the Dutch East Indies, establishing schools, hospitals, and orphanages.
sources: Deliar Noer, The Modernist Muslim Movement in Indonesia
outcome: Muhammadiyah became Indonesia's second-largest Islamic organization with millions of members, shaping moderate reformist Islam in Southeast Asia.

title: Noble Qur'an Press established in Kazan
description: Muslim intellectuals in Kazan establish a modern publishing house producing Islamic texts in Tatar and Russian, becoming the center of jadidist reform literature. The press disseminated reformist ideas throughout the Muslim communities of the Russian Empire until Soviet suppression.
outcome: Became the primary vehicle for jadidist reform thought in the Volga-Ural region.

title: Deaths in 1914 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1914 CE: Ma Qixi — philosopher (Founder of the Xidaotang) in Lintan County; Shibli Nomani — scholar (Indian Islamic historian and biographer of the Prophet) in Azamgarh.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Sheikh Amadu Bamba exiled from Senegal
description: French colonial authorities deport Sufi master Sheikh Amadu Bamba, founder of the Mouride Brotherhood, for a second time due to his growing influence among Senegalese Muslims. His exile and peaceful resistance would transform him into a symbol of anti-colonial resistance throughout West Africa.
sources: Cheikh Anta Babou, Fighting the Greater Jihad
outcome: Bamba's exile strengthened the Mouride movement, which became central to Senegalese Muslim identity.

title: Arab Revolt begins against Ottoman rule
description: Sharif Hussein of Mecca launched the Arab Revolt with British support, seeking Arab independence from the Ottoman Empire. T.E. Lawrence coordinated with Faisal ibn Hussein in guerrilla warfare that captured Aqaba and eventually reached Damascus.
sources: Eugene Rogan, The Fall of the Ottomans
outcome: The revolt contributed to Ottoman defeat but Arab independence aspirations were betrayed by secret Sykes-Picot colonial agreements.

title: Deaths in 1918 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1918 CE: Abdulhamid II — ruler (Last Powerful Ottoman Sultan — Pan-Islamic Caliph) in Istanbul, Ottoman Empire.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Egyptian Revolution of 1919 erupts
description: Mass nationalist uprising led by Saad Zaghloul and the Wafd Party demanded Egyptian independence from British occupation. The revolution united Muslims and Copts and involved widespread strikes, demonstrations, and violent clashes.
sources: Afaf Lutfi al-Sayyid Marsot, Egypt's Liberal Experiment
outcome: Britain granted nominal independence in 1922 while maintaining military presence and control over foreign policy until 1952.

title: Battle of Maysalun and French occupation of Syria
description: Yusuf al-Azma led Syrian forces against French colonial troops at Maysalun Pass, where he was killed defending Damascus. The defeat ended Faisal I's short-lived Arab Kingdom of Syria and established French mandatory rule.
sources: Philip Khoury, Syria and the French Mandate
outcome: French mandatory rule over Syria began, lasting until 1946.

title: Khilafat Movement reaches its peak in India
description: Indian Muslims led by the Ali Brothers and supported by Gandhi launched the Khilafat Movement to pressure Britain to preserve the Ottoman Caliphate. The movement united Hindu-Muslim nationalist cooperation and involved mass civil disobedience.
outcome: The movement collapsed after Mustafa Kemal abolished the Caliphate in 1924, deepening Muslim political consciousness in India.

title: Grand National Assembly opens in Ankara
description: Mustafa Kemal convenes the first Grand National Assembly in Ankara as a rival government to the Ottoman sultanate in Istanbul, marking the beginning of the Turkish national resistance. The assembly brought together diverse Muslim delegates who would later establish the Turkish Republic.
sources: Erik Jan Zürcher, Turkey: A Modern History
outcome: Established the institutional foundation for the Turkish Republic and end of the Ottoman Sultanate.

title: Deaths in 1921 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1921 CE: Ahmad Raza Khan Barelvi — scholar (A'la Hazrat — Reviver of the Barelvi Tradition) in Bareilly, India.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Moplah Rebellion in Malabar
description: Muslim peasants (Moplahs) in Kerala rose against British colonial authorities and Hindu landlords in an armed uprising. The rebellion was brutally suppressed with thousands killed and mass deportations.
sources: R.H. Hitchcock, A History of the Malabar Rebellion
outcome: The rebellion highlighted agrarian grievances and communal tensions in colonial India.

title: Rif Republic declared in Morocco
description: Abd el-Krim declares the independent Republic of the Rif in northern Morocco after defeating Spanish colonial forces at Annual. This short-lived Islamic republic adopted a constitution blending Islamic law with republican governance before falling to combined Franco-Spanish forces.
sources: David Woolman, Rebels in the Rif
outcome: Created the first modern republic in the Muslim world, influencing later anti-colonial movements.

title: Mustafa Kemal abolishes the Ottoman Caliphate
description: The Turkish Republic under Mustafa Kemal Ataturk formally abolishes the Ottoman Caliphate, ending 1,292 years of continuous caliphal authority in Sunni Islam and sending shockwaves through the Muslim world.
sources: Erik Jan Zurcher, Turkey: A Modern History
outcome: Ended the last institutional symbol of Muslim political unity and triggered a global debate about Islamic governance

title: Hijaz falls to Ibn Saud's forces
description: Abdulaziz Ibn Saud's Wahhabi forces conquered Mecca and Medina, ending Hashemite rule over the Hijaz. Sharif Hussein ibn Ali was forced into exile, consolidating Najd and Hijaz under Saudi control.
sources: H. St. J. B. Philby, Saudi Arabia
outcome: The conquest unified much of Arabia under Saudi rule and established Wahhabi doctrine in the holy cities.

title: Emir Abdülkrim defeats Spanish forces at Annual
description: Riffian Berber leader Muhammad ibn Abd el-Krim al-Khattabi inflicted a catastrophic defeat on Spanish colonial forces at the Battle of Annual in 1921, leading to the proclamation of the independent Rif Republic in 1924. His forces killed over 8,000 Spanish troops in one of colonial history's greatest defeats.
sources: David Woolman, Rebels in the Rif
outcome: The Rif Republic demonstrated successful anti-colonial resistance until combined Franco-Spanish forces defeated it in 1926.

title: Rif War ends with Abd el-Krim's surrender
description: Muhammad ibn Abd el-Krim al-Khattabi surrendered to French forces after leading the Rif Republic's resistance against Spanish and French colonial powers in Morocco. His guerrilla tactics influenced later anti-colonial movements worldwide.
sources: David S. Woolman, Rebels in the Rif
outcome: Though defeated, Abd el-Krim became an icon of anti-colonial resistance across the Muslim world.

title: Reza Khan crowns himself Shah of Iran
description: Reza Khan, commander of the Persian Cossack Brigade, deposed the Qajar dynasty and established the Pahlavi dynasty. He pursued aggressive modernization and secularization policies modeled on Mustafa Kemal's Turkey, including banning the veil and traditional dress.
sources: Ervand Abrahamian, A History of Modern Iran
outcome: Reza Shah's authoritarian modernization created tensions with the ulama that would resurface in the 1979 Islamic Revolution.

title: Deaths in 1927 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1927 CE: Inayat Khan — founder (Pir-o-Murshid — Founder of Universal Sufism) in Baroda / Geneva; Sheikh Ahmadou Bamba — Sufi mystic (Founder of the Muridiyya Sufi order in Senegal) in Touba.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Hasan al-Banna founds the Muslim Brotherhood
description: Egyptian schoolteacher Hasan al-Banna founds al-Ikhwan al-Muslimun (the Muslim Brotherhood) in Ismailia, Egypt, creating the most influential Islamist political organisation of the 20th century.

figures.name: Hasan al-Banna
figures.role: founder of the Muslim Brotherhood; created the template for Islamic political activism

sources: Richard Mitchell, The Society of the Muslim Brothers
outcome: Created the model for Islamic political activism that would inspire movements across the Muslim world

title: Turkey adopts Latin alphabet
description: Mustafa Kemal Atatürk officially replaces the Arabic script with a modified Latin alphabet for writing Turkish, severing linguistic ties with the Ottoman and Islamic past. The reform rendered the previous generation's written works inaccessible to new Turkish readers.
sources: Geoffrey Lewis, The Turkish Language Reform
outcome: Marked a decisive break with Ottoman Islamic heritage and accelerated secularization.

title: Allama Iqbal delivers the Allahabad Address
description: The poet-philosopher Allama Iqbal delivers his famous presidential address to the All India Muslim League at Allahabad, articulating the vision of a separate Muslim state in northwest India — the intellectual foundation of Pakistan.

figures.name: Allama Iqbal
figures.role: poet-philosopher; articulated the vision for Pakistan

sources: Iqbal, The Reconstruction of Religious Thought in Islam
outcome: Provided the intellectual and philosophical basis for the creation of Pakistan 17 years later

title: Basmachi Revolt finally suppressed
description: Soviet forces crushed the last remnants of the Basmachi movement, an Islamic resistance against Bolshevik rule in Central Asia. Enver Pasha had been killed leading fighters in 1922, but resistance continued until the early 1930s.
sources: Baymirza Hayit, Turkestan im XX Jahrhundert
outcome: Soviet control over Central Asia was consolidated and Islamic institutions were systematically suppressed.

title: Nahdlatul Ulama founded in Java
description: Traditional Indonesian ulama established Nahdlatul Ulama in response to Muhammadiyah's reformism, defending Shafi'i jurisprudence and Ash'ari theology. Founded by Hasyim Asy'ari, it emphasized traditional pesantren education and Sufi practices.
sources: Greg Fealy, Traditionalist Islam in Indonesia
outcome: Nahdlatul Ulama became Indonesia's largest Islamic organization with over 40 million members, championing moderate traditional Islam.

title: Muhammad Iqbal lectures at Madras and Aligarh
description: Philosopher-poet Muhammad Iqbal delivers his lectures on the Reconstruction of Religious Thought in Islam at universities across India, proposing a dynamic reinterpretation of Islamic philosophy. These lectures articulated a modernist Islamic epistemology engaging Western philosophy.
sources: Muhammad Iqbal, The Reconstruction of Religious Thought in Islam
outcome: Established Iqbal as the leading Islamic modernist philosopher of the 20th century.

title: Atatürk commissions Mahmud Esad Coşan for Turkish Qur'an translation
description: As part of secularization reforms, Mustafa Kemal Atatürk commissioned the first official Turkish translation of the Qur'an and ordered the Islamic call to prayer (adhan) to be recited in Turkish rather than Arabic. These linguistic reforms sought to create a Turkish Islam independent of Arabic tradition.
sources: M. Şükrü Hanioğlu, Atatürk: An Intellectual Biography
outcome: The Turkish adhan remained mandatory until 1950, symbolizing state control over religious practice during the early Republic.

title: Kingdom of Saudi Arabia founded
description: Abdulaziz ibn Abdul Rahman Al Saud unifies the Hejaz and Nejd into the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, creating a state built on the Wahhabi-Saudi alliance forged in 1744.
sources: Alexei Vassiliev, The History of Saudi Arabia
outcome: Created the modern Saudi state that would become the custodian of Islam's two holiest sites and the world's largest oil exporter

title: Faisal I becomes King of Iraq
description: Iraq gained formal independence from British mandate with Faisal I as king, though Britain retained significant military and political influence. The Hashemite monarchy would last until 1958.
sources: Phebe Marr, The Modern History of Iraq
outcome: Iraq joined the League of Nations as an independent state, though British influence remained strong.

title: Ibn Saud unifies Arabia as the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
description: Abdulaziz Ibn Saud completed his thirty-year conquest by unifying Najd and Hijaz into the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. The kingdom was founded on a partnership between the Al Saud dynasty and Wahhabi religious scholars.
sources: Madawi Al-Rasheed, A History of Saudi Arabia
outcome: The discovery of oil in 1938 transformed the kingdom into a major global power and patron of Wahhabi Islam worldwide.

title: Al-Azhar becomes state university
description: Egyptian government formally incorporates Al-Azhar University into the state education system, adding faculties of medicine, engineering, and agriculture alongside traditional Islamic sciences. This reform balanced modernization with preserving Al-Azhar's role as Sunni Islam's premier religious institution.
sources: Indira Falk Gesink, Islamic Reform and Conservatism
outcome: Transformed Al-Azhar into a modern university while maintaining its religious authority.

title: Deaths in 1934 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1934 CE: Ahmad al-Alawi — founder (Shaykh al-'Alawi — Qutb al-Zaman) in Mostaganem, Algeria.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Kashf al-Mahjub published by Reynold Nicholson
description: British orientalist Reynold Nicholson published the first complete English translation of Ali Hujwiri's 11th-century Sufi masterwork Kashf al-Mahjub. This made classical Sufi texts accessible to Western audiences and initiated serious academic study of Islamic mysticism.
sources: Carl Ernst, The Shambhala Guide to Sufism
outcome: Nicholson's work established Islamic mysticism as a legitimate field of academic study in the West.

title: Deaths in 1935 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1935 CE: Rashid Rida — scholar (al-Manar Editor — Bridge between Salafism and Islamic Modernism) in Qalamun (Lebanon) / Cairo.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Persia officially renamed Iran
description: Reza Shah Pahlavi requested that foreign nations use the name Iran instead of Persia in official correspondence. The change emphasized the country's Aryan heritage and modern national identity.
sources: Homa Katouzian, State and Society in Iran
outcome: The name change reflected modernizing nationalism and distancing from imperial associations.

title: Turkey officially adopts Western calendar and Sunday rest
description: As part of Mustafa Kemal's secularization reforms, Turkey officially replaced the Islamic calendar with the Gregorian calendar and changed the weekly rest day from Friday to Sunday. This completed the symbolic break with Islamic temporal traditions.
sources: Erik Zürcher, Turkey: A Modern History
outcome: These reforms symbolized Turkey's radical turn toward European modernity and provoked opposition from religious conservatives.

title: Reza Shah bans women's hijab in Iran
description: Shah Reza Pahlavi issues the Kashf-e hijab decree forcibly unveiling Iranian women as part of his modernization campaign modeled on Atatürk's Turkey. Police enforced the ban by physically removing veils from women in public, causing widespread religious opposition.
sources: Houchang Chehabi, Dress Codes for Men in Turkey and Iran
outcome: Sparked lasting resentment among religious Iranians, contributing to the 1979 revolution.

title: Deaths in 1936 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1936 CE: Mehmet Akif Ersoy — poet (Author of the Turkish national anthem and Islamic modernist poet) in Istanbul.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1937 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1937 CE: Meher Ali Shah — Sufi mystic (Sarkar Golra — Qutb al-Aqtab of Punjab) in Golra Sharif, Pakistan; Abdolkarim Haeri Yazdi — scholar (Founder of the Qom seminary (hawza) and reviver of Shia scholarship) in Qom.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Palace coup deposes King Ghazi of Iraq
description: King Ghazi of Iraq, an Arab nationalist who opposed British influence and supported Palestinian resistance, died in a suspicious car accident widely believed to be British-orchestrated assassination. His death strengthened British control over Iraq through his infant son Faisal II under a regent.
sources: Charles Tripp, A History of Iraq
outcome: Ghazi's death intensified Iraqi anti-British sentiment and contributed to the 1941 nationalist coup and subsequent 1958 revolution.

title: Sarekat Islam banned in Dutch East Indies
description: Dutch colonial authorities outlaw Sarekat Islam, the Islamic trade and political association that had mobilized millions of Indonesian Muslims since 1912. The suppression drove many Muslim activists toward more radical anti-colonial positions and underground organizing.
sources: Deliar Noer, The Modernist Muslim Movement in Indonesia
outcome: Radicalized Indonesian Muslim nationalism ahead of the independence struggle.

title: Deaths in 1938 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1938 CE: Abdurauf Fitrat — scholar (Father of Modern Uzbek Drama and Jadidist Reformer) in Bukhara (Uzbekistan) / Istanbul; Allama Iqbal — philosopher (Shair-e-Mashriq — Poet of the East; Spiritual Father of Pakistan) in Sialkot / Lahore / London / Heidelberg.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Bediuzzaman Said Nursi completes the Risale-i Nur
description: Turkish scholar Bediuzzaman Said Nursi completes the Risale-i Nur collection in exile and prison, a 6,000-page commentary on Quranic themes that argues for Islamic faith using rational and scientific reasoning.

figures.name: Bediuzzaman Said Nursi
figures.role: Turkish scholar; authored the Risale-i Nur collection in prison

sources: Sukran Vahide, Islam in Modern Turkey: An Intellectual Biography of Bediuzzaman Said Nursi
outcome: Created the most widely-read Islamic theological work of the 20th century, with millions of followers in Turkey and beyond

title: Deaths in 1940 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1940 CE: Ibn Badis — scholar (Father of Algerian Nationalism — Islamic Reformer) in Constantine, Algeria.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Lahore Resolution demands Muslim homelands
description: The All-India Muslim League passes the Lahore Resolution calling for independent states in Muslim-majority regions of British India. A. K. Fazlul Huq presented the resolution that would become the constitutional basis for Pakistan's creation.
outcome: Established the constitutional demand for Pakistan as a separate Muslim state.

title: Deaths in 1941 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1941 CE: Parvin E'tesami — poet (Greatest Female Persian Poet of the 20th Century) in Tabriz / Tehran, Iran.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Anglo-Soviet invasion of Iran
description: British and Soviet forces invaded neutral Iran to secure oil fields and supply routes during World War II, forcing Reza Shah to abdicate. His son Mohammad Reza Pahlavi took the throne as Allied forces occupied the country.
outcome: Iran became a critical supply corridor for Allied aid to the Soviet Union during World War II.

title: Rashid Ali al-Gaylani leads nationalist coup in Iraq
description: Iraqi nationalist Rashid Ali al-Gaylani seized power in a coup against the pro-British regent, seeking German support to expel British forces. Britain invaded Iraq in the Anglo-Iraqi War, crushing the nationalist government within weeks.
outcome: British victory restored the monarchy and secured Iraqi oil for the Allied war effort, but nationalist resentment persisted.

title: Deaths in 1942 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1942 CE: Muhammed Hamdi Yazır — scholar (Elmalılı Hamdi Yazır and Elmalılı (1878 – 27 May 1942) was a) in Ankara.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1943 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1943 CE: Ashraf Ali Thanvi — scholar (Hakim al-Ummat — Physician of the Muslim Community) in Thana Bhawan, India.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1944 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1944 CE: Haji-Mirza Hassan Roshdieh — scholar (Founder of the first modern school for Muslims there) in Tabriz.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Arab League founded in Cairo
description: Seven Arab states established the League of Arab States to coordinate policy and resist colonial powers and Zionism. Founding members included Egypt, Iraq, Transjordan, Lebanon, Saudi Arabia, Syria, and Yemen.
sources: Ahmed M. Gomaa, The Foundation of the League of Arab States
outcome: The Arab League became a major regional organization for political coordination among Arab states.

title: First Congress of Muslim Scholars convenes in Jerusalem
description: Leading ulama from across the Arab world gathered in Jerusalem to address the Palestinian crisis and coordinate Islamic responses to Zionist colonization. Amin al-Husseini presided over discussions linking Palestinian resistance to broader Islamic solidarity.
sources: Philip Mattar, The Mufti of Jerusalem
outcome: The congress helped frame the Palestinian cause as a pan-Islamic issue, though Arab military coordination remained weak.

title: Indonesia declares independence
description: Sukarno and Mohammad Hatta proclaim Indonesian independence from Dutch colonial rule, establishing the world's largest Muslim-majority nation. The Jakarta Charter's debate over Islamic law's role shaped Indonesia's pluralist constitution.
sources: M. C. Ricklefs, A History of Modern Indonesia
outcome: Created the world's most populous Muslim-majority democracy after years of struggle.

title: Pakistan created as the world's first Islamic republic
description: The partition of British India creates Pakistan — the world's first modern state founded on Muslim identity — from the Muslim-majority regions of the subcontinent. Millions are displaced and hundreds of thousands killed in sectarian violence.

figures.name: Allama Iqbal
figures.role: intellectual father of Pakistan (died 1938); his vision made real

outcome: Created the largest Muslim-majority nation at independence, fundamentally reshaping South Asian and Islamic geopolitics

title: Partition of India creates mass Muslim migration
description: British India's partition created India and Pakistan, triggering one of history's largest migrations. Between 10-20 million people crossed borders, with over one million killed in communal violence.
outcome: The partition fundamentally reshaped South Asian demographics and created lasting Indo-Pakistani tensions.

title: Deoband scholars split over Pakistan at independence
description: The Jamiat Ulema-e-Hind, led by Maulana Husain Ahmad Madani, opposed the creation of Pakistan on theological grounds, arguing that composite nationalism was compatible with Islam. Rival Deobandi scholars including Shabbir Ahmad Usmani supported the Muslim League and Pakistan, creating a lasting division in South Asian Islamic thought.
sources: Barbara Metcalf, Islamic Revival in British India
outcome: This theological split shaped Muslim political identity in South Asia, with Indian Muslims developing distinct interpretations of citizenship and Islamic governance.

title: First Arab-Israeli War and Palestinian displacement
description: Following the creation of Israel, five Arab armies attack but are defeated. Over 700,000 Palestinians are displaced in what they call the Nakba (catastrophe), creating a refugee crisis that persists to this day.
outcome: Created the Palestinian refugee crisis and the Arab-Israeli conflict that has defined Middle Eastern politics ever since

title: Deaths in 1949 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1949 CE: Hasan al-Banna — reformer (Imam al-Shahid — The Martyred Imam) in Mahmudiyya / Cairo, Egypt; Musa Bigiev — reformer (Islamic Theology Reformer of Rostov-on-Don) in Rostov-on-Don.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Hasan al-Banna assassinated in Cairo
description: Muslim Brotherhood founder Hasan al-Banna was assassinated by Egyptian government agents in retaliation for the Brotherhood's alleged involvement in political violence. His death occurred amid escalating confrontation between the Brotherhood and King Farouk's government.
sources: Richard Mitchell, The Society of the Muslim Brothers
outcome: Al-Banna's martyrdom elevated him to legendary status and the Brotherhood survived to become the most influential Islamist movement globally.

title: Hasan al-Banna assassinated in Cairo
description: Egyptian secret police assassinate Hasan al-Banna, founder of the Muslim Brotherhood, in retaliation for Brotherhood violence against government officials. His death transformed him into a martyr and allowed more radical interpretations of his thought to flourish.
sources: Richard Mitchell, The Society of the Muslim Brothers
outcome: Martyrdom elevated al-Banna's status and radicalized segments of the Muslim Brotherhood.

title: Jordanian annexation of West Bank
description: King Abdullah I of Jordan formally annexes the West Bank and East Jerusalem following the 1948 Arab-Israeli War, placing Islamic holy sites under Hashemite control. The annexation was rejected by most Arab states and the Palestinian leadership.
sources: Avi Shlaim, Collusion Across the Jordan
outcome: Placed al-Aqsa Mosque under Hashemite custodianship until Israeli conquest in 1967.

title: Deaths in 1951 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1951 CE: Mohammad-Taqi Bahar — poet (Poet Laureate of Iran — Last Great Classical Persian Poet) in Mashhad / Tehran, Iran; Rene Guenon — philosopher (Shaykh Abd al-Wahid Yahya) in Blois / Cairo, Egypt.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Libya gains independence under King Idris
description: Libya became independent under King Idris al-Sanusi, ending Italian colonial rule and Allied military administration. The kingdom was organized as a federal state uniting Cyrenaica, Tripolitania, and Fezzan under Sanusi leadership.
sources: Dirk Vandewalle, A History of Modern Libya
outcome: Oil discovery in 1959 transformed Libya's economy, but growing inequality and Western influence led to Gaddafi's 1969 coup.

title: Deaths in 1952 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1952 CE: Abdallah al-Qutbi — philosopher (Philosopher).
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Egyptian Revolution — Nasser era begins
description: A military coup led by the Free Officers movement overthrows King Farouk of Egypt. Gamal Abdel Nasser emerges as leader, championing Arab nationalism, pan-Arabism and non-alignment.
outcome: Inaugurated the era of Arab nationalism and made Egypt the political centre of the Arab world

title: Deaths in 1953 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1953 CE: Neyzen Tevfik — poet (Mawlawiyya Poet of Bodrum) in Bodrum; Ibn Saud — ruler (Founder and first king of Saudi Arabia) in Riyadh.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: CIA-backed coup overthrows Mosaddegh in Iran
description: Mohammad Mosaddegh, who nationalized Iran's oil industry, was overthrown in Operation Ajax orchestrated by CIA and MI6. The coup restored full power to Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi and reversed oil nationalization.
outcome: The coup deepened anti-Western sentiment and contributed to conditions leading to the 1979 revolution.

title: Deaths in 1954 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1954 CE: Mustafa Sabri Efendi — scholar (Last Shaykh al-Islam of the Ottoman Empire) in Tokat / Istanbul / Cairo; Ahmad Amin — scholar (Egyptian historian and author of the Fajr/Duha/Zuhr al-Islam trilogy) in Cairo.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Algerian War of Independence begins
description: The National Liberation Front (FLN) launched armed rebellion against French colonial rule on November 1st. The war would last eight years with brutal tactics on both sides.
sources: Alistair Horne, A Savage War of Peace
outcome: The conflict became one of the bloodiest decolonization wars, ending with Algerian independence in 1962.

title: FLN launches Algerian War of Independence
description: The Front de Libération Nationale initiated coordinated attacks across Algeria on November 1, beginning an eight-year war against French colonial rule. Led by figures like Ahmed Ben Bella, the FLN combined guerrilla warfare with international diplomacy.
sources: Alistair Horne, A Savage War of Peace
outcome: Algeria won independence in 1962 after a brutal war that killed over one million people and inspired anti-colonial movements worldwide.

title: Algerian National Liberation Front launches armed struggle
description: The FLN launches coordinated attacks across Algeria on All Saints' Day, beginning an eight-year war of independence against French colonial rule. Ahmed Ben Bella and other founders initiated what became one of the bloodiest decolonization conflicts.
sources: Alistair Horne, A Savage War of Peace
outcome: Initiated the Algerian War that would claim over 300,000 lives before independence in 1962.

title: Bandung Conference establishes Non-Aligned Movement
description: Leaders from 29 African and Asian nations, including Indonesian President Sukarno, Egyptian President Nasser, and Pakistani Prime Minister Ali, convened in Bandung to chart an independent course between Cold War superpowers. The conference emphasized anti-colonialism and Afro-Asian solidarity.
sources: Christopher Lee, Making a World After Empire
outcome: The conference launched the Non-Aligned Movement, giving Muslim-majority states a platform to assert independence from superpower blocs.

title: Suez Crisis and Nasser's triumph
description: After Nasser nationalized the Suez Canal, Britain, France, and Israel invaded Egypt but withdrew under US and Soviet pressure. Nasser emerged as a hero of Arab nationalism and Third World independence.
outcome: The crisis marked the decline of British and French colonial power and elevated Nasser's pan-Arab leadership.

title: Tunisia abolishes Islamic law courts
description: President Habib Bourguiba implemented sweeping secular reforms including abolishing Sharia courts, banning polygamy, and introducing civil marriage and divorce. The reforms were among the most radical in the Muslim world.
sources: Kenneth Perkins, A History of Modern Tunisia
outcome: Tunisia established one of the most secular legal systems in the Arab world, particularly regarding women's rights.

title: Malaya gains independence as Muslim-led federation
description: The Federation of Malaya achieves independence from Britain with Islam as the official religion and Malay sultans retaining ceremonial authority. Tunku Abdul Rahman became the first prime minister, balancing Malay Muslim identity with multi-ethnic governance.
sources: William Roff, The Origins of Malay Nationalism
outcome: Established a Muslim-majority constitutional monarchy balancing religious and secular governance.

title: Deaths in 1958 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1958 CE: Yahya Kemal Beyatli — poet (Major Turkish poet who bridged Ottoman and Republican literary traditions) in Istanbul.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1959 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1959 CE: Khalifa Abdul Hakim — philosopher (Philosopher of Lahore) in Lahore.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1960 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1960 CE: Bediuzzaman Said Nursi — scholar (Bediuzzaman — Wonder of the Age) in Bitlis / Turkey.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Establishment of OPEC in Baghdad
description: Five oil-producing nations including Saudi Arabia, Iran, Iraq, Kuwait, and Venezuela founded the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries. The cartel aimed to coordinate petroleum policies and protect member interests against Western oil companies.
outcome: OPEC would fundamentally shift global oil economics and give Muslim-majority nations greater control over energy resources.

title: Mauritania gains independence
description: Mauritania becomes independent from France as the Islamic Republic of Mauritania, with Arabic as official language and Sharia law as a legal source. Moktar Ould Daddah became president, navigating between Arab and African identities.
sources: Anthony G. Pazzanita, Historical Dictionary of Mauritania
outcome: Created a bridge state between Arab North Africa and sub-Saharan West Africa.

title: Algeria wins independence after 132 years of French rule
description: After a brutal eight-year war of independence that killed over a million Algerians, France recognises Algerian independence, ending 132 years of colonial rule.
sources: Alistair Horne, A Savage War of Peace
outcome: Became the most celebrated anti-colonial victory in the Muslim world and a symbol of Third World liberation

title: Algeria achieves independence from France
description: Following the Évian Accords, Algeria gained independence after 132 years of French colonial rule and eight years of brutal warfare. Ahmed Ben Bella became the first president of the Algerian Republic, implementing Arab socialist policies.
sources: Martin Evans, Algeria: France's Undeclared War
outcome: Algerian independence inspired liberation movements across Africa and the Third World, establishing Algeria as a leader of anti-imperialist politics.

title: Algeria achieves independence
description: Algeria wins independence from France after 132 years of colonial rule and eight years of brutal war, with Ahmed Ben Bella becoming the first president. The Evian Accords ended a conflict that cost hundreds of thousands of lives.
outcome: Established Algeria as a leader of Third World anti-imperialism and Arab socialism.

title: Deaths in 1963 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1963 CE: Nazim Hikmet — poet (Revolutionary Turkish poet and pioneer of free verse in Turkish) in Istanbul.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Formation of the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation
description: Following the 1969 arson attack on Al-Aqsa Mosque, Muslim-majority nations formed the OIC to safeguard Islamic interests. The organization would grow to include 57 member states making it the second-largest intergovernmental organization after the UN.
sources: Turan Kayaoglu, The Organization of Islamic Cooperation
outcome: The OIC became the collective voice of the Muslim world in international affairs.

title: Deaths in 1964 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1964 CE: Abbas el-Akkad — poet (Philosopher of Aswan) in Aswan.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1965 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1965 CE: Malcolm X — scholar (El-Hajj Malik El-Shabazz — Voice of Black Muslim America) in Omaha / New York / Mecca; M.M. Sharif — philosopher (Philosopher of Lahore) in Lahore.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Malcolm X assassinated after Hajj transformation
description: Malcolm X (El-Hajj Malik el-Shabazz) is assassinated in New York. His 1964 pilgrimage to Mecca had transformed his worldview from racial separatism to universal Islamic brotherhood, documented in his famous letter from Mecca.

figures.name: Malcolm X
figures.role: American Muslim leader; assassinated after his spiritual transformation through Hajj

sources: Malcolm X and Alex Haley, The Autobiography of Malcolm X
outcome: His life and death became the most powerful story of Islamic spiritual transformation in the modern West

title: Indonesian military massacres alleged communists
description: Following an alleged communist coup attempt, General Suharto orchestrated mass killings of suspected communists with support from Islamic groups including Nahdlatul Ulama. Between 500,000 and one million people were killed in one of the 20th century's worst atrocities.
outcome: The massacres eliminated the Indonesian Communist Party and brought Suharto to power for 32 years of authoritarian rule.

title: Deaths in 1966 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1966 CE: Ali Abd al-Raziq — scholar (Islam and the Foundations of Political Power — Secular Islam Thinker) in Abu Jirab / Cairo; Sayyid Qutb — scholar (Milestones Author — Most Influential Islamist Theorist) in Musha / Cairo, Egypt.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Sayyid Qutb executed — Islamist thought radicalises
description: Egyptian thinker Sayyid Qutb is executed by the Nasser regime. His prison writings, especially Milestones (Ma'alim fi al-Tariq), will become the foundational texts of radical Islamist ideology.

figures.name: Sayyid Qutb
figures.role: Muslim Brotherhood thinker; executed by the Egyptian state

outcome: His martyrdom and writings became the ideological foundation for radical Islamist movements globally

title: Six-Day War — Israel captures Jerusalem
description: Israel defeats Egypt, Syria and Jordan in six days, capturing the Sinai Peninsula, Gaza, the West Bank, the Golan Heights, and — most significantly for the Muslim world — East Jerusalem including al-Aqsa Mosque and the Dome of the Rock.
sources: Michael Oren, Six Days of War
outcome: The loss of Jerusalem galvanised Islamic activism worldwide and discredited secular Arab nationalism

title: Deaths in 1969 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1969 CE: Jalal Al-e-Ahmad — philosopher (Philosopher of Tehran) in Tehran.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: King Faisal assumes leadership of OIC after al-Aqsa fire
description: Following the arson attack on al-Aqsa Mosque, Saudi King Faisal convened Muslim leaders in Rabat to establish the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation. The fire galvanized Islamic unity efforts and elevated Saudi Arabia's leadership in the Muslim world.
sources: James Piscatori, Islam in the Political Process
outcome: The OIC became the second-largest intergovernmental organization after the UN, though its effectiveness remained limited.

title: Al-Aqsa Mosque arson attack
description: An Australian Christian fundamentalist sets fire to Al-Aqsa Mosque in Jerusalem, causing international Muslim outrage and damaging the historic prayer hall. The attack prompted the founding of the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation to protect Muslim holy sites.
sources: John Esposito, The Oxford History of Islam
outcome: Catalyzed the creation of the OIC as a permanent international Muslim organization.

title: Al-Aqsa Mosque arson by Australian Christian extremist
description: Denis Michael Rohan, an Australian Christian fundamentalist, set fire to the Al-Aqsa Mosque in Jerusalem, destroying the 800-year-old minbar of Salah al-Din. The attack galvanized Muslim solidarity worldwide and led directly to the founding of the Organisation of Islamic Cooperation.

figures.name: King Faisal
figures.role: Saudi king who convened Islamic summit

sources: Malcolm Kerr, The Arab Cold War
outcome: The first Islamic Summit Conference convened in Rabat in September 1969, establishing the OIC as a permanent pan-Islamic political organization.

title: Deaths in 1971 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1971 CE: Abd El-Razzak El-Sanhuri — scholar (Father of modern Arab civil law who synthesised Islamic and Western jurisprudence) in Cairo.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Bangladesh Liberation War and Pakistani defeat
description: East Pakistan declared independence as Bangladesh after a brutal crackdown by Pakistan's military. Indian intervention helped secure victory for Bengali forces led by the Mukti Bahini, resulting in Pakistan's surrender and the birth of Bangladesh.
outcome: Bangladesh emerged as an independent Muslim-majority nation, ending Pakistan's two-wing structure.

title: Bangladesh Liberation War creates new Muslim state
description: East Pakistan declared independence as Bangladesh after years of economic and political marginalization by West Pakistan. Indian military intervention defeated Pakistani forces, and Sheikh Mujibur Rahman became the first prime minister of Bangladesh.
outcome: The creation of Bangladesh shattered the two-nation theory underlying Pakistan's foundation and became the world's third-largest Muslim state.

title: Bangladesh Liberation War creates new Muslim state
description: East Pakistan secedes to form Bangladesh after a nine-month war against West Pakistan, with Indian military intervention proving decisive. Sheikh Mujibur Rahman led the Bengali nationalist movement that redrew the map of Muslim South Asia.
sources: Srinath Raghavan, 1971: A Global History of the Creation of Bangladesh
outcome: Created the world's third-largest Muslim-majority nation and shattered Pakistan's two-nation theory.

title: Deaths in 1973 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1973 CE: Malik Bennabi — philosopher (Social Philosopher of the Islamic Renaissance) in Constantine, Algeria / Cairo, Egypt; Tahir ibn Ashur — scholar (Shaykh al-Islam of Tunisia) in Tunis, Tunisia; Ahmad Wahib — philosopher (Philosopher of Sampang) in Sampang; Mirza Mahdi Elahi Qomshehei — philosopher (Philosopher of Isfahan) in Isfahan; Asik Veysel — poet (Beloved Turkish folk poet and ashik troubadour) in Sivas; Taha Hussein — scholar (Dean of Arabic literature and pioneer of modern Arabic literary criticism) in Cairo.
outcome: 6 notable figures died this year.

title: October War and Arab oil embargo
description: Egypt and Syria launched a surprise attack on Israel during Yom Kippur, initially gaining territory before eventual stalemate. Arab oil-producing states imposed an embargo on nations supporting Israel, quadrupling oil prices.
sources: Abraham Rabinovich, The Yom Kippur War
outcome: The war restored Arab dignity after 1967 and the oil embargo demonstrated petroleum as a geopolitical weapon.

title: October War and Arab oil embargo
description: Egypt and Syria launch coordinated attacks on Israeli positions during Yom Kippur, with Arab oil producers implementing an embargo that quadrupled oil prices. Anwar Sadat's initial military success restored Arab dignity despite eventual Israeli counteroffensive.
sources: Abraham Rabinovich, The Yom Kippur War
outcome: Demonstrated Arab oil power and set stage for Egyptian-Israeli peace negotiations.

title: Deaths in 1975 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1975 CE: Allameh Sayyed Abul Hasan Rafiee Qazvini — philosopher (Philosopher of Qazvin) in Qazvin.

figures.name: Allameh Sayyed Abul Hasan Rafiee Qazvini

outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Annemarie Schimmel publishes Mystical Dimensions of Islam
description: The German scholar Annemarie Schimmel publishes Mystical Dimensions of Islam, the most comprehensive and sympathetic Western scholarly introduction to Sufism, making Islamic mysticism accessible to a global audience.

figures.name: Annemarie Schimmel
figures.role: German scholar; authored the definitive English introduction to Sufism

sources: Annemarie Schimmel, Mystical Dimensions of Islam
outcome: Made Sufism accessible to Western readers and established it as a legitimate field of academic study

title: Lebanese Civil War begins
description: Sectarian violence erupted between Christian and Muslim militias, Palestinian groups, and foreign interventions in Lebanon. The conflict would devastate the country for fifteen years until 1990.
sources: Kamal Salibi, A House of Many Mansions
outcome: The civil war destroyed Lebanon's multi-confessional system and led to Syrian and Israeli occupations.

title: Lebanese Civil War intensifies with Muslim-Christian division
description: Lebanon's sectarian tensions exploded into full civil war after Palestinian presence and Christian-Muslim power imbalances erupted in violence. The conflict involved multiple factions including Palestinian organizations, Maronite militias, and Shia movements.
sources: Fawwaz Traboulsi, A History of Modern Lebanon
outcome: The fifteen-year civil war devastated Lebanon, killed over 120,000 people, and reshaped Middle Eastern politics with Syrian and Israeli interventions.

title: Lebanese Civil War begins
description: Civil war erupts in Lebanon between Muslim and Christian militias, complicated by Palestinian refugee fighters and Syrian intervention. The conflict shattered Lebanon's confessional political system and devastated Beirut for fifteen years.
sources: Kamal Salibi, A House of Many Mansions
outcome: Destroyed Lebanon's multi-confessional democracy and empowered sectarian militias.

title: Deaths in 1977 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1977 CE: Ali Shariati — scholar (Ideologue of the Iranian Revolution — Islamic Marxist Thinker) in Mazinān / Tehran / Paris / London.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1978 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1978 CE: Musa al-Sadr — scholar (Iranian-Lebanese Shia cleric who mobilised the Lebanese Shia community) in Beirut.
outcome: 1 notable figure died this year.

title: Deaths in 1979 CE
description: Notable deaths recorded in 1979 CE: Qalandar Baba Auliya — Sufi mystic (Chishti Mystic of Jaunpur) in Jaunpur; Syed Abul Ala Maududi — scholar (Founder of Jamaat-e-Islami and major Islamic political theorist) in Lahore.
outcome: 2 notable figures died this year.

title: Iranian Revolution — Ayatollah Khomeini founds Islamic Republic
description: The Shah of Iran is overthrown in a popular revolution led by Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini, who establishes the Islamic Republic of Iran — the first modern theocratic state based on the concept of wilayat al-faqih (guardianship of the jurist).

figures.name: Ayatollah Khomeini
figures.role: leader of the revolution; established the Islamic Republic of Iran

sources: Ervand Abrahamian, Iran Between Two Revolutions
outcome: Created the first modern Islamic state, inspired Islamist movements worldwide and reshaped Middle Eastern geopolitics

title: Soviet Union invades Afghanistan — global jihad begins
description: The Soviet Union invades Afghanistan to support the communist government, triggering a decade-long jihad by Afghan mujahideen backed by the USA, Saudi Arabia and Pakistan.
outcome: Created the global jihad movement, led to the rise of al-Qaeda and reshaped Muslim geopolitics for decades

title: Seizure of the Grand Mosque in Mecca
description: On the first day of the 15th Islamic century (1 Muharram 1400), armed militants seize the Grand Mosque in Mecca, declaring their leader as the Mahdi. Saudi forces retake the mosque after two weeks of fighting.
sources: Yaroslav Trofimov, The Siege of Mecca
